- **Purpose:** Block until the worker runtime reports it is idle, so a test can wait for the reactive graph to quiesce before asserting.
- **Design:** Reaches into `globalThis.commonfabric.rt.idle()` (a promise that resolves on quiescence) inside a predicate driven by waitForCondition; returns false if the hook is not yet present so the poll re-tries.
- **Impact:** Signal-driven quiescence (idle() is notification-based, not a sleep), so this is the good pattern — but it is wrapped by waitForCondition polling until the rt hook exists.
- **Blast radius:** Exposed via waitForRuntimeIdle, used by cfc-authorized-save.test.ts and others. Depends on the runtime exposing an idle() contract; changing that touches runtime-client.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing quiescence primitive for the CFC integration suite; defensible and preferable to sleeps. Medium because it gates real test correctness.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-browser-helpers.ts:59-66` — storage-sync quiescence await (rt.allSynced())  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `runtimeSynced`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / storage-sync quiescence await (rt.allSynced())
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (!rt?.allSynced) return false;
  await rt.allSynced();
  return true;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Block until every space the worker has opened has flushed/synced to storage, so cross-runtime assertions see committed state.
- **Design:** Calls `commonfabric.rt.allSynced()` inside a waitForCondition predicate; returns false until the hook appears so polling retries. Comment notes quiescence is worker-wide, not per-space.
- **Impact:** Signal-driven (awaits a sync-completion promise) rather than sleeping. Correctness-critical for multi-runtime/propagation tests; if allSynced under-reports, tests race.
- **Blast radius:** Exposed via waitForRuntimeSynced; couples to the worker's allSynced contract.
- **Insight:** Good, event-based synchronization primitive. Medium — it gates test determinism across the CFC suite.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-group-chat-demo-multi-runtime.test.ts:123, 129, 149, 178, 199, 239, 243, 251, 259, 272, 284, 295, 301` — cross-runtime propagation polling (harness.waitFor)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `multiple it(...) blocks (via harness.waitFor)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / cross-runtime propagation polling (harness.waitFor)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await harness.waitFor("bob receives alice's message", async () => (await messages(bob)).some((m) => m?.body === "Hello from Alice"));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Wait for state written in one runtime (alice) to propagate to another (bob / alice-tab2) via the shared in-memory storage server before asserting PerUser/PerSession/PerSpace behavior.
- **Design:** `harness.waitFor(label, predicate)` polls the labeled async predicate, retrying through transiently-unsynced reads (comment at 270-271) until it holds or times out; the label feeds the timeout error.
- **Impact:** Correctness-critical for the multi-runtime isolation/propagation assertions; poll-based convergence tolerant of eventual consistency. Determinism depends on the harness poll bound.
- **Blast radius:** Central to every test in the file; couples to MultiRuntimeHarness's waitFor/settle contract (multi-runtime-harness.ts).
- **Insight:** Appropriate eventual-consistency polling for a distributed-ish scenario; labels give good diagnostics. Low (test-only) though it is the backbone of these tests.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-group-chat-demo-multi-runtime.test.ts:138, 158, 184, 269` — explicit quiescence barrier (harness.settle)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `multiple it(...) blocks (via harness.settle)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / explicit quiescence barrier (harness.settle)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await harness.settle();
  ```
- **Purpose:** Force all runtimes to reach quiescence before a negative assertion (e.g. that a PerUser draft did NOT leak, or a rejected room add left the list empty).
- **Design:** `harness.settle()` awaits the harness-wide settle/quiescence across all sessions; used before reads that must observe fully-propagated (or fully-rejected) state.
- **Impact:** Ensures negative/isolation assertions run against settled state instead of racing propagation; signal/barrier-driven rather than a sleep.
- **Blast radius:** Depends on MultiRuntimeHarness.settle correctly draining every session; used at four points guarding leak/rejection assertions.
- **Insight:** Correct barrier usage for negative assertions in a multi-runtime setting. Low (test-only).

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-group-chat-demo-two-browsers.test.ts:160-190, 205-260, 300-375` — poll-until-quiescence (waitForRuntimeIdle) and poll-until-text helpers  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `describe callback / test bodies (via waitForRuntimeIdle, waitForText, waitForDisabled)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / poll-until-quiescence (waitForRuntimeIdle) and poll-until-text helpers
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  waitForRuntimeIdle(page, { timeout: PROPAGATION_TIMEOUT })
  ```
- **Purpose:** Repeatedly re-checks the two browser pages until the runtime reports idle and expected DOM text appears, standing in for an explicit completion event across the multiplayer boundary.
- **Design:** Each helper polls the page (query selectors / runtime idle probe) on an interval until the predicate holds or PROPAGATION_TIMEOUT elapses; multiple pages are polled via Promise.all fan-out per participant.
- **Impact:** Introduces polling latency and CPU churn; ordering of assertions relies on eventual quiescence rather than a deterministic signal, which is a common flakiness source in these suites.
- **Blast radius:** These are the primary synchronization primitive of the whole suite; replacing polling with signals would touch @commonfabric/integration helper implementations and every test that consumes them.
- **Insight:** The helpers are the file's real timing mechanism; the file itself only orchestrates them. Load-bearing and shared, not easily removable without a runtime-idle event API.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-group-chat-demo.test.ts:278-286, 303-312` — polling while-loop via waitFor with fixed 250ms delay  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `waitForAuthorshipState / waitForInvalidAuthorshipState`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling while-loop via waitFor with fixed 250ms delay
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await waitFor(async () => { probe = await readAuthorshipProbe(page, ...); return ... }, { timeout: CFC_GROUP_CHAT_TIMEOUT, delay: 250 });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Repeatedly reads a DOM authorship probe until a verified (or invalid) row appears, because authorship verification settles asynchronously with no event to await.
- **Design:** waitFor invokes the async predicate every 250ms until true or the 60s deadline; on timeout it throws with the last probe JSON for diagnosis.
- **Impact:** 250ms poll cadence plus deep DOM traversal (readAuthorshipProbe walks all shadow roots) adds per-iteration cost; success is timing-dependent, a flakiness surface.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would need an authorship-settled signal from the CFC layer; touches the probe reader and any test asserting authorship convergence.
- **Insight:** Classic poll-for-async-settlement workaround; load-bearing given no completion event, but replaceable if the runtime emitted an authorship-verified notification.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-group-chat-demo.test.ts:170` — poll-until-state (shell.waitForState)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-render-policy-demo.test.ts:74-95, 91-96` — poll-until-idle/text (waitForRuntimeIdle, waitForText, waitForTextAbsent) with 45s per-call timeout override  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Blast radius:** Local overrides only; underlying helpers shared.
- **Insight:** Defensible bounded polling; the 45s override signals a known-slow path worth a completion signal if one existed.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-spec-gallery.test.ts:79-137, 148-155` — polling waitFor (default timeout) plus waitForText poll  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Snippet:**

- **Design:** waitForCfcLabelText calls waitFor with no explicit timeout (uses helper default) invoking a shadow-DOM label probe each iteration; the test body chains many waitForText polls per stage.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-staged-publish.test.ts:91-108` — poll-until-text (waitForText) through saved/reviewed/published stages  &nbsp; ⚪ info

#### `packages/patterns/integration/chatbot.test.ts:160-165, 229-234, 285-290` — polling waitFor for message count, with 30000/60000 timeout ceilings  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `it callbacks (assistant response / clear)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling waitFor for message count, with 30000/60000 timeout ceilings
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await waitFor(async () => { const messages = await page.$$("cf-chat-message", ...); return messages.length >= 2; }, { timeout: 30000 });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Polls the DOM message count until the assistant reply arrives (>=2, >=4) or the history is cleared (===0), because LLM responses settle asynchronously with no completion event exposed to the test.
- **Design:** waitFor invokes a selector-count predicate each poll interval until satisfied or the deadline (30s for first reply, 60s for follow-up, default for clear) elapses.
- **Impact:** The 30s/60s ceilings bound LLM latency; polling adds churn. Convergence is timing-observed, a flakiness surface tied to real model response time.
- **Blast radius:** Local predicates; shared waitFor helper. A message-appended event/signal would remove the poll.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing given the async LLM boundary; the large timeouts reflect genuine model latency, defensible for an LLM integration test.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/convergence-storm.test.ts:86, 95, 161, 189` — quiescence settle with iteration count argument (harness.settle(n))  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `minimalCase beforeAll/it and storm describe beforeAll/it (harness.settle)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / quiescence settle with iteration count argument (harness.settle(n))
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await harness.settle(20);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Drives the multi-runtime harness to quiescence after sends so reads reflect fully converged durable state; the numeric argument requests extra settle rounds proportional to the write-storm depth.
- **Design:** MultiRuntimeHarness.settle() runs settle passes (default and explicit counts of 5 and 20) that repeatedly pump the runtime scheduler until no more work is pending; higher counts drain the deep optimistic pipeline built by idle:false sends.
- **Impact:** Convergence correctness depends on choosing enough settle rounds; too few and the observer read is non-deterministic (the very blackout/wedge this test targets). This is a repeated-pumping quiescence loop, the core determinism lever of the test.
- **Blast radius:** settle is implemented in multi-runtime-harness.ts and used across the multi-runtime integration suite; changing its semantics affects every convergence test. The magic round counts here are tuned to the storm size (K=20).
- **Insight:** Load-bearing manual quiescence loop; the explicit round counts are a smell that the harness lacks a true fixed-point signal, but the whole test exists to probe convergence so the mechanism is intrinsic.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/counter.test.ts:70-104` — polling helper (waitFor) around DOM/cell reads  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `describe 'counter direct operations test' > it callbacks`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling helper (waitFor) around DOM/cell reads
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await waitFor(async () => { ... return initialText?.trim() === "Counter is the 0th number"; });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for the browser-rendered counter view and the piece result cell to reach an expected value after a direct operation, because the integration harness renders asynchronously through the worker/vdom pipeline.
- **Design:** Uses the imported waitFor(predicate) helper (from @commonfabric/integration) which re-invokes the async predicate on an interval until it returns truthy or a harness-level timeout fires; combined with page.waitForSelector('#counter-result', {strategy:'pierce'}) which itself polls the shadow DOM.
- **Impact:** Introduces poll-until-true latency and dependence on harness poll interval/timeout; flakiness risk if the render never settles. No wall clock read in this file.
- **Blast radius:** The waitFor/waitForSelector mechanism lives in the integration harness; changing to an event/signal-driven settle would touch every integration test that polls the rendered view. Locally this test only consumes the helper.
- **Insight:** Standard integration-test polling; load-bearing for cross-worker rendering assertions but replaceable if the harness exposed a view-settled completion signal (which awaitViewSettled elsewhere already approximates).

#### `packages/patterns/integration/default-app.test.ts:700-714` — view-settled poll (awaitViewSettled) before/after click  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `it (notebook rapid-create regression) inline`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / view-settled poll (awaitViewSettled) before/after click
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await waitFor(async () => await awaitViewSettled(page));
        ... await waitFor(async () => { await awaitViewSettled(page); return !!(await findButtonWithText(page, "Create Another")); });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Ensures the notebook toolbar/modal is mounted and interactive (worker idle plus vdom applied and Lit updates drained) before delivering a click, so a single click is not dropped.
- **Design:** awaitViewSettled (a runtime completion signal that the rendered view has caught up) polled inside waitFor until the target button exists.
- **Impact:** Guards against dropped clicks in rapid-create scenarios; correctness-affecting for the regression it targets. Poll latency but condition-driven.
- **Blast radius:** Specific to the rapid-create regression; relies on the view-settled signal contract.
- **Insight:** Defensible and clearly load-bearing (the comment documents the dropped-click race it prevents); a good example of settling on a real signal rather than a sleep.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/default-app.test.ts:789-796, 1543-1550, 1740-1741` — runtime quiescence await (rt.idle()) wrapped in waitFor polling  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `awaitRuntimeIdle / waitForRuntimeIdle / collectActionRunSummary (and other rt.idle() call sites)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / runtime quiescence await (rt.idle()) wrapped in waitFor polling
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const rt = globalThis.commonfabric?.rt; if (!rt?.idle) return false; await rt.idle(); return true;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Blocks the test until the in-browser runtime scheduler queue has drained (idle) or all spaces are synced, so subsequent DOM assertions and metric captures see a settled state. Used pervasively (awaitRuntimeIdle, waitForRuntimeIdle, awaitViewSettled, waitForRuntimeSynced/allSynced).
- **Design:** await rt.idle() / rt.allSynced() are completion-signal driven promises exposed by the runtime; but they are repeatedly wrapped in waitFor(async () => await awaitRuntimeIdle(page)) so the outer layer polls-retries the whole idle probe. Many call sites (e.g. 408, 490, 677, 704, 711, 737, 768, 793, 900, 919, 1547, 1741, 1876, 2497, 2640).
- **Impact:** Waiting for quiescence by re-polling an idle signal; the idle() itself is signal-based (good), but the waitFor wrapper adds poll intervals and a harness timeout. Central to determinism of the whole default-app regression suite; if idle() under-reports quiescence, downstream asserts flake.
- **Blast radius:** These helpers are the settle backbone of the largest integration test in the repo and are re-implemented/imported across home-profile and home-rehydration-churn too; changing the quiescence contract (idle vs synced) would ripple through dozens of assertions and the harness waitFor.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing quiescence machinery. The signal-based rt.idle()/allSynced() is the right primitive; wrapping it in waitFor polling is defensive belt-and-suspenders that is mostly redundant since idle() already resolves on completion.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/instantiate-pattern.test.ts:89-91` — polling for condition (page.waitForFunction on URL change)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `it (instantiate pattern navigation test)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling for condition (page.waitForFunction on URL change)

  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for the soft navigation to change the URL after clicking Add, to assert navigation occurred.
- **Design:** page.waitForFunction re-evaluates the predicate in the page context on an interval until the URL differs or a timeout fires.
- **Impact:** Poll-until-true latency; deterministic on the completion condition (URL change) rather than a fixed sleep, so more robust.
- **Blast radius:** Harness-provided; local usage only.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/multi-runtime-harness.ts:399-418` — polling while-loop with wall-clock deadline  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `MultiRuntimeHarness.waitFor`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling while-loop with wall-clock deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const startedAt = Date.now();
  while (Date.now() - startedAt < timeout) { try { if (await predicate()) return; } catch ... await this.settle(1); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Polls a caller predicate until it resolves truthy, settling all runtimes between attempts, so tests can assert on state that must cross runtimes without hard-coding a delay.
- **Design:** while-loop gated on Date.now() elapsed vs DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS (30_000, line 70); each iteration runs predicate, swallows errors into lastError, then calls settle(1) (one idle round + a 25ms sleep). Throws a descriptive timeout error including the last predicate error on expiry.
- **Impact:** Combines a retry loop with the embedded settle() sleep, so each poll costs at least ~25ms plus idle time. Robust to propagation latency but its own timing is coupled to settle's blind 25ms pause; flakiness inherits from settle.
- **Blast radius:** Central assertion primitive for cross-runtime tests; a refactor to event-driven waiting would touch settle(), the worker idle contract, and every waitFor call site. Moderate.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing and reasonable as a polling harness, but its correctness rests on settle()'s timer, so it is only as sound as that sleep. Defensible test infrastructure.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/nested-counter.test.ts:133-153, 157-168` — polling waitFor with try/catch on stale element handles  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `it(verify nested counter...) inline waitFor + waitForCounter`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling waitFor with try/catch on stale element handles
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await waitFor(async () => { try { const counterResults = await page.$$(...); if (counterResults.length !== 2) return false; ... } catch (_) { return false; } });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Polls the DOM until the expected counter elements are present and show the expected text, retrying past stale-handle exceptions that occur when elements are re-rendered between query and read.
- **Design:** Both sites wrap the shared waitFor primitive: the predicate re-queries via page.$$ / waitForSelector, reads innerText, and returns false (rather than throwing) on count mismatch or any stale-handle exception, so waitFor re-polls until the DOM matches or times out.
- **Impact:** Tolerates re-render/hydration timing so assertions are not flaky against a settling page; cost is polling latency until the condition holds. No control over the underlying settle other than the waitFor deadline.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test file; depends on the shared waitFor. Low.
- **Insight:** Same workaround family as clickNthButton — polling in lieu of an exposed 'render settled' signal. Defensible integration-test hygiene; the try/catch-to-false is the retry mechanism.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/nested-counter.test.ts:174-189` — polling retry via waitFor until click succeeds  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `clickNthButton`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling retry via waitFor until click succeeds
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  return waitFor(async () => { const buttons = await page.$$(selector, {strategy:"pierce"}); if (buttons.length <= index) return false; try { await buttons[index].click(); return true; } catch { return false; } });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Clicks the nth matching button, retrying while the element lacks a stable box model — handles timing issues where the element exists but the page is still settling (re-renders, layout shifts, hydration).
- **Design:** Wraps the shared imported waitFor polling primitive: each attempt re-queries the DOM, bails false if not enough buttons, else tries the click and returns true on success / false on any exception, so waitFor keeps polling until success or its own timeout.
- **Impact:** Absorbs render/layout-settle races so the click test is not flaky; but a persistently unstable box model would only surface as a waitFor timeout. Adds polling latency proportional to how long settling takes.
- **Blast radius:** Used by the increment-button test; relies on the shared waitFor contract (defined elsewhere). Local, low.
- **Insight:** A workaround for browser render-settle timing that has no completion signal exposed to the test; reasonable and common in integration UI tests. The keyword-less retry lives in the waitFor closure returning false to re-poll.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/reload/default-app-notebook.test.ts:187-213` — MutationObserver quiet-period settle with reset timer + max cap  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `collectBrowserLoadMetrics (postRenderStableMs promise)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / MutationObserver quiet-period settle with reset timer + max cap
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const resetQuietTimer = () => { if (quietTimer) clearTimeout(quietTimer); quietTimer = setTimeout(done, 100); };
  const observer = new MutationObserver(resetQuietTimer); ... const maxTimer = setTimeout(done, 1_000);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Measures a DOM quiet-period settle time after reload: waits until the DOM has stopped mutating for 100ms and reports when that quiescence was reached, so reload perf is observable independently of scheduler action counts.
- **Design:** A MutationObserver on document.documentElement resets a 100ms quiet timer on every mutation; when 100ms pass with no mutation, done() fires. A 1_000ms maxTimer caps the wait so a never-quiet DOM still resolves. done() disconnects the observer, clears both timers, and resolves after two chained requestAnimationFrame ticks to land past the next paint.
- **Impact:** The 100ms quiet window and 1s cap are heuristics: too-short quiet windows could report premature settle; a busy DOM hits the 1s cap. This is a measurement (metric), not a control-flow gate on the test outcome, so it mainly affects the reported number, not pass/fail. Double-rAF adds a couple frames of latency to the reading.
- **Blast radius:** Self-contained in the metrics collector run via page.evaluate; only the reloadSummary log/metric consumes postRenderStableMs. Refactor is trivial and local.
- **Insight:** Idiomatic quiescence-detection (debounced MutationObserver + hard cap). Defensible for a perf metric; the magic 100ms/1s numbers are the usual soft spot but low-stakes since it is observational.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/shared-profile.test.ts:83-106, 129-144` — poll-until helper (waitForText / waitForRuntimeIdle)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `it("uses each user's home profile...") / submitProfileCreate`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / poll-until helper (waitForText / waitForRuntimeIdle)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await waitForText(page, "#shared-profile-name", "Ada Lovelace");
  ...
  await waitForRuntimeIdle(page);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for asynchronous multi-runtime state to converge in the DOM (text appears) and for the runtime scheduler to reach quiescence before proceeding, so assertions run against a settled UI rather than a mid-flight render.
- **Design:** Imported helpers from cfc-browser-helpers.ts (not in this chunk); waitForText polls the pierced DOM for a substring, waitForRuntimeIdle blocks until the in-page runtime reports idle. Called at each step of the two-user profile-create flow.
- **Impact:** These convergence waits are the backbone of determinism for the test; without them the profile-name assertions would race the network/runtime. They add latency proportional to actual convergence time, not fixed sleeps.
- **Blast radius:** The underlying helpers live in cfc-browser-helpers.ts and are shared across many CFC integration tests; changing their polling/idle semantics would ripple broadly. Within this file the usage is idiomatic and low-risk.
- **Insight:** Preferable to fixed sleeps — event/idle-driven convergence. Load-bearing for correctness of the observation but the mechanism itself is out-of-file; flagged here as the in-file dependency on a quiescence poll.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/sqlite-read-clearance-multi-runtime.test.ts:94-125` — quiescence polling predicate (harness.waitFor)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `it("returns each reader only their own admissible rows") / settled + harness.waitFor`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / quiescence polling predicate (harness.waitFor)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await harness.waitFor("alice's cleared query settles on her two rows", async () => (await settled(alice, "qClear", 2)) !== undefined);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits until each runtime's reactive SQLite read-clearance query has settled (pending===false, no error, exact row count) before asserting per-reader isolation, so the test observes converged results rather than intermediate pending states.
- **Design:** settled() reads the query-state cell and returns it only when pending is false, error undefined, and result length matches the expected count; harness.waitFor repeatedly evaluates this predicate (the poll/backoff mechanism lives in multi-runtime-harness.ts, not this chunk) until true or its own deadline.
- **Impact:** Determinism of the whole cross-reader-isolation assertion depends on this convergence wait; it avoids reading a half-settled result. Latency tracks real settle time. A predicate that never becomes true would surface as a harness.waitFor timeout.
- **Blast radius:** The waitFor polling and its deadline are owned by MultiRuntimeHarness (shared by many integration tests); this file only supplies the predicate. Refactoring the predicate is trivial; refactoring waitFor is broad.
- **Insight:** Correct, event-shaped use of a poll — it keys on an explicit settled signal (pending flag + row count) rather than a timer, which is the right pattern. Load-bearing for the security regression it guards (#4478).

#### `packages/patterns/integration/storm-driver.ts:42-48, 21, 45` — pipeline vs serial idle-gating of a send loop  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `storm (per-writer send loop)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / pipeline vs serial idle-gating of a send loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await s.send("post", { author, body: `${author}-${n}`, n }, undefined, { idle: IDLE_MODE === "pipeline" ? false : true });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Drives K writes per author to reproduce a convergence-storm divergence; the idle flag chooses whether each send waits for runtime quiescence (serial) or fires without waiting (pipeline), to bisect which timing regime is necessary for the bug.
- **Design:** A for-loop of K awaited sends; idleMode (CLI arg, default pipeline) maps to the send's idle option. In serial mode each send blocks on runtime-idle; in pipeline mode sends are back-to-back. Two writers run concurrently via Promise.all at line 69.
- **Impact:** Directly shapes event ordering and interleaving — the whole point is that pipeline (no idle wait) vs serial (idle wait) changes whether messages diverge across replicas. This is a determinism/ordering probe, not production code.
- **Blast radius:** Diagnostic-only script (run manually, not a test); the idle semantics come from MultiRuntimeSession.send. Changing it affects only this bisection tool's output. Low.
- **Insight:** A deliberate timing-regime toggle used to hunt a race — a debugging instrument, not a shipped mechanism. Interesting because it treats idle-wait vs no-wait as the independent variable of a convergence bug.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/storm-driver.ts:62, 72` — harness.settle quiescence barrier (with attempt bound)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `top-level (module script)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / harness.settle quiescence barrier (with attempt bound)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await harness.settle();
  ...
  await harness.settle(20);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for all sessions to reach a converged/quiescent state before recording the pre-storm link and after the storm before summarizing views, so the driver's reads reflect settled replicas.
- **Design:** harness.settle() with no arg (default rounds) pre-storm; settle(20) post-storm passes a numeric bound (rounds/attempts) to allow extra convergence iterations after a heavy write burst. The settle implementation lives in multi-runtime-harness.ts.
- **Impact:** The settle(20) bound decides how many convergence rounds the driver waits through before reading; too few would report false divergence, too many wastes time. It gates the correctness of the diagnostic snapshot but not any production behavior.
- **Blast radius:** settle is a shared harness primitive; here it is only consumed. The literal 20 is a tuning knob local to this script. Low.
- **Insight:** The explicit 20-round bound is a workaround for the storm needing more settle iterations than the default — itself a symptom of the divergence being investigated. Diagnostic instrument, defensible.

#### `packages/patterns/record/extraction/extractor-module.tsx:1608-1613` — reactive re-execution retry (early return to re-run on next dependency change)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `extractor writeback (early-return keeps extractor alive for retry)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / reactive re-execution retry (early return to re-run on next dependency change)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (!anySuccess && !cleanupWillApply) { console.warn("[Extract] No updates succeeded ... keeping extractor for retry"); return; }
  ```
- **Purpose:** When no module update succeeded and no cleanup is pending, the writeback returns early without trashing the extractor entry, so the extractor persists and the computation re-runs on the next reactive change to retry applying extracted fields.
- **Design:** No timer or backoff; the retry is implicit reactive re-evaluation. Termination happens when at least one update succeeds or cleanup applies, at which point the extractor trashes itself. No cancellation, no delay, no bound on attempts.
- **Impact:** Purely reactive; no CPU spin or wall clock. If inputs never change to allow success, the extractor lingers indefinitely (no attempt cap), but it does not busy-loop since it only re-runs on dependency updates.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch the anySuccess/cleanupWillApply gating and the self-trash logic (buildTrashList/selfIndex). Invariant: extractor removed only after a successful apply. Moderate difficulty due to intertwined cleanup handling.
- **Insight:** Defensible reactive retry that relies on the scheduler; smell is the unbounded lingering with no attempt cap, but it is signal-driven not timer-driven.

#### `packages/patterns/system/home-ben.tsx:251-297` — reactive writeback loop-break (mark-as-processed to reach quiescence)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `home pattern -> writeNarrative (computed)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / reactive writeback loop-break (mark-as-processed to reach quiescence)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (entry.narrative !== "") return null; ... if (error && !result) { ... narrativePending: false; journal.set(newEntries); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** A computed() side-effect writes the LLM-generated narrative back into the journal entry, and on LLM error marks the entry as processed/failed. It exists to prevent an infinite reactive re-execution loop where a still-pending entry keeps re-triggering generateText.
- **Design:** Idempotent guards: skips when generateText is pending, skips when narrative already written, and on error writes a sentinel '[Failed to generate narrative]' plus narrativePending=false so pendingEntry no longer selects it. Termination is driven by the generateText pending->result completion signal, not by any timer.
- **Impact:** No wall clock involved; convergence depends on the reactive scheduler settling. If the error/idempotent guards were removed, the pattern would busy-re-run generateText forever (a real retry storm). Ordering/determinism is governed by the runner scheduler, not by timing.
- **Blast radius:** Refactoring would touch the pendingEntry selector, the generateText wiring, and the journal entry shape (narrativePending/narrative fields). Invariant: exactly one write per entry. Low difficulty but easy to reintroduce a loop.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing reactive-quiescence guard rather than a timing mechanism; the 'prevent retry loop' comments are literal. Signal-driven and defensible; included because it is a self-re-triggering computation whose termination is a manual loop-break.

#### `packages/patterns/tools/importer-prompt.ts:1334-1389` — bounded retry loop with linear backoff and Retry-After honoring  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `AirtableClient.request (inside AIRTABLE_CLIENT_SOURCE template literal)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / bounded retry loop with linear backoff and Retry-After honoring
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  for (let attempt = 0; attempt <= retries; attempt++) { ... await sleep(waitMs); continue; ... if (attempt < retries) await sleep(delay); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Retries failed Airtable HTTP requests (network errors, 429 rate limits, 401 token expiry) so transient failures self-heal without surfacing to the caller.
- **Design:** for-loop capped at config.retries (default 2). 401 triggers refreshToken() and continues without sleeping; 429 sleeps for parsed Retry-After seconds or falls back to delay*(attempt+1) linear backoff; generic catch sleeps a flat delay (default 1000ms) before the next attempt. No jitter; throws lastError after the loop exhausts.
- **Impact:** Bounds total wall time to roughly retries*delay plus any Retry-After waits; correctness depends on the server honoring Retry-After. Deterministic count but non-deterministic durations. Ships as reference source inside the importer prompt, so it defines the retry behavior of every generated importer.
- **Blast radius:** A change would propagate to all generated Airtable importers and should stay in sync with the real packages/patterns/airtable/core/util/airtable-client.ts; tests exercising rate-limit/error paths of generated clients would be affected.
- **Insight:** Reasonable, load-bearing retry design for a rate-limited API; linear backoff without jitter is a mild smell under concurrent callers but acceptable at 2 retries. Notable that it is authored as a template string here, so it is documentation-as-code the LLM reproduces verbatim.

### `packages/piece`

#### `packages/piece/src/manager.ts:857-871` — sync-then-retry-once on missing metadata  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `PieceManager.syncPattern`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / sync-then-retry-once on missing metadata
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  let ref = getPatternIdentityRef(piece);
  if (!ref) { await this.synced(); await piece.sync(); ref = getPatternIdentityRef(piece); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Reads the piece's patternIdentity meta pointer; under remote sync the metadata can transiently lag the result value, so it re-syncs storage and re-reads once before declaring the pattern identity missing.
- **Design:** A single conditional retry: if the first read returns no ref, it awaits synced() (storage quiescence) and piece.sync() again, then re-reads. No loop, no backoff, no delay; it either finds the ref on the second read or throws 'piece missing pattern identity'.
- **Impact:** Bounded (one extra attempt) so no unbounded spin, but it papers over an ordering race between metadata and value propagation. If storage settles slower than one synced() round, the retry still fails and the piece setup throws; a source of intermittent failures under slow/remote sync.
- **Blast radius:** Touches setupPersistent -> syncPattern path used by runPersistent/startPiece across piece and background-piece-service. Replacing it with an explicit completion signal on patternIdentity would remove the race but requires the runtime to expose a wait-for-meta primitive; the FIXME comment above the method already flags it for revisiting.
- **Insight:** Marked with 'FIXME(JA): this really really really needs to be revisited'. Load-bearing workaround for a metadata-vs-value propagation race; a smell that a settle-then-retry-once masks rather than an event-driven wait on the metadata itself.

#### `packages/piece/test/pull-materialization.test.ts:137-149` — polling while-loop with deadline and 1ms sleep  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `waitFor`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling while-loop with deadline and 1ms sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const deadline = Date.now() + timeoutMs;
  while (Date.now() < deadline) { if (predicate()) return; await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 1)); }
  throw new Error(`Timed out waiting ${timeoutMs}ms`);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Test helper that spins until a predicate becomes true (e.g. that a stubbed runtime.setup has been invoked), used at line 333 to wait for releaseSetup to be assigned before asserting setup ordering.
- **Design:** Busy-poll: reads Date.now() against a 1000ms default deadline, checks predicate, sleeps 1ms via setTimeout between checks. Combines a wall-clock timeout with a tight polling cadence; throws on timeout.
- **Impact:** 1ms cadence is CPU-light but purely time-based; correctness depends on the predicate flipping within 1s. Standard test flakiness source if the awaited work is slower under load.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test file; used only by the 'waits for setup to settle' case. Replaceable by awaiting the promise/callback directly since the stub already exposes releaseSetup.
- **Insight:** Test-only polling shim; defensible but could be an explicit deferred/await rather than a poll loop since the signal (releaseSetup assignment) is synchronous within the stub.

### `packages/runner`

#### `packages/runner/integration/memory-v2-reactivity.test.ts:9-20` — polling while-loop with deadline and 10ms sleep  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `waitFor (module-level)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling while-loop with deadline and 10ms sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const started = Date.now();
  while (true) { if (predicate()) return; if (Date.now() - started > timeout) throw ...; await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 10)); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for cross-runtime reactive propagation (receivedAddress / receivedNewCity / receivedPopulation flags set by sink callbacks) to arrive over remote sync before asserting.
- **Design:** while(true) loop: checks predicate, then compares elapsed Date.now() against a timeout, sleeps 10ms between polls. Wall-clock deadline gates the loop; throws 'Timed out waiting for condition' on expiry.
- **Impact:** Polling on 10ms cadence to observe asynchronous cross-runtime updates; the elapsed-time check is the abandon decision. Flaky if propagation latency exceeds the (default) timeout under CI load; determinism depends on network/sync timing.
- **Blast radius:** Used by all three reactivity test cases in this file. Could be replaced by resolving a promise inside the sink callback instead of polling a boolean, removing the timer entirely.
- **Insight:** Classic test-side quiescence poll standing in for an event/signal. Test-only, but the pattern (poll a flag set by a callback) is exactly the sink promise that could be awaited directly.

#### `packages/runner/integration/reconnection.test.ts:9-20` — polling while-loop with wall-clock deadline  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `waitFor (module-level helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling while-loop with wall-clock deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const started = Date.now();
  while (!predicate()) { if (Date.now()-started > timeout) throw ...; await new Promise(r=>setTimeout(r,10)); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for an arbitrary boolean predicate (here: that the subscriber cell saw the reconnect update) to become true, giving up after a timeout so a stuck test fails rather than hangs.
- **Design:** Busy-poll: re-evaluate predicate, and if elapsed wall-clock time (Date.now() delta) exceeds the 5000ms default, throw 'Timed out'; otherwise sleep 10ms via setTimeout and retry. No signal-based completion.
- **Impact:** Test-only. The 10ms poll interval and 5s cap introduce nondeterminism and potential flakiness under slow CI; the loop wakes up to 500 times per wait. Latency of detecting the condition is bounded by the 10ms tick, not by the actual reconnect event.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test file; used once at line 97. Replacing with an event/promise resolved from the sink callback would remove the poll but requires threading a resolver through the subscriber.sink closure. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** A classic poll-until-true test helper standing in for an awaitable reconnect signal. Load-bearing for the test's assertion but a smell: the sink callback at line 75 already fires on the event, so this could be a one-shot promise instead of a poll.

#### `packages/runner/integration/sqlite-cfc-label-link.test.ts:53-69` — polling while-loop with wall-clock deadline + setTimeout sleep  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `runTest`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling while-loop with wall-clock deadline + setTimeout sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const deadline = Date.now() + 25000;
  while (Date.now() < deadline) { await runtime.idle(); await runtime.storageManager.synced(); if (pending===false && arr.length===1) { ready=true; break; } await new Promise(r=>setTimeout(r,50)); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for the sqlite db.query builtin to settle (pending===false and the seeded row present in result) before asserting on the decoded link column and confidentiality label.
- **Design:** Poll loop bounded by a 25s wall-clock deadline; each iteration drives runtime.idle() and storageManager.synced(), checks the query's pending/result state, then sleeps 50ms via setTimeout. Throws 'query never reflected the seeded row' if the deadline passes without readiness.
- **Impact:** Test-only. Convergence is detected by repeated polling rather than a settle signal; the 50ms tick and 25s cap add nondeterminism/flakiness under load and cap total wait. idle()+synced() do most of the quiescing; the poll re-checks a derived predicate they don't directly signal.
- **Blast radius:** This test only. Same idiom is duplicated across the sibling sqlite label tests (label, row-label, db-query-decode). A shared 'await query settled' helper or an event on the query cell would remove all four copies. Medium difficulty due to duplication.
- **Insight:** Waiting-for-quiescence by repeated polling — the canonical retry-loop smell. Load-bearing (the assertions need settled data) but replaceable if the query builtin exposed a completion promise/signal.

#### `packages/runner/integration/sqlite-cfc-row-label.test.ts:58-96` — polling while-loop with wall-clock deadline + setTimeout sleep  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `runTest`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / polling while-loop with wall-clock deadline + setTimeout sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const deadline = Date.now() + 30000;
  while (Date.now() < deadline) { await runtime.idle(); await runtime.storageManager.synced(); if (qPending===false && skimPending===false && countPending===false && clearPending===false && arr.length===2) { ready=true; break; } await new Promise(r=>setTimeout(r,50)); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits until all four db.query results (q, qSkim, qCount, qClear) settle before asserting on per-row labels; qCount is expected to settle with an error.
- **Design:** 30s wall-clock deadline (larger than the 25s siblings because it waits on four queries); per-iteration idle()+synced(), a four-way pending===false check plus result length===2, 50ms setTimeout sleep. On timeout it dumps each query's pending/error/result and throws 'queries never settled'.
- **Impact:** Test-only. More predicates to converge = more sensitive to timing; the 30s cap and 50ms tick govern flakiness. The error-dump on failure aids debugging but the settle detection is still poll-based.
- **Blast radius:** This test; shares the idiom with the other sqlite tests but with a wider predicate. Consolidation harder here due to the multi-query condition. Medium.
- **Insight:** The most elaborate of the poll loops — quiescence across four queries by repeated polling. Load-bearing; a query-settled signal would eliminate the tick and the deadline guesswork.

  ```

- **Blast radius:** Duplicated across the four sqlite tests. Medium if consolidating.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/fetch-program.ts:356,359,381,384` — runtime.idle() quiescence await + editWithRetry  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `startFetch (fetch-program)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / runtime.idle() quiescence await + editWithRetry
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await runtime.idle();
  await runtime.editWithRetry((tx) => { ... });
  ```
- **Purpose:** After resolving a program, waits for the scheduler to reach quiescence (idle) before writing results, so the writeback lands on a settled graph; editWithRetry retries the writeback under commit contention.
- **Design:** runtime.idle() resolves when the scheduler drains its queue (event-driven, not a timer). editWithRetry re-runs the write closure on commit rejection. Both are completion-signal driven despite the retry framing.
- **Impact:** idle() ordering makes writebacks deterministic relative to the reactive graph; retries add bounded attempts under contention. Central to whether settled()/idle()-based tests converge.
- **Blast radius:** Pervasive pattern across builtins; changing idle() semantics is a runtime-wide concern. Here it is a consumer.
- **Insight:** runtime.idle() is a quiescence barrier, arguably a 'wait for quiescence' pattern, but it is driven by the scheduler's completion signal rather than polling, so it is defensible and load-bearing.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/fetch-utils.ts:113,115-148,166-178` — editWithRetry commit-conflict retry wrapper  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `tryClaimMutex / tryWriteResult`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / editWithRetry commit-conflict retry wrapper
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await runtime.editWithRetry((tx) => { ... pending.withTx(tx).set(true); ... });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Both helpers mutate shared cells (mutex claim, result writeback) that other runtimes may be racing on. editWithRetry re-runs the mutation closure when the optimistic-concurrency commit is rejected, so a lost race retries instead of throwing.
- **Design:** editWithRetry (defined in runtime.ts) runs the closure against a fresh transaction and retries on commit rejection up to maxRetries, decrementing recursively. Here it wraps the compare-and-set of the mutex and the hash-guarded result write. Preceded by runtime.idle() so inputs are settled.
- **Impact:** Retries add nondeterministic transaction attempts under contention, affecting latency and CPU under storms; the hash guard keeps them idempotent so correctness holds. Bounded retry count avoids infinite loops.
- **Blast radius:** The retry mechanism itself lives in runtime.ts; these are call sites. Refactoring the concurrency model (e.g. to a real lock) would remove these retries and touch every fetch builtin plus mutex tests.
- **Insight:** Standard optimistic-concurrency retry; load-bearing for multi-runtime correctness. Not a smell, but it is the softer sibling of the lease timeout above and shares its contention profile.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/fetch.ts:481-546,631,650,653` — runtime.idle() barrier + editWithRetry writeback  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `fetchBuiltin post-commit effect / startFetch`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / runtime.idle() barrier + editWithRetry writeback
- **Snippet:**

  ```

  await tryWriteResult(...); / await runtime.editWithRetry((tx) => { ... });
  ```
- **Purpose:** After the network response, waits for scheduler quiescence then writes the result/error under commit-retry so concurrent runtimes converge. tryClaimMutex/tryWriteResult carry the CAS guard.
- **Design:** idle() barrier before writeback; tryWriteResult/editWithRetry re-run the mutation on commit rejection, guarded by input-hash equality so retries are idempotent. All completion-signal driven; retries bounded by editWithRetry's maxRetries.
- **Impact:** Governs ordering and convergence of fetch results across runtimes; retries add bounded attempts under contention. Key to settled()-based test stability.
- **Blast radius:** Same runtime.idle()/editWithRetry machinery shared by all builtins; here a consumer. Changing convergence strategy touches every fetch path and mutex tests.
- **Insight:** Idiomatic optimistic-concurrency + quiescence barrier; defensible. Not a hot loop — retries are conflict-triggered, not polling.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/filter.ts:76-96,115-131,161,219-265,350-383` — await-input-then-settle resume republish (completion-driven, not timer)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `buildFilterAction / awaitInputThenSettle / awaitSyncBeforeInitialRun path`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / await-input-then-settle resume republish (completion-driven, not timer)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const awaitInputThenSettle = (...) => { ...runtime.editWithRetry((settleTx) => { ...result...set([]); }); };
  ```
- **Purpose:** On resume/rehydration the filter must not emit a stale/empty aggregate while predicates and input docs are still streaming in. It holds output until the input confirms, then seeds [] if still empty so a coordinator waiting on the filter is not wedged forever.
- **Design:** Uses awaitSync flags and createResumeRepublisher's awaitPendingThenRepublish to await resolved input cells, then writes via editWithRetry. Progress is driven by cell-resolution/settled signals (the reactive await), not by any timer, sleep, or polling loop. resumeBatchAwaitSync is cleared once a non-empty batch arrives.
- **Impact:** Affects convergence and whether resumed filters wedge; because it is completion-signal driven rather than timed, it is deterministic. editWithRetry adds bounded conflict retries.
- **Blast radius:** Tied to createResumeRepublisher, the RESULT_PRESENCE_SCHEMA seeding, and list-resume tests (list-resume-input-settle, list-shrink-converge). Refactor would touch resume/republish machinery broadly.
- **Insight:** Interesting anti-wedge design: it deliberately seeds an empty result once the input settles so downstream waiters unblock. This is 'wait for quiescence' but driven by settle signals, not polling — defensible and load-bearing for resume correctness. Borderline for inclusion; noted because of the settle/await semantics.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/llm.ts:165-175, 323, 649-663, 975-993, 1520-1522, 1759-1761` — editWithRetry conflict-rebase retry loop (event-gated)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `createUpdatePartialCallback / resetLLMState / llm/generateText/generateObject onComplete + writeback`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / editWithRetry conflict-rebase retry loop (event-gated)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await runtime.idle();
  await runtime.editWithRetry((tx) => { resultCell.key("result").withTx(tx).set(...); });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Commits LLM result/partial/error writes durably even when the local replica is behind the authoritative version, so a conflicting commit does not drop the writeback.
- **Design:** editWithRetry (defined in runtime.ts) recursively re-runs the write fn on CONFLICT up to DEFAULT_MAX_RETRIES, awaiting the conflict's readyToRetry() catch-up gate and pulling the conflicting doc before each retry — an event-gated retry, not a timed sleep. Each builtin path (llm, generateText, generateObject, streamData writeback) uses it after runtime.idle().
- **Impact:** Bounded, signal-driven retries; correctness-preserving rather than flaky. No wall clock, but repeated re-runs of the write fn add latency under contention and the retry count bounds how much conflict churn is tolerated before surfacing an error.
- **Blast radius:** The mechanism itself lives in runtime.ts; these are call sites. A refactor of the retry contract would touch every builtin writeback here plus scheduler/action-run.ts which shares the catch-up-gate protocol. Wide but centralized.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing and defensible: it explicitly replaced a broken immediate-retry loop that burned all retries on one deterministic conflict (CT-1824). Counted as retry-loop because it is a bounded re-attempt of the same work waiting for quiescence, but it is event-driven and not a smell.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/resume-recover.ts:56-107` — await storage-synced event then re-run op (event-driven settle, at-most-one in flight)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `createResumeRecovery.schedule`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / await storage-synced event then re-run op (event-driven settle, at-most-one in flight)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  runtime.storageManager.trackUntilSettled(
    Promise.resolve(synced())
      .then(() => runtime.editWithRetry((recoverTx) => { ... runtime.runner.run(...) }))
      ...
      .finally(() => recovering.delete(elementKey)));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Recovers a list-builtin element whose inline op write was reverted during a resume reconcile (its transaction read catching-up ids and was preempted); re-runs the op once the space has synced so the write lands against confirmed state.
- **Design:** Waits on provider.synced() (a Promise that resolves on a sync event, explicitly 'an event, not a poll'), then editWithRetry re-runs the op only if the result cell is still empty. A recovering Set enforces at-most-one recovery per element key; trackUntilSettled keeps the runtime from declaring quiescence while recovery is pending.
- **Impact:** No wall clock; correctness recovery for a reverted write. Uses editWithRetry (bounded conflict retries) inside. Affects convergence/quiescence semantics: the runtime's settled() must account for these in-flight recoveries or tests would see premature quiescence.
- **Blast radius:** Shared by filter/map/flatMap builtins via elementRuns; invariants are the empty-cell guard, the entry identity check (entry.resultCell===resultCell), and the single-flight Set. A refactor would touch all three list builtins and resume/reload integration tests. Moderate.
- **Insight:** Careful, event-driven recovery — deliberately a sync signal rather than a poll, so it is defensible and load-bearing for resume correctness. Counted retry-loop because it re-attempts (re-runs) the same op after waiting for quiescence, but it is signal-gated, not timed.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/resume-republish.ts:89-155` — await-pending-then-rebuild chain with straggler re-defer (event-driven convergence loop)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `createResumeRepublisher (republishFromConfirmed / awaitPendingThenRepublish)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / await-pending-then-rebuild chain with straggler re-defer (event-driven convergence loop)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  Promise.all(cells.map((c) => c.sync()))
    .then(() => republishFromConfirmed(awaited))
  ...
  if (ok && ok.length > 0) awaitPendingThenRepublish(ok, awaited);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Rebuilds a durable list aggregate (filter/flatMap) after a resume reconcile once the per-element result cells confirm, avoiding writing a partial/shrunk aggregate while elements are still streaming in.
- **Design:** awaitPendingThenRepublish records the cells' ids in an accumulating `awaited` Set, waits Promise.all(cells.map(sync())) (per-cell sync events), then republishFromConfirmed rebuilds via editWithRetry. If any element is still pending (not yet in `awaited`), those stragglers are returned and the chain re-defers on them — a self-re-invoking convergence loop that terminates because `awaited` monotonically grows and settled-undefined elements are honored once rather than re-awaited forever.
- **Impact:** No wall clock; convergence is driven by sync completion signals. The monotonic `awaited` Set is what guarantees termination (prevents an infinite re-defer). trackUntilSettled ties the chain into runtime quiescence. Ordering/convergence-sensitive but deterministic given the sync signals.
- **Blast radius:** Shared by filter and flatMap; invariants are the awaited-Set monotonicity, the pending-vs-settled-undefined distinction, and cellIdentityKey dedup. A refactor risks either partial-shrink writes or an infinite re-await loop; covered by resume-preserve/list-shrink-converge integration tests. Moderate-to-high care.
- **Insight:** Sophisticated, load-bearing resume machinery; the comment stresses sync-as-confirmation over read-time guessing. A recursive re-queue of the same republish work waiting for quiescence — the definitional retry-loop shape — but event-gated and provably terminating, so defensible rather than a smell.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/sqlite-builtins.ts:716-905` — editWithRetry conflict-rebase writeback (event-gated) with stale-hash guard  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `sqliteQuery post-commit effect flush()`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / editWithRetry conflict-rebase writeback (event-gated) with stale-hash guard
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  tx.enqueuePostCommitEffect({ ... async flush() {
    const failQuery = (error) => runtime.editWithRetry((wtx) => { if (result.withTx(wtx).get()?.requestHash !== hash) return; ... });
    const wrote = await runtime.editWithRetry((wtx) => { ... }); } });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Runs the SQLite query in a post-commit effect and writes results/labels/errors back durably, tolerating a replica being behind the authoritative version so the writeback is not lost to a conflict.
- **Design:** Both the success writeback and failQuery use editWithRetry (bounded, event-gated conflict-rebase — see runtime.ts) guarded by a requestHash===hash stale-write check so a superseded query (new inputs -> new hash) does not clobber the current one. The committed-state requestHash dedup (line 712) plays the same role as fetch.ts, surviving abort+retry unlike an in-memory flag.
- **Impact:** No wall clock. The requestHash dedup + editWithRetry combination is the correctness backbone for at-most-once query issuance and stale-writeback protection under concurrent/superseding queries. Retries add latency under contention but are bounded.
- **Blast radius:** Local to sqliteQuery, but the requestHash-dedup pattern mirrors fetch.ts and the editWithRetry protocol is shared runtime-wide. Changing the dedup/guard would touch sqlite integration and label tests. Moderate.
- **Insight:** Defensible, load-bearing: the abort-surviving committed-state dedup is a deliberate choice over an in-memory flag, and editWithRetry is the standard event-gated retry. Counted retry-loop for the conflict re-attempt; not a smell.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/stream-data.ts:174-217` — while(true) stream-reader polling loop  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `streamData (post-commit effect fetch reader loop)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / while(true) stream-reader polling loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  while (true) {
    if (thisRun !== status.run) { controller.abort(); return; }
    const { done, value } = await reader.read();
    ... if (done) break;
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Reads a chunked/streaming HTTP response (Synopsys query, explicitly flagged a demo hack) event-by-event, parsing SSE-style id/event/data lines and writing each parsed record into the result cell as it arrives.
- **Design:** Unbounded while(true) driven by awaited reader.read() (completion-signal per chunk, not a timer). Loop top checks thisRun!==status.run for cancellation via an AbortController; on stale run it aborts and returns; on done it breaks. Each complete record awaits runtime.idle() then editWithRetry to commit.
- **Impact:** The loop is completion-driven (each iteration blocks on reader.read()), so it is not a busy/CPU spin, but the run-generation check plus AbortController is the only stop condition; a stalled server socket keeps the loop parked indefinitely with no timeout. Ordering of writes depends on idle()/editWithRetry interleaving.
- **Blast radius:** Self-contained builtin the header says is deletable; the cancellation contract (status.run generation + controller.abort) and previousCall re-entrancy guard are the invariants. Adding a deadline/timeout would touch error handling and the pending-cell semantics. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** A polling generator loop by the author's own description ("we poll in a generator loop"); a demo-grade workaround with no read timeout and a FIXME acknowledging disconnect handling is wrong. Fragile but low-stakes given demo status.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/wish.ts:1214-1304` — retry-on-failure via memo invalidation  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `createSidecarPatternCache (fetch closure)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / retry-on-failure via memo invalidation
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (fetched) { onSuccess?.(fetched); } else if (options.retryOnFailure) { fetchPromise = undefined; }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Fetch-and-compile cache for sidecar system patterns (suggestion / profile-create / profile-picker). retryOnFailure exists so that a failed fetch (network error, SPA index.html fallback, compile failure) does not permanently poison the cache; the next wish invocation re-attempts the fetch.
- **Design:** The fetch result is memoized in fetchPromise/pattern keyed on URL. On a failed fetch (fetched === undefined) with retryOnFailure set, fetchPromise is reset to undefined so the next fetch() call starts a fresh attempt. There is no timer, backoff, jitter, or attempt cap — the retry is driven purely by the next re-invocation of the wish action (itself re-triggered reactively). A superseded fetch (fetchPromise !== started) resolves to undefined and leaves the cache untouched.
- **Impact:** Determines whether transient failures self-heal. Without a backoff or cap, if the wish action re-fires rapidly against a persistently-failing endpoint it could re-fetch on every re-run (potential fetch storm), but re-runs are gated by the reactive scheduler rather than a hot loop, so amplification is bounded by dependency churn. Affects latency-to-recovery of the profile-create/picker UI after a fetch blip.
- **Blast radius:** Touches launchProfileCreatePattern, launchProfilePickerPattern, launchSuggestionPattern and the three module-level cache instances (suggestionPatternCache, profileCreatePatternCache, profilePickerPatternCache). Refactoring to add backoff/cap would need to preserve the URL-supersession semantics and the deferred onSuccess callback contract. Difficulty: moderate.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing but minimalist: it is a retry mechanism reduced to 'forget the failure so the caller can ask again.' Defensible for system-pattern loading where the reactive scheduler already paces re-invocation. A smell only if wish re-fires in a tight reactive loop against a dead endpoint; a backoff or a short negative-cache TTL would harden it.

#### `packages/runner/src/cell.ts:1074-1094` — bounded re-idle / quiescence polling loop  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `CellImpl.pull`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / bounded re-idle / quiescence polling loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  let round = 0;
  for (; round < 10; round++) {
    if ((storage.pendingCrossSpacePromiseCount?.() ?? 0) === 0) break;
    await (storage.crossSpaceSettled?.() ?? Promise.resolve());
    await this.runtime.scheduler.idle();
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** pull() must return a fully-computed value; a read can lazily kick async loads of cross-space link targets, and each arrival re-runs the read revealing the next hop, so it repeatedly waits for the scheduler to go idle and for cross-space loads to settle until nothing is pending.
- **Design:** After the first scheduler.idle(), it loops up to 10 rounds: break when pendingCrossSpacePromiseCount is 0; otherwise await crossSpaceSettled() then await scheduler.idle() again. The bound is a hard constant of 10; on exhaustion it logs a warn and resolves anyway. Cancellation is via cancel?.() of the temporary effect. Driven by completion signals (settled promise + idle promise) rather than a timer, but the round cap makes it a bounded convergence loop.
- **Impact:** Determines when pull() resolves; affects test timing and correctness of reads across spaces. The zero-iteration fast path preserves prior timing for local pulls. The cap of 10 could in theory truncate very deep cross-space chains, resolving with a stale/incomplete value (mitigated by the warn log).
- **Blast radius:** Touches every caller of Cell.pull() (tests and code needing an up-to-date computed value). Refactoring would need to reconcile with scheduler.idle() semantics, the storageManager crossSpaceSettled/pendingCrossSpacePromiseCount optional hooks, and the manager-global pending pool shared with synced(). Observable behavior: resolution latency and the convergence-bound warning.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing convergence loop, not a naive busy-wait — it is signal-driven (settle + idle promises) with a safety cap. The magic constant 10 is the smell: it is an arbitrary depth bound standing in for true fixpoint detection. Defensible as-is but fragile if cross-space link chains ever exceed the bound.

#### `packages/runner/src/pattern-manager.ts:1363-1399` — editWithRetry with a data-sized retry budget for conflict convergence  &nbsp; 🔴 high

- **Symbol:** `PatternManager.writeBackCompileCache`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / editWithRetry with a data-sized retry budget for conflict convergence
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const writebackMaxRetries = Math.max(16, 2 * importEdges + 8);
  const { error } = await this.runtime.editWithRetry((tx) => { ... }, writebackMaxRetries);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Persists source+compiled module docs into a space on their own transaction; a cold replica discovers exactly ONE stale pre-existing derived edge doc per commit attempt, so convergence needs one retry per pre-existing derived doc. The default DEFAULT_MAX_RETRIES (5) exhausts long before ~24 edge docs converge, leaving the cache permanently unhealed and forcing recompiles on every later cold boot.
- **Design:** Retry budget is computed from the write set: Math.max(16, 2*importEdges + 8) where importEdges is the summed import count across modules. editWithRetry re-runs the mutation, pulling the one conflicting doc each round; conflict-free write-backs commit on the first attempt so the ceiling is only paid during recovery after a compiler-version bump. Final failure throws (throwableStorageError) because persisted refs-only pattern JSON requires a durable closure.
- **Impact:** Correctness/determinism: an undersized retry budget silently degrades to endless recompiles (a real prior bug, CT-1824). The loop is conflict-bounded so it does not burn CPU in the common case, but under storm/recovery it can run many commit rounds, adding latency to cold-boot healing.
- **Blast radius:** Depends on runtime.editWithRetry semantics and the storage engine's one-stale-read-per-commit rejection behavior. Changing the convergence model (e.g. batch-pull all conflicting docs up front) would let this budget shrink but touches editWithRetry, the writeSourceDocs/writeCompiledDocs paths, and compile-cache write-back tests.
- **Insight:** A load-bearing workaround for an engine quirk where each commit only surfaces one stale read. The magic formula (2*edges + 8, floor 16) is fragile: it encodes a storage-engine invariant into an arithmetic guess and would break silently if the per-commit conflict-discovery rate changed.

#### `packages/runner/src/pattern-manager.ts:1335-1353` — editWithRetry (default retry budget) for commit-conflict recovery  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `PatternManager.writeBackSourceCache`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / editWithRetry (default retry budget) for commit-conflict recovery
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const { error } = await this.runtime.editWithRetry((tx) => {
    writeSourceDocs(this.runtime, space, modules, entryIdentity, tx);
  });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Writes source docs for an emitted module set on an independent transaction, retrying on commit conflict (the cache write racing the pattern's own space writes) rather than silently dropping the entry.
- **Design:** Uses runtime.editWithRetry with the DEFAULT_MAX_RETRIES budget (no explicit count, unlike writeBackCompileCache). Retries the whole mutation on conflict; a final failure throws throwableStorageError.
- **Impact:** Moderate: a conflict-driven retry loop on a background write-back path. Bounded by the default retry count, so a pathological conflict storm here could exhaust and throw, failing the source-cache write.
- **Blast radius:** Shares editWithRetry with writeBackCompileCache; any change to the default retry budget or conflict model affects both. Test coverage lives in compile-cache write-back tests.
- **Insight:** Defensible standard optimistic-concurrency retry. Notably it does NOT use the data-sized budget its sibling needs, implying source-doc writes do not hit the one-conflict-per-round pathology; a latent inconsistency worth noting if the two ever diverge.

#### `packages/runner/src/pattern-manager.ts:304-332` — fire-and-forget replication with dedupe-key release so the next child creation retries  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `PatternManager.replicatePatternToSpace`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / fire-and-forget replication with dedupe-key release so the next child creation retries
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  }).catch((error) => {
    this.replicatedClosures.delete(dedupeKey);
    ... });
  this.compileCacheWrites.add(replication);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Replicates content-addressed closures into a child piece's space so a fresh runtime can load it; on failure it releases the dedupe claim so a LATER child-creation attempt retries, rather than retrying inline on the caller's commit path.
- **Design:** A dedupeKey (entryIdentity + space) gates the work; the replication promise is tracked in compileCacheWrites (awaited by flushCompileCacheWrites) and cleared in .finally. Failure logs and deletes the dedupe key. Retry is opportunistic and event-driven (triggered by the next replicatePatternToSpace call), not timed.
- **Impact:** Correctness of fresh-runtime child loads: an unretried failure could leave a child permanently unloadable. The retry cadence is entirely determined by future child-creation events, so a child never re-created is never healed.
- **Blast radius:** Interacts with replicateClosures, the pendingCacheWriteBacks await inside it (line 358, to avoid racing this session's own cold write-back), and flushCompileCacheWrites shutdown semantics. Refactoring to a signal-driven retry queue would touch child-creation call sites and graceful-shutdown flush.
- **Insight:** Deliberately not a timer-based retry; it piggybacks on organic re-invocation, which is elegant but means healing depends on user behavior. Load-bearing for cross-space child pieces (CT-1687).

#### `packages/runner/src/pattern-manager.ts:865-889` — await-leader-then-recurse (completion-signal driven, bounded by concurrent callers)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `PatternManager.loadPatternByIdentity (single-flight follower)`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / await-leader-then-recurse (completion-signal driven, bounded by concurrent callers)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await pending.catch(() => {});
  return await this.loadPatternByIdentity(entryIdentity, symbol, space);
  ```
- **Purpose:** De-duplicates the expensive by-identity load: a follower awaits the in-flight leader promise, then re-enters through the front door to hit the now-populated in-memory indexes; if the leader failed or did not surface this symbol, the follower becomes its own leader.
- **Design:** Keyed by (space, entryIdentity) in inProgressByIdentityLoads. The follower waits on the leader's promise (a completion signal, NOT a timer), then recurses. Each pass consumes one settled leader, so recursion depth is bounded by the number of concurrent callers.
- **Impact:** Ordering/latency only; no wall clock. Reduces redundant SES evaluations. Because it is driven purely by promise settlement it is deterministic and not a flakiness source.
- **Blast radius:** Touches the in-memory artifact indexes and the inProgressByIdentityLoads map. A refactor to explicit shared-promise fan-out (no recursion) would preserve behavior; tests exercising concurrent by-identity loads would be affected.
- **Insight:** Borderline for this audit: it is a quiescence-by-re-attempt pattern (re-queuing the same work) but strictly signal-driven, not timed. Load-bearing and clean; included as a retry-family instance for completeness rather than as a smell.

#### `packages/runner/src/runner.ts:2905-2915` — resolve-then-RetryImmediately re-run  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `Runner.resolvePendingSpaceNamesAndRetry`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / resolve-then-RetryImmediately re-run
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await Promise.all(names.map((name) => this.runtime.resolveSpaceName(name)));
  throw new RetryImmediately(`Resolving in-space target spaces: ...`);
  ```
- **Purpose:** When a handler/action touches an inSpace("name") child whose space DID is not yet resolved, this awaits resolution of every pending space name and then throws RetryImmediately so the scheduler re-runs the same action instead of surfacing the error.
- **Design:** Collects frame.pendingSpaceNames, awaits Promise.all of runtime.resolveSpaceName for each, then throws the RetryImmediately control-flow signal. The re-run is driven by the scheduler catching that signal, not by any timer; retry count is implicitly bounded by names being resolved before the next attempt.
- **Impact:** Determines whether a first invocation is retried transparently vs. failing. Because resolution is awaited before the re-throw, the retry converges (names are resolved on the retry) rather than busy-looping; ordering/receipt reuse relies on event-id stability so retries don't duplicate handlings.
- **Blast radius:** A refactor touches the scheduler's RetryImmediately handling (scheduler/retry-immediately.ts), all three call sites in this file (handler catch 3287, postRun 3254; action catch 3603, postRun 3566), the Frame.pendingSpaceNames contract, and any test asserting single-retry semantics for unresolved in-space targets.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing: this is the runtime's mechanism for lazily resolving space names discovered mid-execution. It is a signal-driven retry (resolution completes, then re-run), not a poll, which is the defensible design; the fragility is that repeated re-entry depends on names actually resolving so the second pass no longer populates pendingSpaceNames.

#### `packages/runner/src/runner.ts:667-669, 1765-1772` — editWithRetry transaction conflict retry  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `Runner.setup / Runner.runSynced`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / editWithRetry transaction conflict retry
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  return this.runtime.editWithRetry((tx) => { this.setupInternal(tx, patternOrModule, argument, resultCell); }).then(({ error }) => { ... })
  ```
- **Purpose:** Runs the pattern-setup write inside runtime.editWithRetry so that a storage-transaction conflict (someone else changed the pattern or argument concurrently) is retried automatically instead of failing.
- **Design:** editWithRetry (defined in runtime, not this file) re-runs the closure on optimistic-concurrency conflict; here the callback re-invokes setupInternal each attempt. Non-conflict callback failures are surfaced by inspecting the returned error (StorageTransactionAborted with 'editWithRetry action threw:' prefix is re-thrown as the real reason).
- **Impact:** Affects convergence under concurrent pattern/argument edits; the comment notes losing the race is acceptable since a later read sees the true value. The retry can re-run setupInternal multiple times, so setupInternal must be idempotent enough to tolerate retries.
- **Blast radius:** A change to the retry policy lives in runtime.editWithRetry, but these call sites depend on its error-shaping contract (the 'editWithRetry action threw:' message prefix) to distinguish real callback failures from conflict aborts. Callers of setup()/runSynced() and their tests are affected.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing concurrency-control retry; the string-prefix matching on the aborted error message is fragile and would break silently if editWithRetry changed its wording.

#### `packages/runner/src/runner.ts:3282-3289, 3599-3606` — catch-and-retry on pending space names  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `Runner.instantiateJavaScriptHandlerNode handler catch / instantiateJavaScriptNode action catch`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / catch-and-retry on pending space names
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (!(error instanceof RetryImmediately) && frame.pendingSpaceNames?.size > 0) {
    return this.resolvePendingSpaceNamesAndRetry(frame).finally(() => popFrame(frame));
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Handler and action bodies may throw while materializing a not-yet-resolved inSpace("name") child; these catch blocks intercept that, resolve the pending names, and retry rather than writing an error result.
- **Design:** Guards on the error not already being a RetryImmediately and on pendingSpaceNames being non-empty, then delegates to resolvePendingSpaceNamesAndRetry which re-throws RetryImmediately. popFrame is deferred (popFrameAfterReturn=false) until the returned promise settles.
- **Impact:** Converts a transient unresolved-space failure into a retried run, affecting whether user handlers/effects appear to fail vs. succeed on first delivery. Frame lifecycle correctness depends on the deferred popFrame path.
- **Blast radius:** Coupled to resolvePendingSpaceNamesAndRetry, RetryImmediately re-throw at 3469, the postRun-path variants at 3254/3566, and frame push/pop bookkeeping. Refactor risk is moderate because the same pattern is duplicated across handler and action.
- **Insight:** Duplicated logic across two node types (handler vs action) is a mild smell; both funnel into the same retry helper, so it is defensible but a candidate for consolidation.

#### `packages/runner/src/runtime.ts:688-695` — bounded quiescence loop (repeat idle+sync+drain until fixpoint)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `Runtime.settled`
- **Category / subtype:** retry-loop / bounded quiescence loop (repeat idle+sync+drain until fixpoint)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  for (let round = 0; round < maxRounds; round++) {
    await this.scheduler.idle();
    await this.storageManager.synced();
    if (this.#pendingAsyncWork.size === 0) return;
    await Promise.allSettled([...this.#pendingAsyncWork]);
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for the runtime to reach full quiescence including async builtin I/O (fetch/LLM/sqlite writeback), whose results re-trigger further reactive work that spawns more async work; repeats the idle/synced/drain sequence until no pending async work remains.
