#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/fetch-program.ts:15,270,296-308` — wall-clock stopwatch timeout on in-flight fetch state  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `fetchProgram action closure (Date.now startTime + PROGRAM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / wall-clock stopwatch timeout on in-flight fetch state
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const isTimedOut = Date.now() - state.startTime > PROGRAM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT;
  if (isTimedOut) { ...state: { type: "idle" } }
  ```
- **Purpose:** The fetchProgram builtin stores a per-input-hash state machine (idle/fetching/success/error) in a durable cache cell. If a fetching entry never completes (tab closed, network hang), a 10s wall-clock timeout resets it to idle so the request can be retried. Bounds abandoned program resolutions.
- **Design:** On transition to fetching it records startTime = Date.now() in the cache entry. On subsequent action runs, if state is fetching it computes elapsed = Date.now() - startTime and, when it exceeds PROGRAM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT (10s), rewrites the entry to idle. The actual abort of the in-flight resolve uses an AbortController; this timeout is the state-recovery half.
- **Impact:** 10s determines how long a stuck program-fetch blocks re-issuing. Because the check only fires when the action re-runs (driven by reactivity), timeout enforcement is not exact — it is lazy, tied to the next scheduler tick that re-evaluates this node. Affects retry latency and can make convergence tests timing-dependent.
- **Blast radius:** Touches the FetchState machine, cache schema, and the addCancel cleanup that also flips fetching->idle. Any refactor to signal-driven completion would remove the polling-on-rerun timeout and its tests.
- **Insight:** Slightly fragile: the timeout only takes effect when something re-triggers the action, so a truly idle stuck entry can linger past 10s until an unrelated re-run. A completion/abort signal already exists (AbortController), so the wall-clock reset is a belt-and-suspenders recovery for the case where the aborting runtime vanished.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/fetch-program.ts:136,139-175,285-293,337` — AbortController cancellation on stop / input change  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `fetchProgram action closure / startFetch (AbortController)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / AbortController cancellation on stop / input change
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  abortController = new AbortController();
  runtime.trackAsyncWork(startFetch(...abortController.signal));
  // addCancel: abortController?.abort("Pattern stopped");
  ```
- **Purpose:** Lets an in-flight program resolution be cancelled promptly when the owning pattern is stopped, so a long/hung HTTP program fetch does not keep running or write stale results after teardown.
- **Design:** A module-scoped abortController is created when a fetch starts and its signal threaded into startFetch. addCancel aborts it ("Pattern stopped") and flips any owned fetching cache entries back to idle inside a manual runtime.edit()/commit. Cancellation is signal-driven, not timer-driven.
- **Impact:** Improves teardown latency and prevents late writebacks; correctness win. No wall clock involved, so deterministic given the stop event.
- **Blast radius:** Coupled to addCancel cleanup and the cache state reset; refactor would touch trackAsyncWork and startFetch's signal handling.
- **Insight:** Clean, event-driven cancellation — the right pattern. Included because AbortController/signal are timing-adjacent, but this is the non-fragile half of the mechanism.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/fetch-utils.ts:9,90-151` — wall-clock mutex lease expiry (TTL on cross-tab lock)  &nbsp; 🔴 high

- **Symbol:** `tryClaimMutex (REQUEST_TIMEOUT const)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / wall-clock mutex lease expiry (TTL on cross-tab lock)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const canClaim = !isPending ||
    (currentInternal.lastActivity < now - timeout);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Implements a cross-tab/cross-runtime mutex for fetch-style builtins so only one runtime performs a given request. A stale lock left by a crashed or slow holder must eventually be reclaimable, so a wall-clock lease timeout (default 5s, overridable per-call via mutexTimeoutMs) lets another claimant take over.
- **Design:** Reads Date.now() as `now`, compares `lastActivity < now - timeout` to decide the previous holder's lease expired. On claim it stamps lastActivity=now and pending=true inside an editWithRetry transaction; the whole check-and-set is done under the storage transaction to stay atomic. Awaits runtime.idle() first so computed inputs are settled before hashing.
- **Impact:** The 5s (or program's 10s) lease directly bounds how long a dropped in-flight fetch wedges a hash; too short causes duplicate concurrent fetches, too long stalls recovery. It is a wall-clock gating decision, so it can make multi-runtime tests timing-sensitive/flaky and affects perceived latency of retrying a stuck request.
- **Blast radius:** Changing the lease model would touch tryClaimMutex, both startFetch callers (fetch.ts, fetch-program.ts), the internalSchema lastActivity field, mutexTimeoutMs option plumbing, and the fetch-mutex tests. Moderate difficulty because correctness of the distributed lock hinges on it.
- **Insight:** Defensible and load-bearing: a lease TTL is the standard way to make a best-effort distributed lock self-healing. Fragile point is wall-clock skew across runtimes and the fixed default; a completion/heartbeat signal could replace the pure timeout but the current design is a reasonable workaround for lacking one.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/fetch.ts:39,81,270-278,342,495` — per-request configurable mutex lease override (mutexTimeoutMs)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `mutexTimeoutForCell / fetchBuiltin action`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / per-request configurable mutex lease override (mutexTimeoutMs)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const value = cell.asSchema(kind.inputSchema).get()?.options?.mutexTimeoutMs;
  return ...value > 0 ? value : undefined;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Lets a pattern override the default 5s fetch mutex lease per request via options.mutexTimeoutMs, threading it into tryClaimMutex. Useful for slow endpoints that legitimately hold the lock longer than the default.
- **Design:** Reads the numeric option, validates finite and positive, else undefined (falls back to REQUEST_TIMEOUT default in tryClaimMutex). Passed as the `timeout` argument to the wall-clock lease check. mutexTimeoutMs is deliberately stripped from the input hash so tuning it does not invalidate the cache.
- **Impact:** Directly tunes the wall-clock lease that governs duplicate-fetch avoidance vs stuck-request recovery; the same flakiness/latency tradeoff as REQUEST_TIMEOUT, now caller-controllable.
- **Blast radius:** Touches input schema, hash normalization (computeInputHashFromValue drops it), and tryClaimMutex. Well-contained but semantically tied to the mutex correctness.
- **Insight:** Sensible escape hatch; load-bearing for slow-endpoint patterns. The care taken to exclude it from the hash shows the timing knob is intentionally orthogonal to caching.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/fetch.ts:309,312-337,442-457,527,542,598,648` — AbortController cancellation on stop / inputs-changed  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `fetchBuiltin action / startFetch (AbortController)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / AbortController cancellation on stop / inputs-changed
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  abortController?.abort("Inputs changed");
  ...abortController = new AbortController();
  await startFetch(...abortController.signal);
  // startFetch: if (abortSignal.aborted) return;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Cancels an in-flight HTTP fetch when the pattern stops or when inputs change under it, preventing stale writebacks and wasted network work. The abort reason distinguishes teardown vs input change.
- **Design:** AbortController created per fetch, signal passed to runtime.fetch. On inputs-mismatch the action aborts with "Inputs changed"; addCancel aborts with "Pattern stopped" and clears the mutex requestId. startFetch checks abortSignal.aborted before writing errors so aborted requests stay silent. Event-driven, no timer.
- **Impact:** Prevents late/incorrect writes and cuts latency on rapid input changes; correctness-positive and deterministic given the triggering event.
- **Blast radius:** Interlocks with the mutex (requestId reset), addCancel cleanup, and startFetch error path. Refactor would ripple across the whole fetch state machine.
- **Insight:** Well-built, signal-driven cancellation — the healthy counterpart to the wall-clock lease. Load-bearing for multi-tab correctness.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/llm-dialog.ts:2597-2611` — Promise.race deadline with setTimeout  &nbsp; 🔴 high

- **Symbol:** `invokeToolPattern (tool-call invocation helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline with setTimeout
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  timeout = setTimeout(() => { reject(new Error("Tool call timed out")); }, TOOL_CALL_TIMEOUT);
  ... await Promise.race([promise, timeoutPromise]);
  ... clearTimeout(timeout); cancel();
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds how long a tool-call sub-pattern/handler may run before the LLM dialog abandons waiting for its result cell to be written, preventing a wedged tool from hanging the dialog turn indefinitely.
- **Design:** setTimeout(TOOL_CALL_TIMEOUT = 120s) rejects a timeoutPromise; Promise.race pits it against the completion promise resolved by result.sink(). finally-block clears the timeout and unsubscribes the sink. Fixed 120s wall-clock bound, no retry, no backoff.
- **Impact:** On timeout the tool call throws and the dialog turn surfaces an error; a slow-but-valid tool could be killed at exactly 120s. The timer is real wall-clock, so behavior is nondeterministic under load and can cause flaky test failures if tools run near the bound. Result resolution is completion-signal driven (sink), so the timeout is purely a safety backstop.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor touches all tool-call sites going through invokeToolPattern, the TOOL_CALL_TIMEOUT constant (line 106), and any test that relies on/exercises tool timeout behavior. Moving to an AbortSignal-based cancellation would need the tool runner to honor a signal.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing safety timeout but fragile: a fixed 120s wall-clock deadline is a classic source of flake and can wrongly abandon slow legitimate work. The completion path is already signal-driven (result.sink); the timeout is the smell — ideally cancellation would flow through an AbortSignal rather than a race against a stopwatch.

#### `packages/runner/src/scheduler.ts:748-816` — shared deadline budget (performance.now) across sync+rehydration  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `Scheduler.queueInitialActionRehydration`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / shared deadline budget (performance.now) across sync+rehydration
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const deadline = performance.now() + Math.max(0, options.timeoutMs ?? DEFAULT_INITIAL_REHYDRATION_TIMEOUT_MS); const remainingMs = () => Math.max(0, deadline - performance.now());
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds the resumed-startup delay: awaiting space sync and the rehydration lookup share ONE timeout budget so a stuck sync cannot double the startup delay. On timeout, falls back to a plain initial run.
- **Design:** Computes a single deadline via performance.now(); remainingMs() re-reads the clock to bound each subsequent step. Runs as a tracked background task; a supersession token cancels it. Timeout maps to a warn + scheduleInitialActionRun fallback.
- **Impact:** Wall-clock deadline gates whether rehydration is abandoned in favor of a fresh run — a correctness-relevant control decision (rehydrated vs recomputed initial state). Bounds worst-case resume latency.
- **Blast radius:** Coordinates with awaitSpaceSyncedWithTimeout and runInitialActionRehydrationWithTimeout (both below). Refactor touches resume/rehydration semantics, the token supersession logic, and startup-latency expectations.
- **Insight:** Well-reasoned single-budget deadline; defensible and clearly documented. Genuine timeout on external progress (sync/storage), load-bearing for resume UX.

#### `packages/runner/src/scheduler.ts:856-877` — Promise.race deadline (setTimeout vs synced())  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `Scheduler.awaitSpaceSyncedWithTimeout`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline (setTimeout vs synced())
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  timeoutId = setTimeout(resolve, ms); ... await Promise.race([syncedPromise, timeout]); ... clearTimeout(timeoutId);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for a space's storage replica to finish syncing but abandons the wait after a bounded time so a stuck sync cannot wedge the initial run forever.
- **Design:** Classic Promise.race between the provider.synced() promise and a setTimeout-backed timeout promise. Clears the timer in finally; swallows a late sync rejection to avoid unhandled-rejection noise. Returns void (caller treats elapsed budget as timeout).
- **Impact:** Timeout on an external completion signal (sync). Determines how long startup blocks on sync; the race outcome gates whether the action proceeds with possibly-unsynced data.
- **Blast radius:** Called from both rehydration and await-sync-before-run paths. Standard, self-contained; low refactor risk beyond tuning the timeout.
- **Insight:** Textbook race-with-timeout guard; defensible and necessary because sync has no hard guarantee of completing.

#### `packages/runner/src/scheduler.ts:1594-1596` — auto-stop timer (durationMs default 5000)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `Scheduler.startDiagnosis`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / auto-stop timer (durationMs default 5000)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  private startDiagnosis(durationMs = 5000): void { startSchedulerDiagnosis(this.diagnosisControlState, durationMs); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Begins non-settling idempotency diagnosis capture and automatically stops it after durationMs, so diagnostic capture is time-boxed rather than running indefinitely.
- **Design:** Delegates the actual timer to startSchedulerDiagnosis (diagnosis helper, not in this chunk); scheduler.ts owns the diagnosisTimeout field (declared line 336, cleared in dispose 1389-1392) and wires get/setDiagnosisTimeout via createDiagnosisControlState (1687-1690).
- **Impact:** Dev/diagnostic-only; the timer bounds how long extra read/write capture runs. No effect on production reactive correctness beyond overhead while active.
- **Blast radius:** Diagnosis subsystem only; the timeout duration and lifecycle are contained. Low risk.
- **Insight:** Defensible time-boxing of an instrumentation mode; low severity because it is diagnostic tooling, not the reactive hot path.

#### `packages/runner/src/scheduler.ts:879-917` — Promise.race deadline (setTimeout resolving 'timeout')  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `Scheduler.runInitialActionRehydrationWithTimeout`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline (setTimeout resolving 'timeout')
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  timeoutId = setTimeout(() => resolve("timeout"), timeoutMs); ... return await Promise.race([this.rehydrateActionFromStorage(...), timeout]);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds the storage rehydration lookup; if it does not resolve within the remaining budget, returns the sentinel 'timeout' so the caller falls back to a fresh initial run instead of hanging.
- **Design:** Promise.race between rehydrateActionFromStorage and a setTimeout that resolves the string 'timeout'. Timer cleared in finally. Distinct sentinel lets the caller distinguish timeout from a genuine no-match.
- **Impact:** Wall-clock bound on a storage read; the race result directly gates rehydrate-vs-recompute control flow. Bounds resume latency.
- **Blast radius:** Paired with queueInitialActionRehydration's shared deadline; consumers rely on the 'timeout' sentinel. Contained.
- **Insight:** Defensible bounded-lookup guard; consistent with the other resume timeouts.

#### `packages/runner/src/scheduler/backpressure.ts:39-56` — bounded retry window (wall-clock, 30000ms)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `CommitBackpressurePolicy.retryWindowMs / DEFAULT_COMMIT_BACKPRESSURE`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / bounded retry window (wall-clock, 30000ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  retryWindowMs: number; ... retryWindowMs: 30_000,
  ```
- **Purpose:** Total wall-clock time a transient conflict may be retried before the committed write surfaces a terminal CommitConvergenceError, measured from the first conflict for a given intent. Ensures a write converges or fails loudly rather than retrying forever.
- **Design:** A duration field on the policy; the actual elapsed-time comparison lives in the commit retry loop (storage/event-execution path, not in this chunk) which reads elapsed against this window and raises CommitConvergenceError (backpressure.ts:108) with attempts/elapsedMs.
- **Impact:** Wall-clock deadline that bounds retrying and converts persistent contention into a surfaced error. Affects how long a conflicted write blocks and when the UI/handler sees failure.
- **Blast radius:** The window feeds CommitConvergenceError construction (elapsedMs) and pairs with computeBackoffDelayMs. Zero-window opts out (first conflict fails terminally). Convergence-storm tests exercise this.
- **Insight:** Defensible bounded-retry deadline that guarantees no silent data loss; the definition lives here even though the clock comparison is elsewhere. Load-bearing correctness contract.

#### `packages/runner/src/scheduler/diagnosis.ts:406-424` — setTimeout auto-stop deadline  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `startSchedulerDiagnosis`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout auto-stop deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  state.setDiagnosisTimeout(setTimeout(() => { stopSchedulerDiagnosis(state); }, durationMs));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds a scheduler self-diagnosis session (non-idempotency / causal-cycle detection) to a fixed wall-clock window (default 5000ms) so diagnosis instrumentation stops collecting after a set duration.
- **Design:** A single setTimeout with durationMs (default 5000) fires stopSchedulerDiagnosis; the handle is stored via setDiagnosisTimeout and cleared with clearTimeout in stopSchedulerDiagnosis (line 434) if diagnosis is stopped early. Guarded against double-start by getDiagnosisEnabled().
- **Impact:** Diagnosis is a dev/debug feature; the timer only bounds how long extra bookkeeping runs. No effect on production correctness or ordering; slightly affects CPU while active.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch startSchedulerDiagnosis/stopSchedulerDiagnosis and the SchedulerDiagnosisControlState timeout getter/setter. Low difficulty; behavior is observable only via the diagnosis report duration field.
- **Insight:** Defensible, self-contained dev tooling. The timer is properly cancellable. Not load-bearing for the runtime.

#### `packages/runner/src/scheduler/pull-events.ts:42-50` — notBefore deadline gate with performance.now() and wake scheduling  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `processPullQueuedEventDuringExecute`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / notBefore deadline gate with performance.now() and wake scheduling
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (queuedEvent.notBefore !== undefined && queuedEvent.notBefore > performance.now()) {
    state.scheduleEventQueueWake(queuedEvent.notBefore);
    return; }
  delete queuedEvent.notBefore;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Implements a scheduled/delayed event: an event carrying a notBefore timestamp is not dispatched until the wall clock reaches that time. Lets handlers defer event delivery to a future moment.
- **Design:** Compares queuedEvent.notBefore against performance.now(). If the deadline has not passed, it registers a wake at notBefore via state.scheduleEventQueueWake and returns without dispatching; the event stays at the head of the queue. Once the clock passes notBefore, the field is deleted and the event dispatches normally. This is a stopwatch/deadline used to gate control flow.
- **Impact:** Directly time-driven event delivery: event ordering and latency depend on performance.now() and the accuracy of the scheduled wake. Affects determinism for any test exercising delayed events; the head-of-queue check means one not-yet-ready event blocks dispatch of that event (though the queue is re-examined on wake).
- **Blast radius:** Touches scheduleEventQueueWake (timer registration), the event queue ordering invariant, and preflight wake scheduling which also calls scheduleEventQueueWake for debounced/throttled dependency deps (lines 79-80). Medium difficulty; localized but timing-critical.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing delayed-event mechanism. Reasonable design (deadline + explicit wake rather than busy-poll), but it is genuine wall-clock control flow and a determinism hazard under fake/real clock skew. The single-head check assumes the queue is time-ordered.

#### `packages/runner/src/scheduler/types.ts:190-198` — notBefore earliest-dispatch gate (type declaration)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `QueuedEvent (type)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / notBefore earliest-dispatch gate (type declaration)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  notBefore?: number;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Declares an optional earliest-dispatch timestamp for a queued event, allowing the scheduler to hold an event until a point in time (a scheduled/delayed dispatch gate) rather than dispatching immediately.
- **Design:** A numeric timestamp compared against the clock by the dispatch loop; if now < notBefore the event is deferred. Declaration only; comparison logic is in the event-queue processing code.
- **Impact:** Enables time-gated event delivery, affecting ordering and delivery latency of events that carry a notBefore. Determinism depends on the clock source used by the comparison.
- **Blast radius:** Event-queue processing that reads notBefore, plus any event-shaping/backoff logic that sets it. Small but part of the timing contract.
- **Insight:** Minor time-gate field; borderline but genuinely timing-related as it bounds when work may start.

#### `packages/runner/src/storage/v2.ts:2530-2563` — Promise.race deadline (setTimeout backstop)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `MemoryReplica.waitForConflictReadRepair`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline (setTimeout backstop)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  timer = setTimeout(() => { logger.warn(...); resolve(); }, CONFLICT_READ_REPAIR_TIMEOUT_MS);
  await Promise.race([readyToRetry(), timedOut]);
  ```
- **Purpose:** After a write conflict, the client blocks inline until the server delivers a catch-up sync that repairs its stale read set (readyToRetry). This 30s deadline is a backstop so a commit cannot hang forever if that sync never arrives on a still-open, never-reconnecting session.
- **Design:** Races the event-driven readyToRetry() promise against a setTimeout(30_000). On timeout it logs a warning and resolves (surfacing the conflict for the scheduler retry path); the timer is always cleared in finally. CONFLICT_READ_REPAIR_TIMEOUT_MS = 30_000 (v2.ts:167).
- **Impact:** Primary path is event-driven and fast; the timer only fires in a pathological never-catching-up state, so it rarely affects latency. Bounds worst-case hang. The 30s value is arbitrary and, if it ever fires under real load, surfaces a spurious conflict.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor touches the conflict-retry contract (readyToRetry on StorageTransactionRejected), the caughtUpLocalSeq waiter machinery, and the scheduler retry path that re-gates on readiness. Moderate: behavior only observable in disconnected/degraded sessions, hard to test deterministically.
- **Insight:** Defensible backstop, not a hot-path mechanism. It exists precisely because the primary wait is a completion signal (an incoming sync); the timeout only guards the case where that signal is permanently lost. Load-bearing for liveness but not correctness.

#### `packages/runner/test/cfc-agent-prompt-injection-demo.test.ts:371-391` — setTimeout deadline guarding a signal-based sink wait  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `waitForMessages`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout deadline guarding a signal-based sink wait
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  timeout = setTimeout(() => { reject(new Error(`Timeout waiting for ${expectedCount} messages and pending=false`)); }, 5000); cancel = result.sink(({ pending, messages }) => { if (pending === false && messages?.length === expectedCount) resolve(); });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for the agent result cell to reach a settled state (pending === false and a specific message count) via a reactive sink, and abandons the wait with a rejection after 5 seconds so a hung test fails instead of hanging forever.
- **Design:** Wraps a result.sink() subscription in a Promise; the sink resolves when the completion condition is met, while a 5000ms setTimeout races to reject. finally() always clears the timeout (clearTimeout) and cancels the sink subscription, so both branches clean up regardless of outcome.
- **Impact:** Test-only. The success path is driven by a real completion signal (the sink), so it is not flaky in the normal case; the 5s bound only matters if the pattern never settles. On slow CI a genuinely-slow-but-correct run could theoretically exceed 5s and produce a false failure, but 5s is generous for this in-memory mock flow.
- **Blast radius:** Local helper used only by the two it() blocks in this file (lines 453, 549). Changing the timeout value or removing it touches only this file's tests. A refactor to a shared settle helper would need the same pending/messages.length settle predicate.
- **Insight:** Defensible test-timeout idiom: the wait is completion-signal driven and the timer is only a safety net with proper cleanup. Minor smell that the settle predicate (pending===false + exact count) is hand-rolled rather than using a shared harness settle utility.

#### `packages/runner/test/cfc-llm-derived-stamp.test.ts:284-301` — setTimeout reject + sink resolve race (Promise deadline)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level test (assistant reply, first case)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout reject + sink resolve race (Promise deadline)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timeout = setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("Timeout waiting for assistant reply")), 5000); const cancel = result.sink(({pending, messages}) => { if (pending === false && messages?.length === 2) { clearTimeout(timeout); cancel(); resolve(); } });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for the mocked LLM assistant reply to land in the result cell (pending flips false and messages reaches length 2), while bounding the wait so a hung reactive graph fails the test instead of hanging CI.
- **Design:** A Promise wraps a sink subscription that resolves on the completion condition; a 5000ms setTimeout rejects if the signal never arrives; clearTimeout + cancel() clean up on success. Completion is signal-driven, the timer is only the escape hatch.
- **Impact:** Test-only. The 5s bound guards against hangs; the happy path is driven by the sink completion signal, not the clock, so it does not add latency or flakiness unless the graph genuinely stalls. Could false-fail on a very slow CI box.
- **Blast radius:** A refactor to a shared 'wait for sink condition with timeout' helper would touch this and the sibling case at lines 400-411; observable behavior (fail-on-hang) must be preserved.
- **Insight:** Standard, defensible test pattern: event-driven success with a wall-clock safety timeout. Slightly fragile due to the fixed 5s constant, but the completion path is correct.

#### `packages/runner/test/generate-object-outbox.test.ts:424-438` — setTimeout deadline racing a signal sink (5000ms)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `waitForPendingToBecomeFalse (module-level helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout deadline racing a signal sink (5000ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timeout = setTimeout(() => { reject(new Error("Timeout waiting for pending to become false")); }, 5000);
      cancel = result.sink((value) => { if (value?.pending === false) { clearTimeout(timeout); ... resolve(); } });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for a result cell's pending field to become false, resolving on the sink callback and rejecting after a 5s deadline so a stuck test fails loudly instead of hanging.
- **Design:** Signal-driven (result.sink) with a 5000ms setTimeout safety deadline; on success it clearTimeout()s and defers unsubscribe via queueMicrotask(cancel) to avoid unsubscribing mid-callback. Proper cancellation on both paths.
- **Impact:** Test-only. This is the good pattern -- primarily completion-signal driven, timer only as a failure bound. The queueMicrotask defer avoids re-entrant unsubscribe. Minimal flake risk unless the runtime genuinely never settles.
- **Blast radius:** Local helper; the 5s bound is a test-timeout safety net that would rarely need tuning.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing and well-designed: signal-first with a wall-clock deadline as a backstop and correct cancellation. The queueMicrotask(cancel) is a deliberate re-entrancy guard, not a timing hack.

#### `packages/runner/test/generate-text-outbox.test.ts:379-392` — setTimeout deadline racing a sink completion signal  &nbsp; ⚪ info

#### `packages/runner/test/llm-dialog-outbox.test.ts:146-166` — setTimeout deadline racing a reactive sink  &nbsp; ⚪ info

#### `packages/runner/test/memory-v2-subscription.test.ts:800-805` — Promise.race deadline via setTimeout(reject)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `it('public close settles a commit held by conflict admission')`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline via setTimeout(reject)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await Promise.race([ closeAndCommit, new Promise((_,reject)=>setTimeout(()=>reject(new Error('close timed out')),250)) ]);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds the test: if closing storage does not settle the held commit within 250ms, fail fast with 'close timed out' rather than hanging the whole test run on a deadlock.
- **Design:** Promise.race between the real work (Promise.all of close() and the held commitPromise) and a 250ms setTimeout that rejects. The losing timer is not cleared, so on the success path a dangling 250ms timer remains until it fires harmlessly.
- **Impact:** Guards against a hang turning into a suite-wide timeout; on success adds no latency but leaves an uncanceled timer (possible op-sanitizer noise). The 250ms bound could false-fail on very slow CI.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this one test. A refactor would clear the timer on settle and/or key the bound off an env var; no external dependents.
- **Insight:** Defensible watchdog for a deadlock-prone close path. Minor smell: the timeout timer is not cleared after the race resolves. Load-bearing as a failure-mode guard, not as normal control flow.

#### `packages/runner/test/scheduler-commit-backpressure.test.ts:397-459, 461-519` — retryWindowMs deadline bounding retry attempts (production timeout under test)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `it("surfaces a terminal error when a transient conflict never converges") / it("fails a zero-window conflict loudly")`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / retryWindowMs deadline bounding retry attempts (production timeout under test)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  commitBackpressure: { baseDelayMs: 1, maxDelayMs: 2, jitter: 0, retryWindowMs: 40 }
  // ... expect(piece.invocations()).toBeLessThan(500);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Exercises the production retry-window timeout: a conflict that never clears must surface a CommitConvergenceError once the wall-clock retry window (40ms, or 0ms) elapses, rather than retrying forever, keeping resource use bounded.
- **Design:** Injects Infinity rejections, configures a short retryWindowMs, and asserts a terminal error appears and the attempt count is bounded (>1 and <500). The window is a stopwatch-based deadline inside the scheduler that decides when to abandon retrying.
- **Impact:** Verifies a production wall-clock deadline that gates abandon-vs-continue on committed writes. Correctness-critical: too-eager abandon drops user writes, too-late wastes CPU. The test pins the loud-failure contract.
- **Blast radius:** Ties directly to the scheduler's retry-window implementation. Changing the deadline semantics or the CommitConvergenceError type breaks these assertions; the bounded invocation counts guard against runaway loops.
- **Insight:** Executable spec for the retry-deadline. The production mechanism it guards is high-severity timing (bounds committed-write retries); the test itself is low, but it documents a load-bearing wall-clock gate.

#### `packages/runner/test/scheduler-event-lineage.test.ts:161-176` — Promise.race negative-assertion window (100ms)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race negative-assertion window (100ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const result = await Promise.race([ idlePromise.then(() => "resolved"), new Promise((resolve) => { timeoutId = setTimeout(() => resolve("pending"), 100); }) ]); expect(result).toBe("pending");
  ```
- **Purpose:** Asserts that a runtime.idle() promise is still pending (has NOT resolved) after a 100ms grace window, i.e. the scheduler is correctly holding an event blocked on a gated origin commit.
- **Design:** Races idle-resolved against a 100ms setTimeout that resolves to "pending"; expects the timer to win. Timer cleared in finally.

#### `packages/runner/test/scheduler-event-lineage.test.ts:187-198` — commit backpressure retry window config (baseDelayMs/maxDelayMs/retryWindowMs)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `describe("scheduler event lineage") > beforeEach`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / commit backpressure retry window config (baseDelayMs/maxDelayMs/retryWindowMs)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  commitBackpressure: { baseDelayMs: 1, maxDelayMs: 5, jitter: 0, retryWindowMs: 250 }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Configures the scheduler's commit-retry backpressure with a deliberately tiny window so tests that inject an unending conflict reach their terminal CommitConvergenceError quickly instead of backing off for the full production window.
- **Design:** Overrides production backoff: base 1ms, max 5ms, no jitter, 250ms overall retry window. Feeds directly into the scheduler's exponential-backoff retry loop that these tests exercise.
- **Impact:** Determines how long conflict-injection tests spin before terminating; also makes retries deterministic (jitter 0). Shrinking the window trades realism for speed and keeps the 1s waitForSchedulerCondition deadline sufficient.
- **Blast radius:** Governs every test in this describe block that triggers retries; changing scheduler backpressure semantics (delay formula, window meaning) would break these values and the terminal-error assertions.
- **Insight:** Test-tuned view into the production commit-backpressure retry loop. The comment explicitly documents intent; a clean, defensible knob rather than a smell.

#### `packages/runner/test/scheduler-event-lineage.test.ts:144-159` — Promise.race deadline (1000ms)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `waitForSignal (helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline (1000ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await Promise.race([ signal, new Promise((_, reject) => { timeoutId = setTimeout(() => reject(new Error(message)), 1_000); }) ]); ... clearTimeout(timeoutId);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Awaits a signal promise (e.g. gate.started or runtime.idle()) but fails fast with a descriptive error if it does not resolve within 1s, so a stuck test surfaces as a clear failure rather than a hang.
- **Design:** Promise.race between the real signal and a 1s setTimeout-backed rejection; the timer is always cleared in a finally block to avoid leaking a pending handle.
- **Impact:** Bounds otherwise-unbounded awaits, improving test failure diagnostics. Adds a 1s ceiling; correct cleanup via clearTimeout prevents dangling timers that could keep the event loop alive.
- **Blast radius:** Several call sites in this file. Removing it would revert to unbounded awaits (hangs on regression); low difficulty, purely test-diagnostic.
- **Insight:** Textbook Promise.race timeout guard with proper timer cleanup. Defensible and well-written; purely test infrastructure.

#### `packages/runner/test/scheduler-events.test.ts:38-50` — Promise.race deadline  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `waitForSignal (test helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await Promise.race([signal, new Promise((_, reject) => {
    timeoutId = setTimeout(() => reject(new Error(message)), 1_000); })]);
  finally { clearTimeout(timeoutId); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds waiting on a promise (typically runtime.idle()) so a hung scheduler surfaces as a labeled test failure instead of the runner-level timeout, giving a clearer diagnostic message.
- **Design:** Promise.race between the real signal and a 1s setTimeout that rejects with the caller's message; timeout id cleared in finally to avoid a dangling timer / leaked op.
- **Impact:** Test-only. Correctly cancels the timer on success. The 1s bound is a fixed wall-clock guard; if scheduler work legitimately exceeds 1s under load, produces a false failure.
- **Blast radius:** Used at several call sites (270, 402, 417, 665). Refactor would replace with a shared timeout util; low difficulty, isolated.
- **Insight:** Defensible diagnostic timeout pattern with proper cleanup. Load-bearing only as a failure-message improvement, not for correctness.

#### `packages/runner/test/scheduler-events.test.ts:399-417` — Promise.race blocked-vs-resolved deadline  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `it(dispatch queued event commits without waiting for server confirmation)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race blocked-vs-resolved deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const idleResult = await Promise.race([
    idlePromise.then(() => "resolved"),
    new Promise((resolve) => { idleTimeoutId = setTimeout(() => resolve("blocked"), 500); })]);
  expect(idleResult).toBe("resolved");
  ```
- **Purpose:** Distinguishes whether runtime.idle() resolves promptly (event commit dispatched without server confirmation) versus staying blocked, by racing idle against a 500ms timer that resolves to the string 'blocked'.
- **Design:** Promise.race; timer resolves (not rejects) to a sentinel so the test asserts 'resolved' won the race; timer cleared in finally (413).
- **Impact:** Test-only. Uses a 500ms wall-clock window as the definition of 'promptly'; correctness of the assertion depends on idle winning within 500ms, so under heavy load a genuinely-correct fast path could still lose and flake.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test. Encodes the not-blocked invariant; a deterministic replacement would need the scheduler to expose commit-dispatch state.
- **Insight:** Wall-clock threshold standing in for a semantic 'did not block' property — a common but fragile way to test non-blocking behavior.

#### `packages/runner/test/scheduler-events.test.ts:764-772, 810-818, 862-870, 901-909, 946-954` — Promise.race deadline (never-settled guard)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `it(settles the commit callback ...) x5`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline (never-settled guard)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const status = await Promise.race([
    commitCallback, new Promise((_, reject) => setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("commit callback never settled")), ...)) ]);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Each of the five tests asserts the commit callback eventually settles (rather than hanging forever) in an error/no-handler path, by racing the callback promise against a timeout that rejects with 'commit callback never settled'.
- **Design:** Repeated Promise.race-with-setTimeout-reject idiom bounding the wait so a regression that drops the callback surfaces as a labeled failure instead of a suite hang.
- **Impact:** Test-only. Guards a real correctness invariant (callback must settle). The fixed deadline is a diagnostic bound; the assertion is really about eventual settlement, so a too-tight bound could false-fail under load.
- **Blast radius:** Five near-identical call sites in this file; ripe for extraction into the existing waitForSignal helper. Low-difficulty consolidation.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing as regression guards against the 'callback silently dropped / hangs forever' bug class; the duplicated inline timeout is a minor DRY smell given waitForSignal already exists.

#### `packages/runner/test/scheduler-v2-cutover.test.ts:33-41` — Promise.race deadline  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `expectSchedulerIdle (module-level helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const result = await Promise.race([
    runtime.scheduler.idle().then(() => "idle"),
    new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(() => resolve("timeout"), 2_000))]);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds a wait for scheduler quiescence so a bug that leaves the scheduler perpetually busy fails the test with an assertion rather than hanging the whole suite.
- **Design:** Races runtime.scheduler.idle() against a 2000ms setTimeout that resolves to the sentinel string "timeout"; the assertion then requires the winner to be "idle". The timer is not cleared when idle() wins, so it lingers until the 2s elapse.
- **Impact:** Determinism knob: on a slow/loaded CI box a legitimately-idle scheduler that resolves after 2s would flip the assertion to failure, making this a potential flake source. The uncleared timer keeps the event loop alive briefly after the test.
- **Blast radius:** Only this test file's helper; any refactor would swap the fixed deadline for an event-driven completion or a FakeTime-driven clock. No production callers.
- **Insight:** Classic test-side watchdog timeout; defensible as a hang guard but the 2s magic number is a fragility smell and the uncleared setTimeout is a minor leak.

#### `packages/runner/test/sqlite-handle-multi-runtime.test.ts:244-253` — Promise.race deadline over settled() drain  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `afterEach hook`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline over settled() drain
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const grace = new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 3000));
  await Promise.race([
    Promise.allSettled([runtimeA.settled(), runtimeB?.settled()]),
    grace]);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds the teardown drain of in-flight async builtin work so a genuinely fighting (bug-state) pair of runtimes cannot hang teardown forever before dispose.
- **Design:** Races Promise.allSettled of both runtimes' settled() against a 3000ms grace setTimeout; whichever wins, teardown proceeds. The grace timer is not cleared if settled() wins, lingering up to 3s.
- **Impact:** Prevents a hung suite when the two-runtime query fails to converge (the exact failure this file probes). The 3s cap trades a possible truncated drain for guaranteed teardown; uncleared timer keeps loop alive briefly.
- **Blast radius:** Local teardown; couples to runtime.settled() semantics. A refactor toward a cancellable settled() would remove the race and the grace timer.
- **Insight:** A deliberate bounded-drain watchdog; defensible given the file explicitly reproduces a never-settling query, though the magic 3s and uncleared timer are minor smells.

#### `packages/runner/test/storage-close.test.ts:56-65` — Promise.race deadline with setTimeout  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `Deno.test("StorageManager.closeNow does not wait for a pending session sync") callback`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline with setTimeout
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  timeout = setTimeout(() => resolve("timed-out"), 50); ... Promise.race([storage.closeNow().then(()=>"closed"), <timer>])
  ```
- **Purpose:** Asserts closeNow() resolves promptly even while a session sync is still pending; the 50ms timer is the losing branch of a race so that a hung closeNow surfaces as a failed assertEquals(result, "closed") rather than hanging the test forever.
- **Design:** Promise.race between the real closeNow() promise and a 50ms setTimeout that resolves to the sentinel "timed-out". The timer is unref'd via Deno.unrefTimer so it never keeps the event loop alive, and clearTimeout runs in .finally to clean up. Threshold is a fixed 50ms with no jitter or retry.
- **Impact:** Introduces a wall-clock race into a test: if closeNow legitimately takes >50ms on a slow/loaded CI machine the assertion flips to "timed-out" and the test flakes. Determinism depends on 50ms being comfortably longer than the expected near-instant resolution.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test. Refactoring would mean replacing the timeout guard with a deterministic fake-timer or an explicit completion signal from the storage manager; touches only this file unless a shared test-timeout helper is introduced.
- **Insight:** Classic defensive test deadline: it guards against a hang but bakes in flake risk. The real signal (closeNow resolving) already exists, so the timer is purely a safety net; a fake clock would remove the wall-clock dependency.

#### `packages/runner/test/storage-close.test.ts:92-101` — Promise.race deadline with setTimeout  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `Deno.test("Provider.destroyNow force-closes after destroy is already pending") callback`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline with setTimeout
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  timeout = setTimeout(() => resolve("timed-out"), 50); ... Promise.race([provider.destroyNow().then(()=>"closed"), <timer>])
  ```
- **Purpose:** Same guard pattern as the first test but for provider.destroyNow(): ensures force-close resolves despite replica.close being stubbed to a never-resolving promise, using the 50ms timer as the abandon branch.
- **Design:** Identical Promise.race construction: 50ms unref'd setTimeout sentinel racing the destroyNow() promise, cleared in .finally. Notably replica.close is stubbed as new Promise(()=>{}) (never resolves), so the timer is the only thing that could resolve if destroyNow failed to force through.
- **Impact:** Wall-clock flake risk if destroyNow exceeds 50ms under load. Because the graceful close never resolves by design, a bug in destroyNow would manifest as the 50ms timeout firing rather than an infinite hang.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test. Same refactor considerations as the sibling test; a fake timer or deterministic completion assertion would eliminate the wall-clock dependency.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing as a hang guard given the intentionally never-resolving stub, but still a wall-clock deadline in a unit test. Defensible but a candidate for fake-timer conversion.

### `packages/runtime-client`

#### `packages/runtime-client/client/connection.ts:253-258` — per-request setTimeout deadline  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `RuntimeConnection.request`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / per-request setTimeout deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timeoutId = setTimeout(() => { this.#settle(msgId); deferred.reject(new Error(`RuntimeClient request timed out: ${data.type}`)); }, this.#timeoutMs);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds every IPC round-trip to the worker so a lost response or a wedged worker cannot leave a caller awaiting forever; abandons the request after DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS (60s).
- **Design:** One setTimeout per in-flight request stored on PendingRequest.timeoutId; on fire it calls #settle (removes bookkeeping) then rejects the deferred with a timeout Error. Cleared in #settle on normal/late response and on abort. Timeout value is a fixed instance field #timeoutMs = 60_000, not configurable at call sites.
- **Impact:** Production correctness/liveness guard on the main IPC path. 60s is long enough that in tests it effectively never fires; determinism is fine. If it fires it converts a stall into a rejected promise rather than a hang, which callers must handle.
- **Blast radius:** Touches every request(): Initialize, Dispose, CellSubscribe/Unsubscribe, VDomMount/Unmount, Idle, etc. Refactor to an AbortSignal.timeout or a shared deadline would touch #settle, PendingRequest shape, and the dispose flow (which relies on the same timeout bounding the Dispose flush wait). Tests assert timeout-independent behavior mostly, so low test churn.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing and defensible: the canonical timeout-per-RPC pattern. Single fixed 60s value is a mild smell (no override for slow ops like compile-cache flush) but adequate. Not replaceable by a pure completion signal since the whole point is to bound a possibly-never-arriving signal.

#### `packages/runtime-client/client/connection.ts:386-403` — bounded wait for Dispose flush confirmation (reuses request timeout)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `RuntimeConnection.dispose`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / bounded wait for Dispose flush confirmation (reuses request timeout)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await this.request<RequestType.Dispose>({ type: RequestType.Dispose }).catch(() => {}); await this.#transport.dispose();
  ```
- **Purpose:** Disposal aborts the lifetime signal synchronously, then sends one Dispose request the worker answers only after flushing pending storage writes, so a normal flush is durable before the transport is torn down.
- **Design:** The Dispose request is exempt from abort-cancellation (no onAbort listener) but still carries the ordinary 60s setTimeout from request(); dispose awaits its resolution, tolerating a timeout or error reply via .catch so teardown always proceeds. Not a separate timer — it piggybacks on the per-request timeout.
- **Impact:** Production durability/correctness boundary: bounds how long teardown blocks on the worker's flush. If the worker never replies, the 60s request timeout unblocks teardown. Determinism fine; test 'waits for worker to confirm dispose before tearing down' uses a held reply plus a 10ms setTimeout to observe pendingness.
- **Blast radius:** Couples dispose() to the request() timeout semantics and to transport.dispose ordering. Changing the request timeout mechanism must preserve that Dispose is bounded and exempt from abort. The connection.test.ts dispose suite exercises this ordering heavily.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing: this is where flush durability meets teardown liveness. Elegant reuse of the request timeout so there is no second timer. The catch-and-proceed on timeout is a deliberate liveness-over-durability tradeoff.

#### `packages/runtime-client/integration/client.test.ts:1059-1073` — Promise.race deadline with setTimeout reject  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Snippet:**

- **Purpose:** Bounds an integration test's wait for a navigaterequest event so a missing navigation fails the test with a clear message in 5s instead of hanging the whole suite.
- **Design:** Classic Promise.race([realWork, timeoutPromise]) deadline; the losing timer is cleared in .finally. Fixed 5000ms budget. Test-only.
- **Impact:** Test-only timeout; affects flakiness only if 5s is too tight under CI load for the navigation round-trip. No production impact.
- **Blast radius:** Isolated to this one test case. A helper like waitFor (already imported here) could subsume it. Trivial to change.
- **Insight:** Minor test-harness safety net. Slight smell that this uses a hand-rolled race while the file elsewhere uses the shared waitFor helper; could be unified but harmless.

### `packages/shell`

#### `packages/shell/integration/blob-upload.test.ts:108-121` — setTimeout deadline racing an onload/onerror completion signal  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `describe("shell blob upload") > it("uploads an image...") > page.evaluate inner Promise`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout deadline racing an onload/onerror completion signal
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timeout = setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("Timed out loading uploaded image")), 5000); image.onload = () => { clearTimeout(timeout); resolve(); };
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds how long the test waits for an uploaded blob image to load via its blobs URL, so a hung/broken blob fetch fails the test with a clear message instead of hanging until the outer test-runner timeout.
- **Design:** Wraps image loading in a Promise; a 5000ms setTimeout rejects with a timeout error while image.onload resolves and image.onerror rejects. Both handlers clearTimeout, so the timer is cancelled on the true completion signal (load or error). Runs inside page.evaluate in the browser context.
- **Impact:** The 5s bound is the only backstop against an indefinitely-pending image load; the actual success path is signal-driven (onload), so under normal conditions the timer never fires. A too-tight bound on a slow CI machine could cause a false-negative flake; too loose delays failure reporting.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this one test. Changing the value only shifts the flake/latency tradeoff for this assertion; no callers or shared invariants. Low refactor difficulty.
- **Insight:** Defensible, idiomatic image-load timeout with proper cancellation on both outcomes. Not a smell. The success path is completion-signal driven; the timer is a pure safety deadline.

#### `packages/shell/integration/worker-runtime.test.ts:22-42` — setTimeout deadline racing worker message/error  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `it("loads the worker runtime bundle in the browser") probe promise`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout deadline racing worker message/error
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timeout = setTimeout(() => {
    worker.terminate();
    resolve({ type: "timeout" });
  }, 5000);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds how long the test waits for a freshly spawned Web Worker to emit its READY message; if the worker neither messages nor errors within 5s the probe resolves as a timeout so the test can fail with a clear message instead of hanging.
- **Design:** A 5000ms setTimeout resolves the wrapping Promise with {type:'timeout'} and terminates the worker; the message and error listeners (once:true) each clearTimeout and resolve first if they fire. Whichever fires first wins the resolve. Cancellation is handled via clearTimeout in both listeners.
- **Impact:** Deterministic upper bound on the probe; on a slow/overloaded CI machine a real-but-slow worker could hit the 5s wall and produce a false timeout failure. Otherwise the message path resolves near-instantly.
- **Blast radius:** Tuning or removing the timeout affects only this test. The subsequent waitFor at line 45 is redundant since probe is already resolved. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Reasonable hang-guard for a worker bootstrap probe. The trailing waitFor(() => probe.type !== 'timeout') is dead-ish (probe is already settled), a minor smell. Load-bearing as a failure-clarity mechanism.

#### `packages/shell/src/lib/debugger-controller.ts:645-655` — duration-bounded diagnosis run (durationMs window)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `DebuggerController.runDiagnosis`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / duration-bounded diagnosis run (durationMs window)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  async runDiagnosis(durationMs = 5000): Promise<void> {
    ...
    const result = await rt.detectNonIdempotent(durationMs);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Runs the runtime's non-idempotent-effect detector for a bounded time window (default 5000ms) so the diagnosis observes scheduler behavior over a fixed interval rather than running indefinitely.
- **Design:** Passes durationMs straight to rt.detectNonIdempotent, which uses it as the observation/sampling window. The controller only guards re-entry with isDiagnosing and surfaces the result; the actual timing/stopwatch lives in the runtime, not here.
- **Impact:** The window length trades detection completeness against how long the debugger UI is busy. Larger windows catch more intermittent non-idempotency but block the diagnosis UI longer. Dev-tooling only.
- **Blast radius:** Changing the default or plumbing affects only the debugger panel and callers of detectNonIdempotent. The real timing mechanism is inside the runner, out of this chunk. Low difficulty here.
- **Insight:** A defensible time-bound for a sampling diagnostic; this file merely forwards the bound. Dev-tooling, not production hot path.

### `packages/toolshed`

#### `packages/toolshed/index.ts:44-92` — Promise.race deadline with setTimeout + AbortController  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `handleShutdown / SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline with setTimeout + AbortController
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timeoutPromise = new Promise((_, reject) => {
    setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("Shutdown timed out")), SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT);
  });
  await Promise.race([ (async () => { ac.abort(); ... })(), timeoutPromise ]);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds graceful server shutdown: on SIGINT/SIGTERM it closes the memory system and flushes OpenTelemetry spans, but abandons the wait after 10 seconds so a hung close cannot block process exit indefinitely.
- **Design:** A 10000ms setTimeout rejects a timeout promise raced against the actual shutdown work via Promise.race. An AbortController (ac) aborts the Deno.serve server. On timeout the catch logs and the process still calls Deno.exit(0). The setTimeout is never cleared, so on a fast shutdown a stray timer can linger until exit.
- **Impact:** Production shutdown path. Determines whether the last telemetry batch and memory close complete before exit; a too-short bound could drop the final span flush, a too-long one delays container restarts. Determinism is fine (exit always happens).
- **Blast radius:** Touches process lifecycle, signal handlers, memory.close(), shutdownOpenTelemetry(), and any deployment/orchestration relying on clean exit codes. Refactoring to a signal-driven completion (no wall-clock) would need memory/telemetry close to expose reliable completion or their own bounds.
- **Insight:** Defensible, load-bearing safety valve — a wall-clock deadline is the standard way to bound external teardown you don't fully control. The uncleared timer is a minor smell but harmless since the process exits.

#### `packages/toolshed/routes/agent-tools/web-search/web-search.handlers.ts:165-214` — AbortController + setTimeout deadline spanning redirect chain  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `ssrfSafeGet`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / AbortController + setTimeout deadline spanning redirect chain
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const ctrl = new AbortController();
  const timer = setTimeout(() => ctrl.abort(), timeoutMs);
  const cleanup = () => clearTimeout(timer);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds an SSRF-safe manual-redirect GET so a slow or malicious host cannot hang the request; the single timeout budget spans the whole redirect chain AND the eventual body read (cleanup is deliberately deferred to the caller).
- **Design:** One AbortController with a setTimeout(timeoutMs) fires ctrl.abort(); the same signal is passed to every fetch across up to 6 redirect hops. clearTimeout runs via cleanup, which is returned to the caller so the deadline continues to cover the body read; on any early exit (bad URL, blocked host, too many redirects, throw) cleanup is called inline to clear the timer.
- **Impact:** Production, security-relevant path. The wall-clock deadline plus manual per-hop host validation is what prevents SSRF and slow-loris style hangs in web-search grounding-URL resolution and page-text extraction. Correctness of timer cleanup matters to avoid leaked timers / premature aborts.
- **Blast radius:** Called by resolveGroundingUrl and fetchPageText with an 8000ms budget. A refactor of the timeout/cleanup contract would touch both callers and the invariant that cleanup is called exactly once after the body is consumed; getting it wrong reintroduces hang or leak risk.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing and carefully designed (the deferred-cleanup contract is documented). A genuine external-call bound, not a smell; fragile only in that callers must honor the cleanup contract.

#### `packages/toolshed/routes/ai/llm/models.ts:505-513` — AbortController + setTimeout fetch deadline  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `loadGatewayModels`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / AbortController + setTimeout fetch deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const controller = new AbortController();
  const timeout = setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), 3_000);
  const res = await fetch(`${url}/v1/models`, { signal: controller.signal });
  clearTimeout(timeout);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds the startup/refresh fetch of the AI gateway's model list to 3 seconds so a slow or unreachable gateway cannot stall model discovery; on timeout the fetch aborts and the caller falls through to skip gateway models.
- **Design:** A 3000ms setTimeout fires controller.abort() on the fetch signal; clearTimeout runs immediately after the awaited fetch resolves. On abort the fetch rejects and is handled by the surrounding try/catch (which warns and returns without gateway models).
- **Impact:** Production model-loading path. The wall-clock bound keeps model discovery responsive and degrades gracefully to a reduced model set if the gateway is slow. Determinism: a borderline-slow gateway may intermittently include or exclude gateway models depending on the 3s race.
- **Blast radius:** Touches loadGatewayModels and the set of models exposed by the LLM service at startup. Changing the bound or making it retry/backoff would affect which models are available and startup latency; the catch-and-skip contract must remain.
- **Insight:** Standard, defensible external-call timeout with proper clearTimeout on success. Load-bearing for graceful degradation; the only subtle effect is nondeterministic model availability near the 3s boundary.

#### `packages/toolshed/routes/health/llm-health.service.ts:81` — AbortSignal.timeout  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `testModel`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / AbortSignal.timeout
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  signal: AbortSignal.timeout(30000), // 30s timeout per model
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds each per-model LLM health-check request so a hung or very slow model does not stall the overall health check indefinitely.
- **Design:** Passes AbortSignal.timeout(30000) as the fetch signal; on abort the fetch rejects, is caught by the surrounding try/catch, and the model is recorded as failed (latencyMs null, error 'Request failed' or the raw error when verbose). All models are tested concurrently via Promise.allSettled.
- **Impact:** Load-bearing timeout that determines whether a model is classified healthy vs failed, which in turn feeds overall status (healthy/degraded/unhealthy), the HTTP status code, and whether a Discord alert fires. A slow-but-working model past 30s is reported as failed — a real correctness/observability consequence.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor touches testModel and, transitively, checkLLMHealth's status aggregation, the /health/llm route status code, and the Discord alert path. Any health-check tests that assert timing/failure semantics would be affected.
- **Insight:** Appropriate, load-bearing per-request deadline for a health probe. The 30s value is a policy choice worth surfacing as config, but the mechanism is defensible and idiomatic.

#### `packages/toolshed/routes/integrations/oauth2-common/oauth2-common.utils.ts:120-126` — AbortController + setTimeout(abort) fetch deadline  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `fetchUserInfo`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / AbortController + setTimeout(abort) fetch deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timeoutId = setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), 10_000);
  const response = await fetch(endpoint, { ..., signal: controller.signal });
  clearTimeout(timeoutId);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds the OAuth userinfo-endpoint fetch to 10 seconds so a slow or unresponsive identity provider cannot hang the login/callback flow indefinitely.
- **Design:** Manual AbortController with a 10_000 ms setTimeout that calls controller.abort(); clearTimeout is called after fetch resolves. On abort/error the catch returns { error: 'Failed to fetch user info' } rather than throwing, so the caller sees a soft failure. Note the clearTimeout runs only on the success path before the awaited json(); an abort during response.json() is not separately bounded.
- **Impact:** Bounds an external network call in the auth path; affects login latency ceiling and resilience to a hung provider. Turning a timeout into a returned error object (not a throw) changes downstream persistence behavior. Modest correctness relevance.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor touches fetchUserInfo and any OAuth provider descriptor that relies on user info; equivalent to AbortSignal.timeout(10_000). Callers depend on the soft-error return shape. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Standard pre-AbortSignal.timeout idiom; could be simplified to AbortSignal.timeout(10_000) to match the llm-health service style. Defensible and load-bearing for the auth flow.

#### `packages/toolshed/routes/link-preview/link-preview.handlers.ts:65-80` — AbortController + setTimeout fetch deadline  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `getLinkPreview (AppRouteHandler)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / AbortController + setTimeout fetch deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const fetchController = new AbortController();
  const fetchTimeout = setTimeout(() => fetchController.abort(), 15_000);
  ... signal: fetchController.signal; ... clearTimeout(fetchTimeout);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds the outbound fetch to the Jina extraction API at 15 seconds so a slow or hung upstream cannot pin the request handler open indefinitely.
- **Design:** Creates an AbortController, arms a 15000ms setTimeout that calls controller.abort(), passes the signal into fetch, and clears the timer immediately after fetch resolves. The timer is only cleared on the fetch settling; the outer try/catch turns an abort into a generic 500 error response.
- **Impact:** Production request-path timeout. Guards handler latency and connection-pool exhaustion. On timeout the whole preview fails to a 500; there is no partial result. Timer is cleared after await returns so no leak on the happy path, though an abort during body read is possible.
- **Blast radius:** Isolated to this handler. Refactoring the timeout value or moving to AbortSignal.timeout() would touch only these lines; observable effect is how long clients wait before a 500 and whether transient slow upstreams succeed. No tests reference the constant.
- **Insight:** Standard, defensible fetch-deadline idiom. Could be simplified to AbortSignal.timeout(15_000), which would drop the manual clearTimeout. Load-bearing for handler robustness; low risk.

#### `packages/toolshed/routes/sandbox/exec/exec.handlers.ts:110-162` — AbortController + setTimeout with client-supplied deadline  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `sandboxExec (AppRouteHandler)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / AbortController + setTimeout with client-supplied deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const controller = new AbortController();
  const timer = setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), timeout);
  ... finally { clearTimeout(timer); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds the entire sandbox lifecycle (ensureSandbox + exec, including the recreate-and-retry branch) to a caller-supplied timeout (default 300000ms / 5 minutes) so a wedged sandbox or slow command cannot hold the handler open forever.
- **Design:** One AbortController is created and its signal threaded into every fetch to the sandbox service (create, check, exec). A single setTimeout fires abort() after `timeout` ms; the timer is cleared in a finally block regardless of outcome. The timeout is read from the request payload with a 300000 default, so it is client-controlled.
- **Impact:** Production request-path timeout covering multiple sequential network calls including a retry. All sub-operations share the one deadline, so time spent in ensureSandbox eats into the exec budget. On expiry every in-flight fetch aborts and the handler returns a 500. Client-supplied timeout means a client could pass a very large value and pin resources.
- **Blast radius:** Handler-local, but the shared-signal design is an invariant: the recreate-retry path (lines 131-150) deliberately reuses controller.signal so the retry is still bounded. A refactor must preserve that. Changing the default or clamping the client value would affect long-running exec use cases. No direct tests here.
- **Insight:** Well-structured single-deadline-across-a-pipeline pattern with correct finally cleanup. The retry-once-on-404 (sandbox reaped) is a distinct control-flow concern layered inside the same deadline. Slight smell: unbounded client-supplied timeout with no server-side ceiling. Load-bearing.

### `packages/ui`

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-location/cf-location.ts:360-364, 405-409, 257, 260` — Geolocation API timeout + maximumAge (cache TTL)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFLocation.requestLocation / startWatching`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Geolocation API timeout + maximumAge (cache TTL)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  { enableHighAccuracy: this.enableHighAccuracy, timeout: this.timeout, maximumAge: this.maximumAge }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds each browser geolocation request: `timeout` (default 10000ms) abandons the fix if the device does not respond in time; `maximumAge` (default 0) sets how stale a cached position may be before a fresh fix is required.
- **Design:** Both values are configurable accessors passed straight into navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition/watchPosition options. The timeout is enforced by the browser, surfacing as a TIMEOUT (code 3) error routed through _handleError. maximumAge=0 means never reuse a cached position.
- **Impact:** timeout bounds an external API call (a genuine deadline that abandons work); maximumAge is a TTL/cache-age gate on position freshness. A TODO (line 74) notes maximumAge should default to 30000ms to reduce battery drain, and TODO line 75 wants a maxWatchDuration auto-stop, currently absent.
- **Blast radius:** Consumers set timeout/maximumAge attributes; the browser owns enforcement so no internal timer to refactor. Adding maxWatchDuration would introduce a new setTimeout auto-stop touching startWatching/_cleanup. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Standard, defensible delegation of timeout/TTL to the platform Geolocation API. The missing maxWatchDuration is a noted safety gap but out of current scope.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-scroll-area/cf-scroll-area.ts:340-360` — setTimeout auto-hide delay (1000ms)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFScrollArea.showScrollbars / hideScrollbarsDelayed`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout auto-hide delay (1000ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  this._hideTimeoutId = globalThis.setTimeout(() => {
    this._verticalScrollbar?.classList.remove("scrollbar-hover");
    ...
  }, 1000);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Keeps the scrollbars visible for 1 second after the pointer leaves before fading them out, the standard overlay-scrollbar auto-hide behavior.
- **Design:** hideScrollbarsDelayed clears any existing hide timer then schedules a 1000ms setTimeout that removes the hover class; showScrollbars and disconnectedCallback clear the timer. Guarded so a re-enter cancels a pending hide.
- **Impact:** Wall-clock timer bounding a UI cosmetic transition; no correctness or determinism impact. CPU negligible. The 1000ms constant is a fixed UX delay.
- **Blast radius:** Self-contained; refactor to a CSS transition-delay or hover state would touch only this class. No test depends on the value. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Classic debounced auto-hide. Defensible and correctly cancelled on hover and disconnect. Pure UI polish.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-secret-viewer/cf-secret-viewer.ts:111-127` — setTimeout auto-hide (30000ms)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFSecretViewer._toggleReveal`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout auto-hide (30000ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  this._autoHideTimeout = setTimeout(() => {
    this._revealed = false; this._autoHideTimeout = undefined; this.requestUpdate();
  }, AUTO_HIDE_MS);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Re-masks a revealed secret value after 30 seconds so a displayed credential does not stay visible indefinitely, a security-hygiene auto-hide.
- **Design:** AUTO_HIDE_MS = 30_000. Revealing schedules the timer; toggling again or disconnectedCallback (line 129-133) clears it. Only armed while _revealed is true; re-toggle clears before re-arming.
- **Impact:** Fixed wall-clock timer gating a security-relevant UI reveal. No determinism/perf concern; the only correctness angle is that the secret stays hidden after the window elapses. Not exercised by timing in tests.
- **Blast radius:** Isolated to this component; changing the policy touches only this class. No external callers. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing for the security intent (auto re-mask) but simple and correctly cancelled. Defensible; a mildly interesting non-cosmetic use of a UI timer.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-toast/cf-toast.ts:310-326` — setTimeout auto-dismiss timer  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CfToast._startTimer / _clearTimer`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout auto-dismiss timer
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  this._timer = setTimeout(() => { this._timer = null; this.open = false; this._emitDismiss("timeout"); }, this.duration);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Auto-dismisses the toast after `duration` ms (default 5000) so transient notifications disappear on their own; guarded so duration<=0 means persistent.
- **Design:** Single setTimeout keyed on the `duration` property; started when open flips true (or duration changes while open), cleared on close, on manual dismiss, and in disconnectedCallback. Restarts on duration change. Handle stored in _timer with a null-guard clear. Emits a 'timeout'-reason dismiss event when it fires.
- **Impact:** Straightforward wall-clock auto-hide; correct lifecycle handling (clear on disconnect prevents acting on a removed element). No hot-path or determinism concern beyond timing-based UX.
- **Blast radius:** Consumers listening for the cf-dismiss event with reason 'timeout' and the `duration` attribute depend on this. Well-contained; refactor risk low.
- **Insight:** Defensible, idiomatic auto-dismiss timer with clean cancellation; the model UI toast pattern. Not a smell.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-tools-chip/cf-tools-chip.ts:414-425` — setTimeout hover-out close delay  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CfToolsChip._onMouseLeave`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout hover-out close delay
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  this.#closeTimer = globalThis.setTimeout(() => { if (!this._hovering && !this._toggledOpen) { this.open = false; } this.#closeTimer = undefined; }, this.closeDelay);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Delays closing the tools panel by closeDelay (default 200ms) after the pointer leaves, so a user can move the cursor from the chip into the floating panel without it snapping shut.
- **Design:** setTimeout stored in #closeTimer; cleared on mouse-enter, on a new mouse-leave, and in disconnectedCallback. The deferred callback re-checks _hovering and _toggledOpen before actually closing, so an intervening re-enter cancels the close. A grace-period debounce over hover intent.
- **Impact:** UX-only; prevents flicker/premature close. Correct cancellation on disconnect. No correctness/determinism concern.
- **Blast radius:** Only the chip's open/close behavior and the closeDelay attribute. Local.
- **Insight:** Textbook hover-intent grace timer, defensible. The re-check inside the callback makes it robust to stale fires.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-voice-input/cf-voice-input.ts:550-554` — setTimeout hard recording cap  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `CfVoiceInput._startRecording (max-duration timeout)`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / setTimeout hard recording cap
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  this.maxDurationTimeout = setTimeout(() => { if (this._recordingState === "recording") { this._stopRecording(); } }, this.maxDuration * 1000);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds recording length: after maxDuration seconds it force-stops the recorder so recordings cannot run indefinitely (resource/upload-size cap).
- **Design:** setTimeout at maxDuration*1000 ms; callback re-checks the recording state before stopping. Handle stored in maxDurationTimeout, cleared in _stopRecording and _cleanup. A wall-clock deadline that abandons/ends the recording task.
- **Impact:** Hard bound on an in-progress operation; if maxDuration is misconfigured the recording stops early or too late. Correct teardown avoids double-stop. Determinism fine given the state guard.
- **Blast radius:** Recording lifecycle and the maxDuration property; downstream _stopRecording -> onstop -> _processRecording chain. Contained within the component.
- **Insight:** Legitimate load-bearing timeout that caps an unbounded operation; the state re-check makes it safe. Defensible.

### `packages/utils`

#### `packages/utils/src/sleep.ts:41-42` — Promise.race deadline (setTimeout reject)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `timeout`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / Promise.race deadline (setTimeout reject)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  export const timeout = (ms: number, message: string): Promise<never> =>
    new Promise((_, reject) => setTimeout(() => reject(new Error(message)), ms));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Produces a promise that rejects after `ms` so callers can Promise.race a long-running operation against a wall-clock deadline and abandon it.
- **Design:** setTimeout that rejects with the given message. No AbortController wiring, so racing this does not cancel the underlying operation; the timer keeps running even if the race is already won.
- **Impact:** Bounds API/fetch calls by wall-clock time; determines whether work is abandoned. The un-cancelled loser timer leaks until it fires, and the raced operation continues running in the background.
- **Blast radius:** Shared helper used in Promise.race deadline patterns across packages; switching to AbortSignal.timeout would touch all call sites and change cancellation semantics. Moderate-to-high difficulty.
- **Insight:** Classic Promise.race deadline. Load-bearing for bounding external calls but fragile: it abandons the waiter without cancelling the work, and the orphaned timer can keep a process alive.

#### `packages/utils/test/sleep.test.ts:27-31` — timer-based rejection (timeout utility)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `describe("timeout") > it("rejects ... after the timeout")`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / timer-based rejection (timeout utility)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await expect(timeout(1, "took too long")).rejects.toThrow("took too long");
  ```
- **Purpose:** Verifies the timeout() utility rejects with the supplied message after the given delay.
- **Design:** Calls timeout(1, msg) and asserts rejection; exercises the abandon-after-deadline primitive that production code uses to bound calls.
- **Impact:** Test-only, ~1ms. Exercises the deadline-rejection primitive that is load-bearing elsewhere in the codebase.
- **Blast radius:** Local; couples to the timeout() implementation contract (reject with message after ms).
- **Insight:** Info-level coverage of a genuinely load-bearing timeout primitive defined in the utils package.

### `scripts`

#### `scripts/benchmark-object-hashing/chrome.ts:46-49` — page.waitForFunction poll with 10-minute deadline  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `main`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / page.waitForFunction poll with 10-minute deadline
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await page.waitForFunction(() => (window as any).benchmarkComplete === true, { timeout: 600000 });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Blocks the headless-Chrome benchmark runner until the in-page benchmark sets window.benchmarkComplete, abandoning after 10 minutes so a hung benchmark does not wait forever.
- **Design:** Astral's waitForFunction polls the predicate in the page and rejects if the 600000ms (10-minute) deadline is hit; the surrounding try/finally closes the browser regardless.
