- **Impact:** Dev tooling only. The 10-minute bound prevents an indefinite hang; polling interval is Astral's default. No production impact.
- **Blast radius:** Local to the chrome benchmark script; a very slow benchmark could exceed the fixed 10-minute bound and fail spuriously.
- **Insight:** Reasonable deadline on an out-of-process wait; the magic 600000 could be a named constant but is fine for a one-off benchmark runner.

#### `scripts/fuse-bench.ts:336-359` — polling while(true) with wall-clock deadline and 50ms sleep  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `benchWrite`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / polling while(true) with wall-clock deadline and 50ms sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  while (true) {
    const elapsed = Date.now() - t0;
    if (elapsed >= timeout) { timedOut = true; break; }
    const current = await readFileSafe(fullResultPath);
    if (current !== null && current !== baseline) break;
    await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 50));
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Measures write-to-result latency by polling the FUSE result file every 50ms until its content differs from the pre-write baseline, or abandoning once the per-iteration timeout (default 5000ms) elapses.
- **Design:** while(true) loop reads Date.now()-t0 against the timeout deadline each pass, reads the result file, breaks on change; otherwise setTimeout(50)-sleeps and retries. The 50ms poll interval is the sampling granularity of the measured latency; the wall-clock check bounds the wait.
- **Impact:** Benchmark harness. The 50ms poll interval quantizes the reported latency (can add up to ~50ms error per sample) and the Date.now() deadline decides when an iteration is counted as an error vs a sample. This is exactly the kind of wall-clock-gated control flow the audit targets.
- **Blast radius:** Local; the measured p50/p95 numbers depend on the poll interval and timeout. A refactor to an event/subscription-driven completion signal would change reported latencies and remove the polling error term.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing for the benchmark's core metric, but polling-based measurement inherently biases latency upward by up to the poll interval; a cell-subscription completion signal would be more accurate. Fragile in that a too-short timeout silently converts slow writes into errors.

### `tasks`

#### `tasks/integration.ts:236-243` — per-test wall-clock timeout passed to `cf test`  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `runPatternTests / enqueue`
- **Category / subtype:** timeout / per-test wall-clock timeout passed to `cf test`
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  [ ...cfCmd, "test", "--timeout", "180000", "--root", patternsDir, testFile ]
  ```
- **Purpose:** Bounds each pattern test invocation to 180 seconds so a hung test is abandoned rather than stalling the whole integration run.
- **Design:** Passes --timeout 180000 (ms) as a CLI flag to the cf test subprocess; enforcement happens inside the cf test runner, this file just sets the bound. Per-file, not shared.
- **Impact:** A too-tight bound falsely fails slow tests; too-loose lets a hang burn CI time. Directly affects test flakiness and CI wall-time.
- **Blast radius:** Every pattern test file. The number is duplicated knowledge with any other place that sets test timeouts; enforcement lives in the cf test runner (out of this chunk).
- **Insight:** Legitimate computation bound. The magic 180000 is a smell only in that it is a bare literal; otherwise a defensible safety timeout.

## Sleeps (timer / hot-loop idling)  (205)

### `packages/background-piece-service`

#### `packages/background-piece-service/src/space-manager.ts:150-174` — polling while-loop with fixed sleep  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `SpaceManager.execLoop`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / polling while-loop with fixed sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  while (this.isRunning) {
    if (!this.workerController.isReady()) { await sleep(this.pollingIntervalMs); continue; }
  ```
- **Purpose:** The core scheduler loop that drives background piece execution. It repeatedly polls three conditions (worker not ready, a piece already active, or no task due yet) and sleeps between polls so exactly one piece runs at a time and due tasks fire when their timestamp passes.
- **Design:** An unbounded while-loop gated on isRunning. Each iteration either sleeps pollingIntervalMs (default 100ms) and continues, or shifts the earliest pendingTask (kept sorted by timestamp) and runs it. Task readiness is decided by comparing pendingTasks[0].timestamp against Date.now(). No event/condition-variable wakeup; progress is purely timer-driven.
- **Impact:** Adds up to ~100ms latency to every state transition (worker-ready, piece-free, task-due). Continuous CPU wakeups even when idle. Timing is non-deterministic relative to task readiness, which makes latency and ordering dependent on the wall clock rather than on completion signals.
- **Blast radius:** Central to the service. Replacing it with signal-driven wakeups (resolve a promise when the worker becomes ready / activePiece clears / a task is pushed, plus a single timer to the next timestamp) would touch pushTask, processPiece, watch, stop, and every test that relies on the 100ms cadence. Observable behavior (poll cadence, latency floor) would change.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing but a clear smell: a busy-ish poll loop standing in for what is really an event-driven scheduler. The three sleep-and-continue branches could each be replaced by awaiting a completion signal. Fragile under fast test clocks; fine for a 60s-cadence background service in practice.

#### `packages/background-piece-service/test/service-modules.test.ts:495, 542, 638, 784, 786, 811, 839, 1213, 1281` — setTimeout(resolve, N) yield/sleep  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `various it(...) blocks (SpaceManager / service / shutdown tests)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(resolve, N) yield/sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  ```
- **Purpose:** These awaited setTimeout sleeps let the production polling/scheduler machinery advance: they yield the event loop so emit() callbacks, the SpaceManager exec loop (driven by pollingIntervalMs/rerunIntervalMs), worker error propagation, and signal-handler shutdown callbacks can run before assertions.
- **Design:** Mostly setTimeout(resolve, 0) to flush the current macrotask turn; a few use small non-zero delays (2, 5, 8 ms at lines 638, 786, 811) chosen to be a few multiples of pollingIntervalMs:1 / deactivationTimeoutMs so the real timer-driven loop ticks at least once during the wait.
- **Impact:** Test-only but a flakiness surface: the non-zero-delay sleeps race the production setInterval/setTimeout scheduler ticks; if the machine is slow or the loop timing shifts, the awaited window may not cover the expected number of ticks, making assertions on disabledAt/status/instances.length intermittently fail.
- **Blast radius:** Confined to these tests, but tightly coupled to SpaceManager's timer constants; any change to pollingIntervalMs default handling or the exec-loop cadence would require re-tuning these millisecond sleeps. Converting to a deterministic fake clock would remove the race.
- **Insight:** Smell typical of timer-driven scheduler tests: real wall-clock sleeps to synchronize with a real interval loop. Load-bearing for coverage of the loop branches but fragile; a mockable clock or an explicit 'tick' hook would be more robust.

#### `packages/background-piece-service/test/service-modules.test.ts:685-688, 710-712, 722-726, 735-737, 808-810` — setTimeout side-effect scheduler (flip state after N ms)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `SpaceManager tests (stop / execLoop branch coverage)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout side-effect scheduler (flip state after N ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimeout(() => {
    (manager as never as { isRunning: boolean }).isRunning = false;
  }, 2);
  ```
- **Purpose:** These setTimeout callbacks flip internal SpaceManager state (isRunning, activePiece, MockWorker.respondByDefault) after a short delay to force the exec loop / stop() barrier to observe a state transition mid-flight, exercising the loop's exit and active-work-drain branches.
- **Design:** Fire-and-forget setTimeout with delays of 0-2 ms mutating private fields via casts, racing the awaited execLoop()/stop() so the loop sees the change on a subsequent iteration; not cancelled.
- **Impact:** Test-only. Timing-coupled: the callback must land while stop()/execLoop is still spinning; if the loop exits first or later than expected the branch under test may not be hit, risking flaky coverage. Deterministic ordering is not guaranteed because it depends on real timer scheduling relative to the loop's own timers.
- **Blast radius:** Only these tests. Sensitive to SpaceManager loop cadence and deactivationTimeoutMs; a fake clock would make the state flips deterministic.
- **Insight:** Workaround to drive private state machine transitions through wall-clock timers rather than an injected control signal. Fragile-by-construction; acceptable for coverage but a candidate for a deterministic-clock refactor.

### `packages/cf-harness`

#### `packages/cf-harness/src/gateway/openai-client.ts:174-193` — abortable setTimeout sleep  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `sleep (module-level helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / abortable setTimeout sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timeout = setTimeout(() => { signal.removeEventListener("abort", onAbort); resolve(); }, ms);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Idles for a fixed number of milliseconds between chat-completion retry attempts so the gateway does not hammer the upstream OpenAI-compatible endpoint immediately after a transport error.
- **Design:** Wraps setTimeout in a Promise. Short-circuits when ms<=0 or already aborted. When a signal is supplied it registers a one-shot abort listener that clears the timer and rejects with the abort reason; on normal completion it removes the listener. No jitter.
- **Impact:** Introduces real wall-clock latency (delay grows linearly with attempt number, base 1000ms) on the retry path. Because it is timer-driven rather than completion-driven, any test exercising the retry path must either inject a zero delay or advance a fake clock or it becomes slow/flaky.
- **Blast radius:** Only reachable from #fetchChatCompletion's retry branch. Refactoring to event-driven backoff would touch the OpenAIGatewayClient retry loop and its unit tests (openai-client.test.ts); the abort semantics are load-bearing for cancelTurn so cancellation behavior must be preserved.
- **Insight:** Clean, cancellation-aware sleep primitive; defensible. The delay itself is a deliberate backoff, not a smell. The only fragility is the fixed timer in tests, which is mitigable via the injectable retryDelayMs option.

### `packages/cli`

#### `packages/cli/integration/fuse-exec.sh:282-289` — liveness-probe loop plus fixed settling sleep  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `top-level test body (mount readiness)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / liveness-probe loop plus fixed settling sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  for _ in $(seq 1 30); do if ! kill -0 "$MOUNT_PID"; then error ...; fi; sleep 0.1; done; sleep 1
  ```
- **Purpose:** After starting the FUSE mount, spend ~3s confirming the daemon stays alive (not crash-on-start), then a flat `sleep 1` to let the mount settle before the first path wait.
- **Design:** 30 iterations of sleep 0.1 checking kill -0 liveness, followed by an unconditional sleep 1. Neither is driven by a readiness signal; the sleep 1 is pure settling slack.
- **Impact:** Adds a fixed ~4s to every run of this integration test and the sleep 1 is a blind guess that neither guarantees readiness (wait_for_path follows) nor is needed if wait_for_path is robust. Pure test wall-time cost and a potential flakiness papering-over.
- **Blast radius:** Top-level test script only. The `sleep 1` is likely removable given the subsequent wait_for_path retries; removing it would speed the test but risks exposing a latent readiness race.
- **Insight:** The `sleep 1` is a classic smell: an unconditional settle sleep layered before a proper polling wait. The liveness loop is reasonable crash detection; the trailing flat sleep is the questionable part.

#### `packages/cli/integration/fuse-exec.sh:484` — fixed settle sleep  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level test body (post-clear settle)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed settle sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  wait_for_piece_value "lastMessage" '""'
    sleep 0.5
  ```
- **Purpose:** A flat half-second pause after asserting a cleared lastMessage value, presumably to let subsequent state settle before the next step.
- **Design:** Unconditional `sleep 0.5` with no condition or signal; runs after a wait_for_piece_value assertion.
- **Impact:** Adds a fixed 0.5s to the test and provides no correctness guarantee; if a real race exists it only reduces (not eliminates) flakiness.
- **Blast radius:** One line in the test script; likely removable in favor of an explicit wait_for_* assertion on whatever state the next step depends on.
- **Insight:** Fixed settle sleep smell; should be replaced by an explicit condition wait if it guards anything, or removed if it does not.

#### `packages/cli/lib/fetch-mock.ts:113-132` — setTimeout delay racing an AbortSignal  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `delayOrAbort`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout delay racing an AbortSignal
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timer = setTimeout(()=>{ signal.removeEventListener('abort',onAbort); resolve(); }, ms); signal.addEventListener('abort', onAbort, { once:true });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Test/mock fetch helper: holds a mocked HTTP response for a fixed real-time delay (delayMs) before resolving, so tests can exercise in-flight fetch behavior; rejects early with the signal's reason if the request is aborted.
- **Design:** A Promise wrapping setTimeout(ms). If no signal, plain setTimeout(resolve, ms). With a signal, an onAbort handler clears the timer and rejects with signal.reason; the timer handler removes the abort listener on fire. Cleans up timer/listener on both paths.
- **Impact:** Deterministic in the sense that runtime.settled() awaits the actual delayed promise, but it introduces real wall-clock delay into tests, which can slow suites and (if delays are large) contribute to flakiness. Purely test-support code.
- **Blast radius:** Confined to the fetch-mock test utility and its consumers (fetch-delay.test.tsx and similar). A refactor to manually-released gates (as the file's own comment notes) would change the mock's API for tests that need precise ordering.
- **Insight:** Well-formed sleep-with-abort; the file comment already acknowledges that precise ordering needs a manual gate instead. Test-only, defensible, low severity.

#### `packages/cli/lib/view/pager.ts:186` — setTimeout(fn, 0) deferral (yield to next macrotask)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `runPager`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(fn, 0) deferral (yield to next macrotask)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (semantics) deps.setTimer(() => semantics.prewarm(), 0);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Warms the TypeScript semantic program after the first frame is painted, while the user is reading, so the one-time program-build cost is not paid synchronously on the first info-card keypress on the interactive path.
- **Design:** Schedules prewarm via deps.setTimer with a 0ms delay, deferring it past the current turn so draw() at 182 completes and the frame reaches the terminal first. No cancellation captured (the returned canceller is discarded); harmless because prewarm is idempotent and cheap-to-abandon.
- **Impact:** Moves a latency spike off the first keystroke to background time. Because the canceller is dropped, a prewarm can fire after the pager has otherwise finished, but prewarm degrades to nothing so this is benign. Adds a scheduled task that tests with a fake setTimer must account for.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor touches only this line and any test asserting prewarm timing. Trivial difficulty. Could be replaced by queueMicrotask, but the intent is explicitly to yield past the paint (macrotask), so setTimeout(0) is correct.
- **Insight:** A deliberate yield-to-event-loop to reorder paint before work; defensible micro-optimization. Minor smell that the returned canceller is discarded, but harmless.

#### `packages/cli/lib/view/pager.ts:65-68` — setTimeout/clearTimeout wrapper (injectable timer primitive)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `realPagerDeps (setTimer)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout/clearTimeout wrapper (injectable timer primitive)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimer: (handler, ms) => { const id = setTimeout(handler, ms); return () => clearTimeout(id); },
  ```
- **Purpose:** Provides the real timer primitive behind the injectable PagerDeps abstraction so the driver's debounce and deferral logic can run against a real terminal in production and a fake clock in tests.
- **Design:** Thin wrapper: setTimeout returns an id, the returned closure clears it. This is the single point where the pager touches wall-clock scheduling; all timing in the module funnels through it.
- **Impact:** Foundational to the debounce (line 131) and prewarm (line 186). Its existence is what makes the pager's timing testable; swapping it for a controllable fake in tests removes flakiness from those paths.
- **Blast radius:** Central: changing the signature ripples to scheduleReparse, the prewarm call, the PagerDeps interface (49-50), and every fake deps object in the test suite. Low difficulty but wide surface.
- **Insight:** Good design: timing is dependency-injected rather than reaching for global setTimeout directly. This is the opposite of a smell. Reported for completeness as the timer mechanism itself.

#### `packages/cli/mod.ts:32` — await setTimeout sleep (fixed 50ms drain before Deno.exit)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `main (success path)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / await setTimeout sleep (fixed 50ms drain before Deno.exit)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  console.log(profileDoneMarker);
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 50));
  ```
- **Purpose:** After printing the profile-done marker, sleeps 50ms so the marker line is flushed to the pipe/consumer before Deno.exit(0) tears the process down. Guards against a profiling harness missing the marker due to an unflushed stdout.
- **Design:** Hardcoded 50ms await-on-setTimeout, gated on the CF_PROFILE_DONE_MARKER env var. No signal-based flush; it is a blind wait chosen to be long enough for the write to land.
- **Impact:** Adds a fixed 50ms to CLI shutdown whenever profiling is active. The 50ms is a guess; too short risks a truncated marker on a slow/backed-up pipe, too long just wastes time. Timing-dependent handshake, a classic flake source if the reader is slow.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch both occurrences (success and error paths) and the profiling harness that consumes the marker. The robust replacement is to await an explicit stdout flush / writer completion signal instead of a timer. Low difficulty, contained to mod.ts and the profiling tooling.
- **Insight:** A workaround smell: a magic sleep standing in for a real flush/drain signal. Only active under the profiling env var, so low blast radius, but fragile by nature.

#### `packages/cli/mod.ts:39` — await setTimeout sleep (fixed 50ms drain before Deno.exit)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `main (error path)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / await setTimeout sleep (fixed 50ms drain before Deno.exit)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  console.log(profileDoneMarker);
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 50));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Mirror of the success-path sleep: after an error, print the profile-done marker and wait 50ms to let it flush before Deno.exit(1), so the profiling harness still observes completion on failure.
- **Design:** Identical hardcoded 50ms await-on-setTimeout, gated on CF_PROFILE_DONE_MARKER. Blind timer, no flush signal.
- **Impact:** Adds a fixed 50ms to failing-CLI shutdown under profiling. Same fragility as the success path: the marker can be missed if the delay is too short on a slow consumer.
- **Blast radius:** Same as the success-path finding; the two should be refactored together into a shared flush helper. Contained to mod.ts and the profiling harness.
- **Insight:** Duplicate of the success-path sleep; the duplication itself argues for extracting a flush-then-exit helper backed by a real drain signal rather than a timer.

#### `packages/cli/test/exec.test.ts:883-885` — setTimeout deferred side effect  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `resolveMountedCallableFile test ("...")`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout deferred side effect
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimeout(() => {
    void Deno.writeTextFile(filePath, "");
  }, 50);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Delays clearing/truncating the mounted file by 50ms so the concurrent resolveMountedCallableFile call under test observes the file mid-flight, exercising a race between resolution and an external write.
- **Design:** A fire-and-forget setTimeout with a fixed 50ms delay; no clearTimeout, no cancellation, resolution is awaited independently rather than being sequenced against the timer.
- **Impact:** Test-only. The 50ms constant is a wall-clock guess about relative ordering; if resolveMountedCallableFile ever slows below 50ms the intended interleaving changes, making the test time-fragile though not currently flaky.
- **Blast radius:** Isolated to this one test case; refactoring to a deterministic hook (e.g. an await point injected into the resolver) would remove the timing assumption but touch only this file.
- **Insight:** Classic timer-as-ordering smell in a test; replaceable by an explicit synchronization signal, but low stakes.

#### `packages/cli/test/fetch-mock.test.ts:153` — setTimeout(0) macrotask yield  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `makeMockFetch suite - tick() helper`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(0) macrotask yield
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const tick = () => new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 0));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Yields one macrotask so a 0ms timer fires before the mock's 40ms delayMs timer, letting the test assert the delayed fetch has NOT yet resolved.
- **Design:** A zero-delay setTimeout wrapped in a Promise, awaited once between kicking off the fetch and checking the done flag; relies on 0ms timers ordering ahead of 40ms timers.
- **Impact:** Test-only ordering primitive. Correctness depends on the runtime honoring 0ms-before-40ms timer ordering, which Deno does; not flaky in practice.
- **Blast radius:** Confined to the delayMs tests in this file; if makeMockFetch stopped using real timers the helper would become unnecessary.
- **Insight:** Deliberate, minimal macrotask yield to observe an in-flight delayed response; defensible test scaffolding.

#### `packages/cli/test/view-pager-cov.test.ts:53-114` — fake timer registry + fireTimers pump  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `makeFake - PagerTty.read / deps.setTimer`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fake timer registry + fireTimers pump
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimer: (h) => { const id = nextTimer++; timers.set(id, h); return () => timers.delete(id); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Provides a deterministic fake for the pager's debounce timer: setTimer registers a handler and returns a cancel function, and the read loop's `fireTimers` step synchronously invokes all pending timer handlers, replacing real setTimeout so debounced reparses can be driven without wall-clock delay.
- **Design:** A Map<number, handler> with a monotonically increasing id; the read() for(;;) loop pumps steps and, on a fireTimers step, drains and clears all registered timers by calling each handler. Cancel deletes the id from the map. This models the production pager's setTimer/clearTimer debounce seam.
- **Impact:** Test-only harness that makes the production debounce deterministic. It mirrors the production timer contract (register returns a canceller), so the tests can assert scheduling, rescheduling (clear+reschedule), and cleanup of pending timers on exit.
- **Blast radius:** Tightly coupled to the pager's PagerDeps.setTimer signature and debounce behavior; any change to how the pager schedules/cancels reparse timers must update this fake and the fireTimers pump.
- **Insight:** Well-designed injectable-timer seam that eliminates real timing from a UI debounce test. The for(;;) read loop is a controlled test pump, not a busy-wait.

#### `packages/cli/test/view-pager-pty.test.ts:71` — fixed boot-delay sleep  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `runInteractive (feed IIFE)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed boot-delay sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await sleep(startMs); // let deno boot, draw the first frame, enter the read loop
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits a fixed startMs (default 1800ms) before sending any keystrokes so the spawned Deno CLI has time to boot, render the first frame, and enter its terminal read loop.
- **Design:** Single fixed-duration sleep at the start of the feed loop; the value is a guess sized to cover process spawn plus first render. No readiness signal from the child.
- **Impact:** Directly determines whether the first keystroke is consumed; too short and inputs are dropped (test flake), too long and every test case pays the latency. 1800ms x number of PTY cases dominates this file's runtime.
- **Blast radius:** Paired with the 20000ms timeout; all Step sequences assume the child is ready after this. A refactor to a real readiness handshake (wait for first frame bytes on stdout) would remove the guess and could shrink startMs to near-zero.
- **Insight:** The most fragile timing in the file: a fixed boot sleep racing an unobserved child startup. Prime candidate to replace with an output-driven wait-for-first-frame barrier.

#### `packages/cli/test/view-pager-pty.test.ts:74, 134-141, 154-157, 168` — per-keystroke inter-step delay  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `runInteractive (feed loop) and test cases`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / per-keystroke inter-step delay
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await writer.write(ENC.encode(step.send)); if (step.waitMs) await sleep(step.waitMs);
  ```
- **Purpose:** After sending each keystroke, optionally pauses waitMs so the pager can process the input and redraw before the next key is sent (e.g. 400ms to pass the reparse debounce, 150ms for the save prompt to appear).
- **Design:** Reads step.waitMs from each Step and sleeps that many ms between writes; values are per-case hand-tuned (40/60/120/150/400ms). Notably line 155's 400ms is explicitly sized to outlast the pager's reparse debounce.
- **Impact:** These delays synchronize the test with the pager's internal debounce/redraw timing; wrong values cause dropped or interleaved input and flaky assertions. The 400ms comment ties the test directly to a production debounce interval in pager.ts.
- **Blast radius:** Every interactive test case's Step array carries these magic numbers. If the pager's debounce interval changes, the 400ms wait must change too — an implicit coupling between test and production timing that a comment, not a shared constant, records.
- **Insight:** Timing smell coupling test to an undeclared production debounce value. Load-bearing for coverage of the debounced-reparse and save-prompt paths, but brittle; sharing the debounce constant or waiting on rendered output would harden it.

#### `packages/cli/test/view-pager-pty.test.ts:14` — setTimeout promise sleep helper  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level (sleep helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout promise sleep helper
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const sleep = (ms: number) => new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, ms));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Provides an async delay used throughout the PTY test to pace keystrokes and allow the child CLI to boot and redraw between inputs.
- **Design:** A one-line Promise wrapping setTimeout with a millisecond argument; no cancellation, no signal, purely timer-driven resumption.
- **Impact:** This is the root sleep primitive for the whole file; because the test drives a real pseudo-terminal, timings gate whether keystrokes land after the child has drawn a frame, making it a source of flakiness on slow/loaded CI machines.
- **Blast radius:** Every await sleep(...) call in this file depends on it (boot wait, per-step waits). Replacing with an output-driven barrier (wait for expected screen content) would touch runInteractive and every Step's waitMs value.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing test scaffolding but a classic timing smell: the correct fix is to wait on observed terminal output rather than fixed millisecond guesses. Defensible only because driving `script`/PTY output synchronization is genuinely hard.

### `packages/deno-web-test`

#### `packages/deno-web-test/test/bundle-retry.test.ts:16-31` — setTimeout monkeypatch (instant fire, delay recording)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `withInstantTimers`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout monkeypatch (instant fire, delay recording)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  globalThis.setTimeout = ((cb, delay?) => { delays.push(delay ?? 0); cb(); return 0; }) as typeof setTimeout;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Test scaffold that replaces the global setTimeout so backoff-driven retry tests run instantly while capturing each requested delay for assertion; restored in a finally.
- **Design:** Swaps globalThis.setTimeout for a shim that records the delay and invokes the callback synchronously, runs the test body, then restores the original in finally. Enables assertions like assertEquals(delays, [250,500,1000,2000]).
- **Impact:** Test-only; makes the retry/backoff schedule deterministic and fast. No production effect.
- **Blast radius:** Only these tests. Confirms the backoff schedules of sleepForRetry / runBundleWithRetry; if those change, the asserted delay arrays here must change too.
- **Insight:** Clean deterministic testing of timing code by dependency-injecting the timer — the right pattern; noted so the sleep mechanism is not silently dropped.

#### `packages/deno-web-test/utils.ts:257-260` — timer-based exponential-backoff sleep  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `sleepForRetry`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / timer-based exponential-backoff sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const delayMs = 250 * 2 ** (attempt - 1); await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, delayMs));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Provides the exponential-backoff delay between bundle retry attempts, idling execution before re-attempting the transient-failing bundle.
- **Design:** Pure function of attempt: 250 * 2^(attempt-1) ms via setTimeout-wrapped promise. No cancellation or jitter. Tests replace globalThis.setTimeout with a synchronous shim to make it instant and record delays.
- **Impact:** The single point that turns the retry loop into a wall-clock wait; contributes all of runBundleWithRetry's added latency. Deterministic only because tests stub setTimeout.
- **Blast radius:** Called by runBundleWithRetry (utils.ts) and exercised directly by bundle-retry.test.ts (asserts [250,500,1000]). A change to the base/multiplier ripples into both retry-schedule assertions.
- **Insight:** Textbook backoff sleep; fine. Only smell is the duplicated 250 * 2^(attempt-1) formula also present verbatim in browser.ts launchWithRetry, inviting a shared helper.

### `packages/fs-sync-example`

#### `packages/fs-sync-example/src/daemon.ts:228-233` — debounce  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `runSyncLoop / scheduleSync`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / debounce
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const debouncedSync = debounce(sync, 100);
  function scheduleSync() { syncAgain = true; debouncedSync(); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Coalesces rapid-fire triggers (filesystem watch events and edit-queue sink notifications) into a single sync run, so a burst of writes does not fire a sync per event.
- **Design:** Uses @std/async debounce with a fixed 100ms trailing delay. Every trigger source (Deno.watchFs loop, editsCell.sink, initial call) routes through scheduleSync, which sets the syncAgain flag then calls the debounced function; the timer resets on each call.
- **Impact:** Introduces a fixed 100ms latency floor between a change and the sync reacting. Under steady change traffic it can starve (timer keeps resetting). Affects observable sync latency and could make timing-sensitive tests flaky, but keeps CPU/IO bounded.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch every trigger path (watcher loop, editsCell.sink, initial scheduleSync). The syncAgain flag interlock with the debounce would need re-thinking. Any test asserting sync timing after a write depends on this delay. Low-to-moderate difficulty.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing coalescing typical of a filesystem daemon; the 100ms is a magic number tuned by feel. Could be replaced by an explicit run-again latch (which already partly exists via syncAgain) plus immediate scheduling, but debounce is defensible here.

### `packages/fuse`

#### `packages/fuse/cell-bridge.test.ts:1118, 1120` — setTimeout(0) macrotask yield  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `Deno.test 'CellBridge queues piece prop rebuilds for the same prop'`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(0) macrotask yield
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Yields to the event loop so the enqueued rebuild job (which starts asynchronously) can begin before the test asserts on the recorded events array, verifying the queue runs one job at a time.
- **Design:** Two setTimeout(0) awaits interleaved with enqueue() calls to let the first job start and stall on firstCanFinish before the second is enqueued. Timing-based rather than awaiting a job-started signal.
- **Impact:** Test-only ordering hack; if the rebuild scheduling changed to defer more than one macrotask, the assertions ['start-first'] could become flaky. Zero production impact.
- **Blast radius:** Only this test. If enqueuePiecePropRebuild's scheduling changes, these yields may need adjusting. Trivial.
- **Insight:** Classic macrotask-yield to observe async scheduling; a signal-based hook (job-started callback) would be sturdier but this is fine for a unit test.

#### `packages/fuse/cell-bridge.test.ts:1566, 2309, 2365, 2416` — fixed setTimeout(250ms) wait for debounced rebuild  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `Deno.test summary-rebuild / status-metrics / undefined-sink tests`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed setTimeout(250ms) wait for debounced rebuild
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  resultCell.set({ summary: 'after' });
  // Wait for debounce (150ms) + rebuild
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 250));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits out the production debounced projection rebuild (comment says 150ms debounce) plus rebuild execution before asserting the regenerated pieces.json / result file / status metrics reflect the new cell value.
- **Design:** Mutates the result cell, then sleeps a fixed 250ms chosen as 150ms debounce plus ~100ms slack for the rebuild to finish. Purely wall-clock; no signal that the rebuild completed is awaited.
- **Impact:** Test-only but the highest flakiness risk in the file: a 250ms budget over a 150ms debounce leaves only ~100ms for the async rebuild, so a slow CI machine can miss the assertion. Also inflates test runtime (four tests each pay 250ms). No production impact.
- **Blast radius:** Only these four tests. Coupled to the debounce interval baked into cell-bridge.ts (150ms); changing that interval silently invalidates the 250ms budget. Trivial to edit but fragile.
- **Insight:** Sleeping past a debounce is the canonical brittle test pattern. The bridge exposes status.rebuilds metrics and pieceSubs cancellation, so a poll-until or an onCfcProjectionRebuilt/onRebuild completion hook would remove the guesswork; strong candidate for a signal-based rewrite.

#### `packages/fuse/cell-bridge.test.ts:1940, 2078, 2138` — fixed setTimeout(10ms) wait for deferred rename  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `Deno.test subscribePiece rename / FS-projection-switch tests`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed setTimeout(10ms) wait for deferred rename
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  resultCell.set({});
  // rename is deferred via setTimeout(0)
  await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 10));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for the production code's deferred (setTimeout(0)-scheduled) directory rename / FS-projection reconciliation to run after a result-cell sink fires, before asserting the tree was updated.
- **Design:** Fires a synchronous cell mutation, then sleeps a fixed 10ms to let the deferred handler execute. The comment at 1936 explicitly notes the production rename is deferred via setTimeout(0), so 10ms is a padding margin over one macrotask.
- **Impact:** Test-only. A 10ms fixed wait is a flakiness risk under CI load if the deferred work ever takes longer than 10ms, though setTimeout(0) work should complete well within it. No production effect.
- **Blast radius:** Only these subscribePiece tests. Tied to the production setTimeout(0) deferral in cell-bridge.ts; if that deferral grows a debounce, these waits break. Trivial per-test.
- **Insight:** Sleeping to observe a timer-deferred side effect is inherently racy; a completion signal or fake timers would be more deterministic. Acceptable for a small margin, but a smell repeated across tests.

#### `packages/fuse/cell-bridge.ts:2812-2863` — debounce (150ms trailing)  &nbsp; 🔴 high

- **Symbol:** `CellBridge.subscribePiece (input/result sink debounce)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / debounce (150ms trailing)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (debounceTimer !== undefined) clearTimeout(debounceTimer);
  debounceTimer = setTimeout(() => { ... this.invalidateRootPropCache(...); await this.enqueuePiecePropRebuild(...); }, 150);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Debounce cell-change notifications for a piece's input/result so the reactive graph's multiple intermediate updates collapse into a single subtree rebuild after it settles.
- **Design:** Each sink event clears the prior 150ms timer and re-arms it; only the trailing edge (150ms of quiet) triggers invalidate + rebuild. The cancel closure also clears the outstanding timer on unsubscribe. Fixed 150ms window.
- **Impact:** Trades rebuild freshness for CPU: a stream of updates yields one rebuild 150ms after the last. Directly affects how quickly FUSE reflects external mutations and is a prime source of test timing sensitivity (tests must wait >150ms or force a flush).
- **Blast radius:** The sink registration, debounceTimer closure, invalidateRootPropCache, enqueuePiecePropRebuild, and cancel cleanup. Any integration test that writes then reads must account for this 150ms; tuning it shifts latency/CPU tradeoff globally.
- **Insight:** A hard-coded 150ms debounce standing in for a real 'graph settled' signal (which runtime.idle could provide). Load-bearing but fragile: the magic constant couples correctness of timing-sensitive tests to a guess about settle time.

#### `packages/fuse/cell-bridge.ts:348-351` — exponential backoff (capped)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `CellBridge._reconnectDelayMs`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / exponential backoff (capped)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  return Math.min(2000 * Math.pow(2, this._disconnectCount - 1), 30_000);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Compute the delay before the next reconnection attempt after the FUSE backend connection is lost, so probes back off rather than hammering an unavailable backend.
- **Design:** Pure function of _disconnectCount: 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s doubling, capped at 30s. No jitter. Feeds _scheduleReconnect's setTimeout delay.
- **Impact:** Controls reconnect cadence and CPU/network load during outages. Deterministic given the counter, but the counter is bumped both by markDisconnected and by each failed _attemptReconnect so growth is monotonic across an outage.
- **Blast radius:** Callers: _scheduleReconnect, markDisconnected (log message), _attemptReconnect. Changing the schedule affects observable reconnect latency and the wording in the READ-ONLY log line; no tests appear to assert the exact numbers here.
- **Insight:** Standard defensible backoff. Lack of jitter is a minor smell for a single-client mount but harmless (no thundering herd). Load-bearing for graceful degradation.

#### `packages/fuse/cell-bridge.ts:353-362` — setTimeout reconnect scheduler with unref  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `CellBridge._scheduleReconnect`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout reconnect scheduler with unref
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timerId = setTimeout(() => { this._reconnectTimer = null; this._attemptReconnect(); }, this._reconnectDelayMs());
  Deno.unrefTimer(timerId);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Schedule a deferred reconnection attempt after the backoff delay while keeping the timer alive across event-loop turns.
- **Design:** Clears any existing timer first, sets a new setTimeout at the backoff delay, then Deno.unrefTimer so the pending reconnect does not keep the Deno process alive. Stores the id in _reconnectTimer for later cancellation.
- **Impact:** Timer-driven idle-then-resume. unrefTimer ensures a hung reconnect loop never blocks process exit. Correctly single-flighted by clearing the prior timer.
- **Blast radius:** Touches _reconnectTimer field, markDisconnected, _attemptReconnect. A refactor to event-driven reconnect (e.g. transport 'open' signal) would remove this timer entirely but requires the transport to expose a reconnect signal.
- **Insight:** The unref is a nice touch that prevents the mount from wedging shutdown. Timer-based because there is no completion signal for 'backend came back'; a workaround, not fragile.

#### `packages/fuse/cell-bridge.ts:654-662` — polling while-loop with 50ms setTimeout sleep  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `CellBridge.connectSpace`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / polling while-loop with 50ms setTimeout sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  while (this.connecting.has(spaceName)) {
    await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 50));
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** De-duplicate concurrent connectSpace calls: a second caller waits for an in-flight connection to finish rather than starting its own.
- **Design:** Busy-polls the `connecting` Set every 50ms until the flag clears, then reads the resulting state. No completion promise is shared; it polls a boolean flag.
- **Impact:** Latency of the waiting caller is quantized to 50ms and it wakes repeatedly regardless of progress. If the in-flight connect throws without a state being set, the waiter throws after the flag clears. Non-deterministic wake timing; minor test flakiness risk.
- **Blast radius:** Only within connectSpace and its `connecting` guard. Replaceable by storing an in-flight Promise per space in a Map and awaiting it directly — that would remove the poll and propagate errors precisely. Low-difficulty refactor.
- **Insight:** Clear smell: a poll-a-flag pattern where a shared in-flight promise would be cleaner and event-driven. Load-bearing for correctness (prevents duplicate space trees) but the mechanism is a workaround.

#### `packages/fuse/mod.ts:1753-1771` — recursive setTimeout debounce with retry-on-busy  &nbsp; 🔴 high

- **Symbol:** `scheduleFlush`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / recursive setTimeout debounce with retry-on-busy
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const timer = setTimeout(() => { scheduledFlushes.delete(handle); if (handle.flushing) { scheduleFlush(handle, 10); return; } flushHandle(handle)... }, delayMs);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Debounce/coalesce multi-write save sequences (markdown editors emit several small writes plus flushes) so the daemon commits settled data instead of intermediate chunks, and provides a safety-net flush if flush()/release() never arrive.
- **Design:** setTimeout with caller-supplied delayMs (25ms markdown, 500ms write safety-net); clearScheduledFlush cancels a pending timer, and each schedule replaces the prior one via a WeakMap. If a flush is already in flight when the timer fires, it re-schedules itself at a fixed 10ms to retry once the buffer is stable.
- **Impact:** Timing-value-dependent: too short risks committing partial saves, too long adds write latency. The reset-on-each-write behavior makes coalescing dependent on inter-write spacing, which is nondeterministic and a source of flush-timing races.
- **Blast radius:** scheduledFlushes WeakMap, clearScheduledFlush, write/flush/release callbacks, and writeStats all coordinate here. Replacing the timer debounce with an idle/quiescence signal from the writer would be a substantial refactor. High difficulty.
- **Insight:** Fragile: relies on magic numbers (25/500/10 ms) tuned to editor and Docker VirtioFS behavior described in comments. This is a workaround for a missing end-of-write signal from the FUSE/VFS layer; load-bearing but brittle.

#### `packages/fuse/mod.ts:1834-1839` — deferred safety-net flush (setTimeout via scheduleFlush, 500ms)  &nbsp; 🔴 high

- **Symbol:** `writeCb (Deno.UnsafeCallback write handler)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / deferred safety-net flush (setTimeout via scheduleFlush, 500ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  scheduleFlush(handle, 500);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Safety net: schedule a deferred flush in case flush()/release() never arrive, because Docker Desktop VirtioFS on macOS doesn't forward those through FUSE-T/NFS mounts, leaving writes buffered forever.
- **Design:** Each write() resets the 500ms timer via scheduleFlush, so rapid multi-write sequences coalesce and only flush after writes settle for 500ms.
- **Impact:** Without this, data can be lost/buffered indefinitely on certain mounts. The 500ms window trades durability latency for coalescing; test flakiness possible if assertions read before the timer fires.
- **Blast radius:** Same machinery as scheduleFlush; changing the delay affects durability guarantees and any integration test timing. Moderate.
- **Insight:** A workaround for a platform bug (missing flush forwarding). Load-bearing on affected mounts; the 500ms is a guess. Ideally driven by a real close/flush signal.

#### `packages/fuse/mod.ts:1877-1891` — conditional short debounce (scheduleFlush, 25ms)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `flushCb (Deno.UnsafeCallback flush handler)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / conditional short debounce (scheduleFlush, 25ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (shouldDelay) { scheduleFlush(handle, 25); } else { clearScheduledFlush(handle); flushHandle(handle)... }
  ```
- **Purpose:** For markdown value writes and truncate-pending handles, delay the flush 25ms so an intermediate empty/truncated buffer from a multi-write save is not committed; otherwise flush immediately.
- **Design:** Branches on truncatePending or markdown fsProjection; the delaying branch defers via scheduleFlush(25), the immediate branch cancels any pending timer and flushes now.
- **Impact:** Correctness-affecting: avoids committing an intermediate empty file. The 25ms window is a heuristic; if a save legitimately takes longer than 25ms between chunks it could still commit partial data.
- **Blast radius:** Coupled to writeTarget kind/fsProjection detection and scheduleFlush. Moderate.
- **Insight:** Another magic-number heuristic guarding against O_TRUNC-then-write races. Workaround for lack of transactional save semantics from editors; fragile but defensible.

#### `packages/fuse/mod.ts:988-990` — setTimeout(fn, 0) deferral  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `lookup callback (Deno.UnsafeCallback in main/mount setup)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(fn, 0) deferral
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimeout(() => { bridge.prepareLookup(parent, name).catch(() => {}); }, 0);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Fast-path FUSE lookup: reply to the kernel immediately from the already-hydrated tree, then kick off background hydration for not-yet-populated entries so the reply is not blocked on async bridge work.
- **Design:** A zero-delay setTimeout defers the prepareLookup() call to a later macrotask so the synchronous FUSE reply happens first; errors are swallowed. No cancellation, no coalescing.
- **Impact:** Decouples reply latency from hydration; hydration becomes eventually-consistent rather than synchronous. Ordering between the immediate reply and the background hydration is nondeterministic, which could surface stale entries briefly.
- **Blast radius:** Refactoring to an event/queue-driven hydration would touch bridge.prepareLookup semantics, the tree hydration state machine, and any tests asserting lookup-then-read consistency. Moderate difficulty.
- **Insight:** Classic 'reply now, work later' deferral. The 0ms timer is a macrotask yield rather than a real delay; queueMicrotask would order it earlier. Defensible but a smell — a proper background work queue would be clearer.

#### `packages/fuse/tree-builder.ts:306-308, 709-710` — setTimeout(0) cooperative yield every N items  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `yieldToEventLoop / buildJsonTreeAsyncImpl`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(0) cooperative yield every N items
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise<void>((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0)); ... if (processed % ASYNC_BUILD_BATCH_SIZE === 0) { await yieldToEventLoop(); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** When building a large filesystem subtree from a JSON value, yield to the event loop every 200 processed nodes so the async build does not starve the FUSE daemon's event loop and other pending requests.
- **Design:** yieldToEventLoop awaits a setTimeout(_, 0) macrotask; called once per ASYNC_BUILD_BATCH_SIZE (200) items in the build loop. Batch size is a fixed constant.
- **Impact:** Keeps the daemon responsive during large tree builds at the cost of throughput. The 200-item batch and setTimeout(0) granularity affect latency vs responsiveness tradeoff; not correctness.
- **Blast radius:** buildJsonTreeAsyncImpl and any callers relying on build completion timing. Small; batch size tunable in one place.
- **Insight:** Cooperative-yield pattern. setTimeout(0) yields a full macrotask, which is heavier than needed (queueMicrotask would not yield to I/O); here a macrotask yield is intentional to let FUSE requests run. Defensible.

### `packages/generated-patterns`

#### `packages/generated-patterns/integration/pattern-harness.ts:162-163` — fixed sleep(100) to let debounce timers settle  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `runPatternScenario (cleanup)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed sleep(100) to let debounce timers settle
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  // Small delay to allow any pending debounce timers to either fire or be cancelled
  await sleep(100);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Before disposing the runtime, wait 100ms so any pending debounce timers in the pattern under test either fire or get cancelled, avoiding post-dispose errors.
- **Design:** Unconditional await sleep(100) after runtime.idle() and before dispose. Fixed magic number, not tied to any signal.
- **Impact:** Adds a flat 100ms to every scenario's teardown and is a guess — a slower debounce than 100ms would still leak. Contributes to test wall-clock time and potential flakiness.
- **Blast radius:** Every pattern scenario using this harness pays the 100ms. Replacing with a real drain of pending timers would need runtime support. Small but pervasive.
- **Insight:** A sleep-to-settle smell: it papers over the lack of a way to drain/flush pending debounce timers deterministically. Info (test-only) but a clear candidate for a signal-driven replacement.

#### `packages/generated-patterns/integration/patterns/ct-1334-fetchjson-derive-subpattern.test.ts:29` — setTimeout artificial async delay in fetch mock  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `mocked fetch closure (test setup)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout artificial async delay in fetch mock
- **Snippet:**

  ```

  ```
- **Purpose:** In the mocked fetch for the test endpoint, introduce a 10ms delay before returning the response to simulate real network latency so the fetchjson-derive-subpattern reactivity is exercised asynchronously.
- **Design:** A 10ms setTimeout-backed promise inside the fetch mock, gating the mocked Response. Fixed delay.
- **Impact:** Ensures the pattern's async/derive path runs with a real microtask/macrotask gap rather than resolving synchronously; minor added test latency.
- **Blast radius:** Only this test's mock. Trivial.
- **Insight:** Deliberate latency injection to make the test representative of async fetch. Info-level, test-only, harmless.

### `packages/html`

#### `packages/html/test/main-renderer.test.ts:303, 317, 338, 343` — await setTimeout(resolve, 0) macrotask settle  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level Deno.test bodies (render lifecycle / mount-failure tests)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / await setTimeout(resolve, 0) macrotask settle
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Yields a macrotask so the async mount pipeline (render() wiring its cancel closure, registering the active render, or the deferred dispose) completes before the test asserts on getActiveRenders() / connection.unmountCalls / captured onError errors.
- **Design:** Four occurrences, each a zero-delay setTimeout wrapped in a Promise and awaited. Used to let promise-chained mount/dispose work flush; a macrotask (setTimeout 0) rather than a microtask is chosen so any queued microtasks in the mount chain also drain first. No cancellation or bound; each is a single one-shot yield.
- **Impact:** Test-only synchronization. Because it waits a fixed scheduler turn rather than an explicit completion signal, it is a potential flakiness source if the mount pipeline ever needs more than one macrotask turn to settle. Adds negligible wall time.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would replace the timer yields with an awaitable completion signal exposed by render()/dispose() (e.g. a returned promise). Touches only this test file's assertions and any shared MockConnection timing assumptions. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Common test smell: sleep(0) as a stand-in for 'await the thing that actually finished'. Replaceable by awaiting the mount/dispose promise directly. Low risk today because the pipelines settle in one turn, but fragile by construction.

#### `packages/html/test/worker-reconciler-cell-child.test.ts:105-1474` — fixed-duration setTimeout sleep (await new Promise + setTimeout 10ms)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `Deno.test("worker reconciler - cell child optimization") t.step callbacks (multiple steps)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed-duration setTimeout sleep (await new Promise + setTimeout 10ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 10));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Lets the asynchronous WorkerReconciler process a mount() or Cell.set() and flush its emitted VDom ops into the collector before the test asserts on collector.getOps(). Bridges the gap between an async subscription-driven render and a synchronous assertion.
- **Design:** A hardcoded 10ms delay wrapped in a Promise, awaited after every mount/set/update. Appears 53 times across the file, always with the identical 10ms constant. No cancellation, no polling, no adaptive backoff — a flat guess at how long the reconciler needs.
- **Impact:** Pure test-only timing. Adds ~10ms of wall-clock latency per await (roughly half a second cumulative across the file's ~53 sleeps). Classic flakiness vector: if the reconciler ever takes longer than 10ms on a slow or loaded CI machine, ops will not have flushed and assertions fail nondeterministically; the fixed delay makes the test neither fully deterministic nor minimally fast.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch every t.step in the file. The clean replacement is awaiting a completion signal (e.g. runtime.idle(), which the sibling file worker-reconciler-cell-props.test.ts already uses in its final step) or an explicit reconciler-flushed promise. MockCell.sink calls back synchronously, so the async gap is really the reconciler's internal scheduling; a deterministic hook would remove all 53 sleeps. Difficulty: mechanical but voluminous.
- **Insight:** A smell, not load-bearing logic: the 10ms is a magic number chosen empirically. The same test suite's props file demonstrates the better pattern (runtime.idle()) in one place, so this file is inconsistent with its sibling and ripe for conversion to a signal-driven wait.

#### `packages/html/test/worker-reconciler-cell-props.test.ts:231-1471` — fixed-duration setTimeout sleep (await new Promise + setTimeout 10ms)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `Deno.test("worker reconciler - Cell<Props>") t.step callbacks (multiple steps)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed-duration setTimeout sleep (await new Promise + setTimeout 10ms)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 10));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Same idiom as the cell-child file: waits for the async WorkerReconciler to render props / apply prop updates and emit VDom ops before the test inspects the ops collector or asserts on set-prop / set-event / remove-prop ops.
- **Design:** Hardcoded 10ms Promise+setTimeout delay, awaited after mount and after each Cell.set / tx.commit. Occurs 39 times, always 10ms. Notably the file ALSO uses await runtime.idle() (lines 1562, 1574) in its final static-event-prop step, so two different quiescence mechanisms coexist in one file.
- **Impact:** Test-only latency and a nondeterminism risk identical to the sibling file: a slow machine that needs more than 10ms to flush reconciler ops produces spurious failures. The coexistence of runtime.idle() and the 10ms sleep shows the sleeps are avoidable — idle() is the deterministic completion-signal path.
- **Blast radius:** Converting the 39 sleeps to runtime.idle() (already imported and used here) would touch every step but is low-risk since the correct primitive is proven within the same file. Watch for steps that commit a transaction (tx.commit at 1000/1080/1148) where op flush ordering matters — idle() must encompass both commit propagation and reconciler flush.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing only as a crutch; genuinely replaceable. This file is self-documenting evidence that the sleeps are unnecessary because runtime.idle() already does the job deterministically two lines away. Fragile magic-number timing that should be swept in favor of the signal-driven idle await.

#### `packages/html/test/worker-reconciler-cfc-render-policy.test.ts:371,405,445,484,522,585,632,651,696,703,736,769,805,859,914,953,994,1014,1050,1067,1107,1115,1158,1162,1197,1231,1271,1306,1365,1399,1434,1493,1564,1640,1645,1682,1693,1762,1775,1817,1872,1929,2040,2160,2270,2327,2335,2356,2393,2412,2445,2463,2502,2517,2559,2583,2627,2708,2735,2761,2776,2806,2832,2854,2874,2938,2965,2995` — fixed 10ms macrotask settle sleep  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level test bodies (worker reconciler render-policy suite)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed 10ms macrotask settle sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 10));
  ```
- **Purpose:** After mutating a cell / flushing initial props, the test waits a fixed 10ms to let the asynchronous worker reconciler process the change and repaint the DOM before asserting on rendered output.
- **Design:** About 67 identical inline `new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 10))` calls scattered through every test case, hardcoding a 10ms wall-clock delay with no condition check, no polling and no cancellation. A couple appear paired (e.g. 1158/1162, 1640/1645) to bracket a flushInitial() call.
- **Impact:** Directly drives test flakiness and total suite runtime: 10ms is a guess at how long the reconciler takes, so on a slow/loaded CI machine the render may not have completed (false failure) and on a fast machine every case still pays the full 10ms. Ordering/determinism depend on the timer firing after the reconciler's own microtasks.
- **Blast radius:** Refactoring to a signal/settle-based wait (poll on rendered DOM state, or await a reconciler quiescence promise) would touch all ~67 sites plus any shared helper introduced; observable behavior (pass/fail timing) unchanged if the wait becomes condition-driven, but each case would need a concrete completion predicate. Low difficulty mechanically, tedious due to count.
- **Insight:** Classic test smell: a magic 10ms sleep standing in for 'reconciler is done'. Replaceable by an explicit settle/quiescence await (the codebase already has settle helpers elsewhere). Load-bearing only in the sense that removing it without a replacement predicate breaks the assertions.

#### `packages/html/test/worker-reconciler-space-stamp.test.ts:91,114` — setTimeout(resolve, 0) op-flush yield  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level test bodies (space-stamp suite)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(resolve, 0) op-flush yield
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0)); // op flush
  ```
- **Purpose:** Yield the event loop one macrotask so queued reconciler/storage operations flush before the test asserts, per the inline `// op flush` comment.
- **Design:** Two `setTimeout(resolve, 0)` awaits used as a zero-delay macrotask boundary; no condition, no cancellation. Relies on all pending work completing within a single event-loop turn.
- **Impact:** A single 0ms turn may not be enough if the pending work spans multiple microtask/macrotask rounds, risking intermittent under-settling; otherwise negligible latency. Determinism hinges on the flush completing in one turn.
- **Blast radius:** Replacing with an explicit flush/settle await would touch both sites and require a concrete quiescence signal from the reconciler pipeline. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Milder than the render-policy suite (0ms yield rather than a 10ms guess) but the same pattern of yielding to let async work drain rather than awaiting a completion signal.

### `packages/iframe-sandbox`

#### `packages/iframe-sandbox/src/common-iframe-sandbox.ts:398-411` — randomized jitter sleep before scheduling next check  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CommonIframeSandboxElement.requestHealthCheck`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / randomized jitter sleep before scheduling next check
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const jitter = 100 + (Math.random() * 400);
  await sleep(jitter);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Insert a randomized 100-500ms idle gap before the next health-check ping, spreading checks out to 'avoid janky cycles' when many frames run at once.
- **Design:** performance.now() is sampled before and after the sleep; if the observed elapsed exceeds jitter + HEALTH_CHECK_TIMEOUT it treats the main thread as having been blocked (Firefox srcdoc case) and fails the check. Timer-driven idle, not signal-driven.
- **Impact:** Purely a spacing delay; the post-sleep elapsed comparison doubles as a coarse main-thread-block detector. Only active when health checking is enabled (currently off).
- **Blast radius:** Bounded to requestHealthCheck; tied to the recursive poll above. Low difficulty on its own.
- **Insight:** The clever bit is reusing the stopwatch delta across the sleep to infer that the main thread was frozen. Defensible but heuristic; part of the same disabled subsystem.

#### `packages/iframe-sandbox/test/iframe.test.ts:107` — fixed 100ms settle sleep  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level test body (subscription/unsubscribe test)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed 100ms settle sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await sleep(100);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Wait a fixed 100ms after setting values on an unsubscribed key to give any (erroneous) update a chance to arrive, then assert that no further updates were delivered — proving unsubscribe took effect.
- **Design:** Single unconditional sleep(100) with no predicate; used specifically to test a negative (that nothing happens), where a poll cannot short-circuit because the expected outcome is the absence of change.
- **Impact:** 100ms is a guess at 'long enough for a stray update to have arrived'; too short risks a false pass (update in flight not yet delivered). Adds fixed latency to the suite.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test; hard to replace with a signal because it asserts non-occurrence. Trivial.
- **Insight:** One of the defensible fixed sleeps: proving nothing fires genuinely needs a bounded wait rather than a completion signal. Still a magic constant.

### `packages/js-compiler`

#### `packages/js-compiler/typescript/compiler.ts:355-365` — for(;;) generator drain with await yieldToEventLoop() between steps  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `TypeScriptCompiler.compileToModulesInterleaved`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / for(;;) generator drain with await yieldToEventLoop() between steps
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const steps = this.compileToModulesSteps(program, inputOptions); for (;;) { const next = steps.next(); if (next.done) return next.value; await yieldToEventLoop(); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Runs the CPU-bound TypeScript compile pipeline while yielding a macrotask turn at each module boundary, so in the browser runtime worker queued IPC (cell traffic) is not starved for the whole multi-hundred-ms-to-seconds compile.
- **Design:** Drives the compileToModulesSteps generator; after each yielded step it awaits yieldToEventLoop() (a MessageChannel-based macrotask hop that periodically also takes a setTimeout(0) turn to avoid starving timers). The synchronous sibling compileToModules uses the same for(;;) drain with no yield.
- **Impact:** Deliberately trades total compile latency for event-loop fairness: bounds the worker stall to the longest single step rather than the whole pipeline. Affects responsiveness/ordering of IPC delivery during compilation; not a correctness gate (same outputs as the sync driver).
- **Blast radius:** Callers that pick interleaved vs sync compilation (browser runtime worker vs CLI). Coupled to yieldToEventLoop semantics in packages/utils/src/sleep.ts. Changing the yield granularity affects worker responsiveness. Medium difficulty, real behavioral surface.
- **Insight:** A thoughtful cooperative-yielding design, not a naive sleep: it idles the compile between steps to let the event loop breathe. Load-bearing for worker responsiveness. The for(;;) is just a generator drain, not a busy-wait.

### `packages/lib-shell`

#### `packages/lib-shell/test/runtime.test.ts:227, 263, 266` — await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 0)) to let fire-and-forget handler settle  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `RuntimeInternals navigate handler tests`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 0)) to let fire-and-forget handler settle
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Yields one macrotask turn so a fire-and-forget navigate handler (dispatched via client.emit) runs and settles before the test asserts on its side effects / unhandled rejections.
- **Design:** setTimeout(resolve, 0)-backed promise awaited between emitting a synthetic event and asserting. At line 263 it parks the handler on a synced() gate; 265 rejects the gate; 266 yields again to let the abort propagate. Pure test sequencing.
- **Impact:** Test-only ordering; introduces macrotask ordering assumptions rather than awaiting an explicit completion signal, a mild flakiness vector if the handler needs more than one turn. No production effect.
- **Blast radius:** Confined to these three test cases. Would only change if the handler exposed an awaitable completion. Trivial.
- **Insight:** Standard test-time settle sleep; borderline fragile (relies on exactly one macrotask being enough) but low stakes. Info-level.

### `packages/llm`

#### `packages/llm/src/client.ts:459-461` — setTimeout(resolve, 0) zero-delay yield  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `LLMClient.generateObject`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(resolve, 0) zero-delay yield
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  return mockResponse;
  ```
- **Purpose:** In mock mode, before returning a canned generateObject response, it yields the event loop once so the mock path is asynchronous like the real fetch path, avoiding synchronous resolution that could mask ordering bugs in callers.
- **Design:** A Promise wrapping setTimeout with a 0ms delay; no cancellation, no abortSignal wiring, unconditional single yield. Only reached when mockCatalog.isEnabled() and a matching mock is found.
- **Impact:** Test-only code path. Introduces one macrotask tick of latency to make mock responses non-synchronous. Slightly affects task ordering in tests but is deterministic; does not gate or abandon work.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor (e.g. to queueMicrotask or direct return) would touch only mock-mode consumers; a few LLM mock tests could observe changed microtask-vs-macrotask ordering. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** A defensible test-affordance to keep the mock's async shape faithful to production. Minor smell that it uses a macrotask (setTimeout 0) rather than a microtask, but harmless.

#### `packages/llm/src/client.ts:552-554` — setTimeout(resolve, 0) zero-delay yield  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `LLMClient.sendRequest`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(resolve, 0) zero-delay yield
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  return mockResponse;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Same mock-mode async simulation as generateObject but on the streaming/sendRequest path: yields once before returning the mock so streaming callers see asynchronous behavior even without a real network stream.
- **Design:** Promise-wrapped setTimeout with 0ms delay, unconditional, no cancellation. Runs after the optional one-shot callback fires with the full mock text. Only reached in mock mode with a matching response.
- **Impact:** Test-only latency of one macrotask tick. Deterministic; does not bound or abandon work. Note the mock streaming callback delivers the whole text at once rather than incremental chunks, so timing-sensitive chunk tests are unaffected by this sleep specifically.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor touches only mock-mode streaming consumers and any test asserting on tick ordering. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Parallel to the generateObject case; load-bearing only insofar as it keeps mock and real paths async-shaped. Defensible.

### `packages/memory`

#### `packages/memory/test/v2-client-test.ts:2329` — setTimeout(resolve, 0) macrotask yield after waitFor  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `Deno.test 'memory v2 client does not restore a closed session after reconnect'`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(resolve, 0) macrotask yield after waitFor
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await waitFor(() => transport.connectionCount >= 2); await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  ```
- **Purpose:** After the reconnect fires (connectionCount>=2), yields one macrotask to let any (unwanted) restore attempt settle so the subsequent assertion that a closed session is NOT restored is meaningful.
- **Design:** Real-time setTimeout(0) awaited to drain a turn of the event loop following the poll-based waitFor.
- **Impact:** Real-timer yield; combined with the real-time waitFor above it introduces nondeterministic ordering. Reliable in practice but not signal-driven.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test. Low.
