- **Purpose:** Yields to the macrotask queue so a rejected live-update promise inside setupWorkerConsoleToggle has time to settle and run its catch handler before the test asserts the error was logged.
- **Design:** Awaits a Promise resolved by a zero-delay setTimeout, draining pending microtasks/macrotasks; no cancellation, no real delay value beyond scheduler ordering.
- **Impact:** Test-only. Relies on macrotask ordering rather than a completion signal from the code under test, so it is a mild flakiness risk if the rejection chain needs more than one macrotask turn.
- **Blast radius:** Isolated to this one test case; replacing it with an awaitable hook exposed by the toggle helper would remove the timer dependency but requires the production code to surface its live-update promise.
- **Insight:** Standard test yield to flush a fire-and-forget rejection path; defensible but a signal-based await would be more deterministic.

### `packages/state-inspector`

#### `packages/state-inspector/html.ts:257-258` — setTimeout auto-dismiss  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `flash`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout auto-dismiss
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  f.classList.add("on");
  setTimeout(()=>f.classList.remove("on"),900);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Shows a transient 'flash' toast message (e.g. 'copied id') in the state-inspector debug UI and auto-clears it after a fixed delay so the notice fades on its own.
- **Design:** A single one-shot setTimeout of 900ms removes the 'on' CSS class after the fixed delay. No handle is captured, so overlapping flashes are not debounced or cancelled; a later flash re-adds the class and its own timer fires independently.
- **Impact:** Purely cosmetic in a dev/debug page. Overlapping calls can cause the toast to disappear early because the first timer removes the class while a second message is showing. No correctness or determinism impact on the runtime.
- **Blast radius:** Trivial and self-contained to this one helper; a refactor to a cancellable timer or CSS animation touches only flash() and its callers within this HTML file.
- **Insight:** Standard UI toast auto-dismiss; low-value smell (uncancelled timer) but defensible for a debug tool. Not load-bearing.

### `packages/toolshed`

#### `packages/toolshed/routes/health/health.handlers.ts:343` — setTimeout deferred revert  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `copyText (client-side inline <script>)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout deferred revert
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimeout(() => { el.classList.remove("copied"); el.textContent = orig; }, 1200);
  ```
- **Purpose:** After copying text to the clipboard, briefly shows a 'copied!' confirmation then reverts the element's text and style after 1.2 seconds.
- **Design:** One-shot setTimeout of 1200 ms fired inside the clipboard writeText().then() callback; no handle stored, so rapid repeated copies can stack timers and revert prematurely.
- **Impact:** Purely cosmetic UI feedback timing in the browser; no server behavior, correctness, or test impact.
- **Blast radius:** Isolated to the dashboard's copy-to-clipboard helper. Trivial to change.
- **Insight:** Ubiquitous UI 'flash a confirmation then revert' pattern. Defensible; the only nit is the unstored timer handle allowing overlap on rapid clicks.

#### `packages/toolshed/routes/integrations/oauth2-common/oauth2-common.utils.ts:102` — setTimeout deferred window.close  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `generateCallbackHtml (embedded callback-page <script>)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout deferred window.close
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  window.opener.postMessage({ ... }, window.location.origin);
  setTimeout(() => window.close(), 2000);
  ```
- **Purpose:** After the OAuth callback page posts its result back to the opener window, it auto-closes the popup 2 seconds later so the user is not left with a dangling window.
- **Design:** One-shot setTimeout of 2000 ms in the generated callback HTML, fired only when window.opener exists, immediately after postMessage. No cancellation; fixed delay chosen to let the opener process the message.
- **Impact:** Purely cosmetic client-side UX timing in the OAuth popup; no server behavior or determinism impact. The delay is a soft assumption that the opener has consumed the message by then.
- **Blast radius:** Isolated to the generated callback HTML string; equivalent popups exist per provider through createCallbackResponse. Trivial to change.
- **Insight:** Common 'give the opener a moment, then close' popup idiom. Defensible; the 2s delay is a guess rather than a signal that the opener finished, but the consequence of a bad guess is only a window that closes slightly early or late.

#### `packages/toolshed/routes/storage/memory/memory.test.ts:1091, 1243` — fixed setTimeout sleep after server restart  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `reconnect / alias-reconnect test cases`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed setTimeout sleep after server restart
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await server.shutdown();
  server = Deno.serve({ port }, app.fetch);
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 100));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Gives the freshly restarted Deno.serve listener a fixed 100ms to bind the port before the test creates a new runtime and attempts to reconnect the websocket, simulating a server bounce.
- **Design:** Unconditional 100ms sleep between server shutdown/restart and the reconnect attempt. Not gated on any readiness signal from the new server; just a hardcoded delay.
- **Impact:** Test-only timing dependency. If the OS is slow to rebind the port the reconnect may race; if fast, the 100ms is wasted latency. A determinism/flakiness risk typical of fixed sleeps around process/port lifecycle.
- **Blast radius:** Two reconnect test cases. Could be replaced by polling the new server for readiness (a fetch/connect retry) but that reintroduces a poll loop. Low difficulty, low stakes.
- **Insight:** Textbook fixed-sleep-for-readiness smell in tests. Fine for a test, but the value is magic and could flake on a loaded CI machine. Info severity.

### `packages/ui`

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-autocomplete/cf-autocomplete.ts:883-902` — setTimeout(0) debounce / defer-to-next-task  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFAutocomplete._handleInput`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(0) debounce / defer-to-next-task
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (this._debounceTimer !== null) { clearTimeout(this._debounceTimer); } this._debounceTimer = setTimeout(() => { ... this._query = newValue; ... }, 0);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Defers the internal Lit state update (_query/_isOpen/_highlightedIndex) out of the input event handler to the next macrotask so the browser paints the keystroke before running the dropdown filter, reducing perceived typing lag. clearTimeout cancels a stale pending update so fast typing does not flash intermediate dropdowns.
- **Design:** A single setTimeout(0) timer stored in _debounceTimer; each keystroke clears the previous timer and schedules a new one. Cleared in disconnectedCallback. There is also a separate 50ms debounce in the value cell controller (line 378) for cell writes.
- **Impact:** Introduces a one-task delay between keypress and dropdown filter update; makes deterministic testing of the dropdown require awaiting a macrotask. CPU-cheap. The extensive comment (lines 866-881) documents a verified perf investigation, so this is intentional.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch _handleInput, the _debounceTimer field, disconnectedCallback cleanup, and any test that types and immediately asserts dropdown contents. Low difficulty but the perf comment warns against reverting to synchronous updates.
- **Insight:** Defensible, well-documented UI micro-optimization. Timer value of 0 means it is a task-deferral, not a real debounce interval; the naming ('_debounceTimer') is slightly misleading. Not correctness-critical.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-autocomplete/cf-autocomplete.ts:910-912, 1097-1104` — requestAnimationFrame defer-until-DOM-updated  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFAutocomplete._handleFocus / _scrollHighlightedIntoView`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / requestAnimationFrame defer-until-DOM-updated
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  requestAnimationFrame(() => { this._input?.select(); }); ... requestAnimationFrame(() => { const option = ...; option.scrollIntoView({ block: 'nearest' }); });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits one animation frame so the pending Lit render/DOM update has flushed before selecting input text or scrolling the highlighted option into view (querying the just-rendered element).
- **Design:** One-shot requestAnimationFrame with no stored handle or cancellation; relies on the frame firing after Lit's async render. Uses the rAF as a proxy for 'DOM is now updated'.
- **Impact:** Correct behavior depends on the rAF landing after the render commit rather than on an explicit updateComplete signal; usually reliable but timing-coupled. UI-only, no test-flakiness beyond focus/scroll assertions.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor could use Lit's `await this.updateComplete` instead of rAF, touching _handleFocus and _scrollHighlightedIntoView. Trivial, self-contained.
- **Insight:** Minor smell: rAF used as a stand-in for 'after render' where Lit's updateComplete promise is the signal-driven alternative. Low stakes.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-cfc-authorship/cf-cfc-authorship.test.ts:159, 410` — test setTimeout sleep to await polling  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `CFCFCAuthorship test: retries the resolved cell label / represented-principal profile`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / test setTimeout sleep to await polling
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 150)); ... await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Test-only waits: the 150ms sleep gives the component's 100ms label-retry poll time to fire and flip authorshipState to 'verified' once labelAvailable is set; the 0ms sleep flushes a microtask/subscription notification before asserting.
- **Design:** Raw setTimeout-wrapped promises awaited inline in Deno.test cases. The 150ms value is chosen to exceed one LABEL_RETRY_INTERVAL_MS (100ms) tick.
- **Impact:** Introduces wall-clock dependence into the test suite; the 150ms sleep is a source of potential flakiness under load (if the poll tick slips past 150ms) and adds fixed latency to the run. Directly couples the test to the production polling cadence.
- **Blast radius:** If the production retry interval or mechanism changes, this sleep must change too. Replacing the production poll with a subscription would let the test await a signal instead of sleeping. Confined to this test file.
- **Insight:** Test smell that mirrors the production polling workaround: because the component polls rather than signals, the test can only observe the result by sleeping past a poll tick. Info/low severity as test-only, but flags the same underlying design gap.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-code-editor/cf-code-editor.ts:1409-1417` — setTimeout(...,0) re-trigger of autocompletion  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFCodeEditor (updateListener extension)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(...,0) re-trigger of autocompletion
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (status === null) { setTimeout(() => startCompletion(update.view), 0); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Re-opens the CodeMirror autocompletion popup when the cursor is inside a '[[' backlink context but completion has closed, keeping the completion menu sticky during backlink entry.
- **Design:** On each docChanged update, if a backlink query is active and completionStatus is null, a zero-delay setTimeout calls startCompletion on the next macrotask (deferred so it runs after CodeMirror finishes applying the current update). No dedup/cancel; can fire repeatedly across updates.
- **Impact:** Ensures the completion UI stays open; deferral avoids re-entrant dispatch during an in-progress update. Could theoretically re-queue on every keystroke inside a backlink, but startCompletion is idempotent when already open.
- **Blast radius:** Local to the completion-keeping extension; changing the timing could reintroduce the closed-popup glitch. UI-only, no direct test coverage.
- **Insight:** A workaround for CodeMirror's completion lifecycle where you cannot call startCompletion synchronously inside an update listener; defensible but a smell (re-scheduling work from within an update).

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-code-editor/cf-code-editor.ts:506` — setTimeout(...,0) deferral  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFCodeEditor.createBacklinkClickHandler (mousedown handler)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(...,0) deferral
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimeout(() => this.handlePillClick(view, event), 0);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Defers backlink-pill navigation out of the CodeMirror mousedown handler so the current DOM/selection event finishes before the async navigation runs.
- **Design:** Zero-delay setTimeout schedules handlePillClick on the next macrotask; no cancellation, fires once per pill mousedown. preventDefault is called first, then the deferred handler navigates.
- **Impact:** Decouples navigation from the event dispatch; a task-boundary reorder rather than a real delay. Minor ordering dependency; not hot-path.
- **Blast radius:** Local to the click handler; replacing with queueMicrotask or a direct call could change ordering relative to CodeMirror's own event handling. No tests target this directly.
- **Insight:** Classic 'yield to the event loop before doing async work' setTimeout(0); defensible but replaceable by queueMicrotask if microtask timing suffices.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-copy-button/cf-copy-button.ts:146-152, 164-169` — setTimeout auto-reset timer  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFCopyButton._handleClick / disconnectedCallback`

- **Purpose:** Shows a transient 'Copied!' confirmation state after a successful clipboard write, then reverts to the default label after feedbackDuration (default 2000ms).
- **Design:** On copy success sets _copied=true, clears any prior _resetTimeout, then schedules a setTimeout(feedbackDuration) to flip _copied back and request a re-render. The timer is cleared on re-click and on disconnectedCallback to avoid leaks/late updates.
- **Impact:** Pure UI feedback timing; affects only the visible label. Proper cancellation on disconnect prevents updates on a detached element. No correctness or determinism impact beyond the visual state.
- **Blast radius:** Fully local to the component; feedbackDuration is a public property. Trivial to refactor. No timing-sensitive tests noted.
- **Insight:** Textbook transient-UI-state timer with correct cleanup; defensible, not a smell.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-fab/cf-fab.ts:543-568, 490-508` — setTimeout animation-completion timer  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFFab.updated (expanded-change branch) / disconnectedCallback`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout animation-completion timer
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  this.collapseTimeout = setTimeout(() => { this.collapsing = false; this.toggleAttribute("collapsing", false); this.collapseTimeout = null; }, 400) as unknown as number;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Keeps a 'collapsing' attribute/class on the FAB for the duration of the collapse animation (400ms), then clears it so the animated state matches the CSS transition timing.
- **Design:** When expanded flips true->false, sets collapsing=true and schedules a 400ms setTimeout to clear it. Expanding again clears the timeout immediately. Cleared on disconnectedCallback. The 400ms is a magic number that must track the CSS animation duration (which is variable via --cf-theme-animation-duration).
- **Impact:** UI-only; the hardcoded 400ms can desync from the CSS animation-duration variable, leaving the collapsing class removed early or late if the theme changes the duration. Not correctness-critical.
- **Blast radius:** Local to the FAB expand/collapse state machine. Refactor to an 'animationend'/'transitionend' listener would remove the magic-number coupling. No direct test coverage in-chunk.
- **Insight:** A smell: a JS timer hardcoded to 400ms mirroring a CSS transition whose duration is themeable via a CSS variable — a transitionend event would be more robust than this fixed sleep.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-fab/cf-fab.ts:605-618` — setTimeout auto-hide timer  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Design:** Sets showPreview=true, clears any existing _previewTimeout, then schedules a 5000ms setTimeout to hide it. Cleared on disconnectedCallback. Triggered by a Cell subscription callback when a new preview message arrives while collapsed.
- **Impact:** UI-only auto-dismiss; proper cleanup on disconnect. No correctness impact.
- **Blast radius:** Local; the 5s duration is a hardcoded constant. Trivial to change. No timing-sensitive tests in-chunk.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-file-download/cf-file-download.ts:580-590` — debounce timer (setTimeout, cancel-and-reset)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFFileDownload._scheduleAutosave`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / debounce timer (setTimeout, cancel-and-reset)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (this._autosaveTimer) clearTimeout(this._autosaveTimer);
  this._autosaveTimer = setTimeout(() => this._performAutosave(), CFFileDownload.AUTOSAVE_INTERVAL);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Debounces autosave-to-folder so edits are batched and written at most once per interval rather than on every keystroke, reducing filesystem churn.
- **Design:** Single setTimeout with a fixed 60_000ms (AUTOSAVE_INTERVAL) delay; any earlier pending timer is cleared before arming a fresh one. Cleared in disconnectedCallback and _disableAutosave.
- **Impact:** Autosave latency is bounded by the 60s interval; a tab close within the window relies on the visibilitychange/beforeunload fast-path instead. Timer-driven, not signal-driven, so exact save timing is non-deterministic.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch _performAutosave race handling (line 548 reschedule), the visibilitychange/beforeunload handlers, and disconnect cleanup. Low difficulty; purely local UI concern.
- **Insight:** Defensible debounce for a UI autosave. Load-bearing for battery/IO reduction but replaceable by an idle/settle signal if one existed.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-file-download/cf-file-download.ts:686-689` — fixed setTimeout before URL.revokeObjectURL  &nbsp; 🟡 low

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-input-otp/cf-input-otp.ts:72` — setTimeout(0) deferred autofocus  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Purpose:** Defers focusing the first OTP input until after the current task/render so the shadow DOM inputs exist and focus lands correctly.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-input/cf-input.ts:602-611, 638-642` — debounce/throttle strategy config delegated to cell controller  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFInput.updated / CFInput.firstUpdated`

- **Snippet:**

  ```
  this._cellController.updateTimingOptions({ strategy: this.timingStrategy, delay: this.timingDelay });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Exposes an input timing strategy ('immediate' | 'debounce' | 'throttle' | 'blur') with a delay so value commits to the underlying cell are debounced/throttled rather than firing on every keystroke.
- **Design:** The component itself holds no timer; it forwards strategy/delay (default 'debounce'/300ms per lines 429-430,480) into _cellController.updateTimingOptions, which owns the actual debounce/throttle machinery. Config is pushed on firstUpdated and whenever timingStrategy/timingDelay change.
- **Impact:** Determines when input edits reach the reactive cell; a debounce means downstream reactivity and any test observing cell writes must account for the 300ms delay. The real timing mechanism lives in the cell controller (out of this chunk).
- **Blast radius:** Callers set timingStrategy/timingDelay attributes; the actual debounce lives in the cell controller so refactoring the mechanism touches that controller and every cf-input/cf-textarea consumer plus their tests.
- **Insight:** Sound separation: the component is just a config surface. Load-bearing for typing UX; the interesting timing code is the controller it delegates to.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-map/cf-map.ts:485-494` — debounce (setTimeout)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFMap._initializeMap (ResizeObserver callback)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / debounce (setTimeout)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  this._resizeTimeoutId = setTimeout(() => { this._map?.invalidateSize(); ... }, RESIZE_DEBOUNCE_MS);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Debounces ResizeObserver container-size callbacks so Leaflet's invalidateSize() runs only once after resize activity settles, preventing layout thrashing on every pixel change during responsive/tab/accordion resizes.
- **Design:** On each ResizeObserver fire it clearTimeout()s any prior pending timer and schedules a new 150ms (RESIZE_DEBOUNCE_MS) setTimeout; the timer id is stored in _resizeTimeoutId and cleared in _cleanup() on disconnect.
- **Impact:** Adds up to 150ms latency before the map redraws after a resize; reduces CPU/layout cost. Timer-driven, so it can introduce nondeterminism/flake in tests that assert on post-resize map state.
- **Blast radius:** Local to the component; a refactor to an event/rAF-driven invalidate would touch _initializeMap, _cleanup, and any test asserting resize behavior. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Standard, defensible UI debounce. Load-bearing for performance but not correctness; replaceable by requestAnimationFrame coalescing.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-profile-badge/seal-liveness.ts:46-68` — recursive requestAnimationFrame loop  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `tick / startLoop (module-level seal-liveness controller)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / recursive requestAnimationFrame loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const tick = (t) => { rafId = 0; for (const c of clients) c.updateSeal(...); if (clients.size > 0) rafId = requestAnimationFrame(tick); else stopLoop(); };
  ```
- **Purpose:** Drives an always-on reflective sheen on every registered anti-forgery seal that tracks the host cursor even when the cursor is far from the seal (a sandboxed iframe can't see host pointer events elsewhere), making forgeries detectable.
- **Design:** One shared pointermove listener updates module cursorX/cursorY; a single recursive requestAnimationFrame loop calls updateSeal on all registered clients each frame. Loop starts on first registerSeal, self-terminates when clients empty or when prefers-reduced-motion turns on (live matchMedia change listener stops loop and clears seals); cancelAnimationFrame on teardown.
- **Impact:** Runs a per-frame loop continuously while ≥1 seal is mounted — steady CPU/GPU cost proportional to seal count. Cost bounded by culling offscreen seals and skipping far-cursor writes. Reduced-motion honored live.
- **Blast radius:** Shared singleton across all cf-profile-badge seals; refactor touches registerSeal/unregisterSeal callers, the SealLivenessClient contract, and the leak-guard test (seal-liveness.test.ts). Medium difficulty; per-frame perf is the invariant.
- **Insight:** A deliberate, load-bearing security animation (not decorative) with careful leak management (one shared listener+rAF, teardown on last unregister). Well-engineered; the per-frame cost is the price of the anti-forgery property. Not a smell.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-submit-input/cf-submit-input.test.ts:50-51` — setTimeout(0) test tick helper  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level (tick helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(0) test tick helper
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const tick = () => new Promise<void>((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Lets a pending setTimeout(0) callback in the component (the deferred field-clear) run so the test can assert post-defer state.
- **Design:** Returns a promise resolved via setTimeout(resolve, 0); awaited at lines 169-253 between acting and asserting. Mirrors the component's own macrotask defer so the queued clear runs first.
- **Impact:** Test-only synchronization by draining the macrotask queue. Correct as long as the component uses the same macrotask deferral; couples test timing to implementation timing.
- **Blast radius:** Only these tests; if the component switches away from setTimeout(0) the tick helper must change. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Standard test tick to flush a deferred timer. Info-level; benign but a coupling point between test and implementation timing.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-submit-input/cf-submit-input.ts:196-202` — setTimeout(0) deferral after event dispatch  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `CFSubmitInput (submit handler)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(0) deferral after event dispatch
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimeout(() => {
    this._submitting = false;
    if (this.value !== submitted) return;
    this.value = ""; ...
  }, 0);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Clears the input field only after the framework's host-level click listener has read event.target.value during the same dispatch, so the submitted text is captured before the field is wiped.
- **Design:** A zero-delay setTimeout defers the clear past the current click dispatch; the comment notes a microtask defer fired too early in practice, so a macrotask is used deliberately. Guarded to only clear if the field still holds the submitted text (a back-to-back create may have typed the next name), and always resets the in-flight flag.
- **Impact:** Ordering-sensitive: relies on setTimeout(0) landing after the synchronous host click listener. This is an event-ordering workaround, a class of pattern prone to flakiness if dispatch timing shifts. The value-equality guard makes the clear itself race-safe against a fast follow-up submit.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch the interaction with the framework's host-level click listener and the tests that use a tick() helper (setTimeout(0)) to observe the clear. Medium difficulty because the timing is coupled to external dispatch semantics.
- **Insight:** A deliberate macrotask-over-microtask ordering hack, documented as chosen because the microtask was too early. Fragile in principle (depends on host listener running synchronously) but well-guarded and low-stakes (local component state only, no durable write). A completion-signal from the host would be cleaner but none exists.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-tags/cf-tags.ts:255-263` — setTimeout(0) post-render focus deferral  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CfTags.handleTagClick`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(0) post-render focus deferral
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimeout(() => { const input = this.shadowRoot?.querySelector(`#tag-input-${index}`); if (input) { input.focus(); input.select(); } }, 0);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Defers focusing/selecting the newly-shown tag edit input until after Lit has rendered the input element into the shadow DOM, since requestUpdate is async and the element does not exist synchronously.
- **Design:** Zero-delay macrotask timer used as a 'run after next render' hook; no cancellation, no handle stored. The querySelector null-guard tolerates the element being gone (e.g. re-render / disconnect) before the timer fires.
- **Impact:** Focus timing is best-effort; a 0ms timer may fire before Lit's render completes in some engines, occasionally missing focus. No correctness impact beyond a UX nicety. Not deterministic under test.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor to `await this.updateComplete` (Lit's render-settled promise) would make focus deterministic and is a local, low-risk change touching only this method. No external callers rely on the timing.
- **Insight:** Classic setTimeout-0 render-defer smell; the codebase already uses updateComplete elsewhere (cf-textarea, cf-voice-input), so this is replaceable by the proper Lit signal.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-tags/cf-tags.ts:334-341` — setTimeout(0) post-render focus deferral  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CfTags.handleAddTagClick`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(0) post-render focus deferral
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimeout(() => { const input = this.shadowRoot?.querySelector("#new-tag-input"); if (input) { input.focus(); } }, 0);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Defers focusing the freshly-shown 'new tag' input until after Lit renders it, because showingNewInput=true only triggers an async re-render.
- **Design:** Identical zero-delay timer pattern to handleTagClick; fire-and-forget, no stored handle, null-guarded querySelector.
- **Impact:** Same as the other focus deferral: best-effort focus, potential race with render, non-deterministic in tests.
- **Blast radius:** Same local fix (await updateComplete). No external contract.
- **Insight:** Duplicate of the line 255 pattern; both should be replaced by updateComplete-based focus.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-updater/cf-updater.ts:137-141` — setTimeout state auto-reset  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CfUpdater.handleClick`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout state auto-reset
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  setTimeout(() => { this.updateState = "idle"; this.requestUpdate(); }, 3000);
  ```
- **Purpose:** After a register-for-updates request finishes (success or error), resets the button label back to its idle state after 3 seconds so the transient success/error message clears itself.
- **Design:** Fire-and-forget setTimeout with no stored handle and no cancellation; runs unconditionally after the fetch resolves/rejects. If the element is removed within 3s, the callback still fires and mutates a detached element (harmless but sets state on a disconnected component).
- **Impact:** Purely cosmetic auto-reset. Minor: overlapping clicks could schedule multiple resets, and there is no clear-on-disconnect, so it can touch a removed element. Non-deterministic in tests.
- **Blast radius:** Only this component's button-label state. A refactor to store/clear the handle (like cf-toast does) is trivial and local.
- **Insight:** Mild smell: unlike the sibling cf-toast timer, this one is not cancelled on disconnect and has no handle. Low stakes but inconsistent with the codebase's cleaner timer patterns.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/core/cell-controller.test.ts:669-670` — setTimeout(resolve, 0) macrotask yield  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `settleWrites (test helper)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(resolve, 0) macrotask yield
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const settleWrites = () => new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Test-only: yields to the macrotask queue so an in-flight CellHandle.set() round-trip (mock resolves asynchronously) completes before assertions about the settled/released state run.
- **Design:** Promise wrapping setTimeout(_, 0); awaited between a setValue and the assertion. Used repeatedly across the rebind/settle test cases (lines 683, 724, 799, 806, 859, 896, 919).
- **Impact:** Test determinism aid, not production code. Relies on a 0ms macrotask being enough to drain the mock's async resolution; borderline flaky if the mock ever needs more than one macrotask hop.
- **Blast radius:** Confined to this test file; no production callers.
- **Insight:** Standard 'let microtasks/macrotask settle' test idiom. Info-level: not a real timing mechanism, just an async yield.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/core/form-field-controller.test.ts:387` — setTimeout(resolve, 10) simulated async latency  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `form-field flush test (mock cell.set)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout(resolve, 10) simulated async latency
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 10));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Test-only: injects a 10ms artificial delay inside a mock cell.set() to exercise the 'async flush' path and confirm flush() awaits the write.
- **Design:** One-shot setTimeout inside a mock; no cancellation. Only present in the async-flush test case.
- **Impact:** Adds 10ms to that one test's runtime; simulates network latency to verify await semantics. No production effect.
- **Blast radius:** Single test case; no production callers.
- **Insight:** Info-level test scaffolding to simulate a slow write; not a production timing pattern.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/core/input-timing-controller.ts:150-165` — debounce (setTimeout, restarted each keystroke)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `InputTimingController.debounce`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / debounce (setTimeout, restarted each keystroke)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (this.timeoutId !== null) clearTimeout(this.timeoutId);
  this.pendingCallback = callback;
  this.timeoutId = setTimeout(() => { this.timeoutId = null; this.pendingCallback = null; callback(); }, this.options.delay);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Coalesces rapid input events so the bound cell is only written once the user pauses typing for `delay` ms (default 300ms). Avoids writing/dispatching a value-change on every keystroke.
- **Design:** Classic trailing-edge debounce: each schedule() clears the prior setTimeout and starts a new one for `delay` ms; only the last callback survives. pendingCallback is mirrored so flush()/onBlur can run it immediately; cancel() clears both. hostDisconnected() cancels on teardown.
- **Impact:** Introduces a real 300ms wall-clock latency between input and cell write; determines test flakiness because tests must advance a fake clock (time.tick) or await it. Ordering: a debounced write can land after a later remote delivery, which is exactly what cell-controller's settle/release logic compensates for.
- **Blast radius:** This is the shared timing engine for CellController (default {strategy:'debounce', delay:300}) and thus every bound cf input/form field. Refactoring the timer (e.g. to a signal or rAF) would touch CellController.setValue/flush, form-field commit, and the fake-clock assertions in input-timing-controller.test.ts and cell-controller.test.ts.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing and defensible: debounce is the intended UX contract, not a workaround. The 300ms default is duplicated as a literal in cell-controller.ts (lines 157, 564) rather than sourced from one constant, a minor smell. flush()/onBlur give deterministic escape hatches so it is testable.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/core/input-timing-controller.ts:167-194` — leading+trailing throttle (Date.now gate + setTimeout trailing edge)  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `InputTimingController.throttle`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / leading+trailing throttle (Date.now gate + setTimeout trailing edge)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const now = Date.now(); const timeSinceLastCall = now - this.lastCallTime;
  if (timeSinceLastCall >= this.options.delay && this.options.leading) { ... callback(); }
  else if (this.options.trailing) { this.timeoutId = setTimeout(() => { ... callback(); }, remainingTime); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Rate-limits input dispatch to at most one per `delay` window: fires immediately on the leading edge if enough time has elapsed, otherwise schedules a trailing-edge call so the final value is not dropped.
- **Design:** Reads Date.now() to measure elapsed time since lastCallTime and gate the leading edge; when within the window it computes remainingTime = delay - elapsed and schedules a single trailing setTimeout, mirroring the callback into pendingCallback for flush()/onBlur. lastCallTime is re-stamped from Date.now() inside the trailing timer.
- **Impact:** The Date.now() read is control-flow (decides fire-now vs defer), so it feeds gating, not just a timestamp. Adds up to `delay` ms latency to the trailing write; the internal Date.now() means throttle behaviour is not fully controlled by the injected fake clock the way pure setTimeout is, a potential source of test nondeterminism.
- **Blast radius:** Same shared controller as debounce; used when a field opts into {strategy:'throttle'}. A refactor would touch throttle assertions in input-timing-controller.test.ts (leading/trailing edge tests) and any CellController configured for throttle.
- **Insight:** Reasonable throttle implementation; the mix of Date.now() (for the gate) and setTimeout (for trailing) is standard. Minor fragility: lastCallTime uses Date.now() rather than the same clock source the timer is driven by, so under fake timers the elapsed math can diverge from tick() unless the fake also patches Date.now.

- **Design:** Promise around setTimeout(_, 0); single use in this test.
- **Impact:** Test determinism aid only; no production effect.

### `packages/utils`

#### `packages/utils/src/sleep.ts:80-91` — macrotask yield with periodic setTimeout(0) timer hop  &nbsp; 🔴 high

- **Symbol:** `yieldToEventLoop`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / macrotask yield with periodic setTimeout(0) timer hop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await messageYield();
  const now = performance.now();
  if (now - lastTimerTurnAt >= TIMER_TURN_BUDGET_MS) {
    lastTimerTurnAt = now;
    await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 0));
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Yields at least one macrotask so queued timers and worker IPC message events run before the continuation of a long CPU-bound pipeline (e.g. per-module pattern compilation), while periodically paying a real timer hop so armed timers are not starved by a pure message-yield chain.
- **Design:** Falls back to setTimeout(0) when MessageChannel is absent. Uses performance.now() against a module-level lastTimerTurnAt and an 8ms TIMER_TURN_BUDGET_MS fairness budget to decide when to add a setTimeout(0) hop; schedules that timeout from the message callback to keep nesting depth low and avoid the browser nested-setTimeout clamp.
- **Impact:** Central to interleaving long compute against event delivery; the performance.now() reading gates whether a timer hop is taken, directly shaping ordering and fairness. Affects throughput and whether due timers fire during heavy pipelines.
- **Blast radius:** Shared yield primitive for CPU-bound pipelines; the module-level lastTimerTurnAt is shared across all yielders, so any refactor changing the budget or task source affects timer fairness everywhere it is awaited. High difficulty, subtle cross-cutting invariant.
- **Insight:** The most substantive timing mechanism in this chunk: a hand-tuned macrotask-yield fairness scheduler blending posted-message yields with budgeted setTimeout(0) hops and a wall-clock budget. Load-bearing and clever, but fragile — the 8ms budget and depth-1 scheduling trick encode host-specific quirks (Deno message/timer interleave, browser nested-timeout clamp) that are easy to break.

#### `packages/utils/src/sleep.ts:46-55` — MessageChannel macrotask yield  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `messageYield`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / MessageChannel macrotask yield
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const { port1, port2 } = new MessageChannel();
  port1.onmessage = () => { port1.close(); port2.close(); resolve(); };
  port2.postMessage(null);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Yields exactly one macrotask turn through the posted-message task source so already-queued message events (worker IPC) run before the continuation, without the microtask-only behavior of awaiting a resolved promise.
- **Design:** Creates a MessageChannel, posts a null message, resolves in the onmessage handler after closing both ports to avoid resource-sanitizer leaks. One turn per call.
- **Impact:** Controls interleaving/ordering of a CPU-bound pipeline against queued IPC; not timer-driven but idles the continuation until the message task runs. Affects determinism of when queued work is observed.
- **Blast radius:** Internal helper for yieldToEventLoop; changing the task source would alter ordering guarantees that long compile pipelines rely on. Moderate difficulty, subtle.
- **Insight:** A deliberate, well-documented macrotask yield chosen over the microtask a resolved-promise await gives. Load-bearing for interleaving; subtle enough that a naive simplification to await Promise.resolve() would silently break IPC starvation avoidance.

#### `packages/utils/src/sleep.ts:6-7` — setTimeout promise sleep  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `sleep`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout promise sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  export const sleep = (timeout: number) =>
    new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, timeout));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Core promise-based sleep used across the repo to idle for a fixed number of milliseconds before resuming.
- **Design:** Wraps setTimeout(resolve, timeout) in a Promise. No cancellation, no unref; a caller that abandons the promise still holds the timer to completion.
- **Impact:** Any await of this idles the caller purely on a wall-clock timer, not on a completion signal; a primary source of arbitrary fixed delays and thus test flakiness/latency wherever it is used.
- **Blast radius:** Foundational shared helper with many importers; replacing timer-based waits with event/signal-driven waits would touch every call site. High difficulty, high fan-out.
- **Insight:** The canonical timer-sleep primitive. Load-bearing but a smell magnet: most timing-related flakiness in the codebase ultimately routes through helpers like this.

#### `packages/utils/test/logger.test.ts:1388-1391` — busy-wait spin loop  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `describe(timing) > it(should record timing with timeStart/timeEnd pair)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / busy-wait spin loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const start = performance.now();
  while (performance.now() - start < 2) {
    // busy wait
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Burns ~2ms of CPU so the logger's timeStart/timeEnd measurement records a non-zero, measurable elapsed value in the assertion.
- **Design:** Tight while loop spinning on performance.now() until 2ms have elapsed; synchronous, blocks the thread, no yielding.
- **Impact:** Test-only; deterministic in that it always produces >=2ms but wastes CPU and slows the test slightly. Could still be flaky if the clock has coarse resolution.
- **Blast radius:** Confined to this single test; replaceable with an injected/mock clock. Trivial.
- **Insight:** A test smell — spinning the CPU to fabricate elapsed time. Info-level; would be better served by a controllable clock but harmless.

#### `packages/utils/test/logger.test.ts:1533-1536` — busy-wait spin loop  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `describe(timing) > it(should record timing via time() direct)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / busy-wait spin loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const delay = performance.now();
  while (performance.now() - delay < 2) {
    // busy wait
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Burns ~2ms so the logger.time(startTime, ...) direct-measurement path records a measurable elapsed value.
- **Design:** Tight synchronous while loop spinning on performance.now() until 2ms elapse; blocks the thread with no yield.
- **Impact:** Test-only CPU spin; deterministic minimum elapsed but wastes cycles and depends on clock resolution.
- **Blast radius:** Confined to this single test; trivially replaceable with an explicit endTime argument or mock clock.
- **Insight:** Same busy-wait test smell as the timeStart/timeEnd case; info-level and harmless but avoidable given time() already accepts an explicit end timestamp.

#### `packages/utils/test/sleep.test.ts:11-16` — synchronous busy-wait spin loop  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `busySpin`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / synchronous busy-wait spin loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const end = performance.now() + ms;
  while (performance.now() < end) { /* burn */ }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Holds the event loop synchronously for ~ms to simulate a CPU-bound compile step, so the yieldToEventLoop timer-budget test can exercise the case where a long synchronous stretch elapses between yields.
- **Design:** A while loop that repeatedly reads performance.now() and burns CPU until the wall clock passes a computed end time; no cancellation, no yield to the loop while spinning.
- **Impact:** Test-only. Deliberately consumes CPU; the 3ms spins in the timer-budget test are tuned so the TIMER_TURN_BUDGET_MS threshold elapses within a bounded number of iterations. Slower CI hosts still pass since the bound is loose.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test file; changing the spin duration could destabilize the timer-turn-budget assertion at line 58-78 if the budget no longer elapses within 20 iterations.
- **Insight:** Defensible test scaffolding; a synchronous busy-wait is the correct tool to model a CPU-bound step that cannot be replaced by an async timer here.

#### `packages/utils/test/sleep.test.ts:19-25` — async sleep with wall-clock lower-bound assertion  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `describe("sleep") > it("resolves after the given timeout")`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / async sleep with wall-clock lower-bound assertion
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const start = performance.now();
  await sleep(5);
  expect(performance.now() - start).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(4);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Verifies the sleep() utility resolves only after the requested delay elapses.
- **Design:** Awaits sleep(5) and asserts the measured elapsed time is at least 4ms; the 4ms lower bound (vs 5) keeps the assertion from flaking since timers never fire early but scheduling granularity varies.
- **Impact:** Test-only, ~5ms wall time. Low flake risk by design (loose lower bound).
- **Blast radius:** Just this test; tied to the sleep() implementation in @commonfabric/utils/sleep.
- **Insight:** Standard, well-guarded timer test; the comment shows awareness of the early-fire flake class.

### `scripts`

#### `scripts/start-local-dev.sh:144-152` — fixed sleep after kill to let the OS release the port  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `free_port_if_forced`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed sleep after kill to let the OS release the port
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  echo "$pids" | xargs kill 2>/dev/null
  sleep 1
  pids=$(get_pids_on_port "$port")
  ```
- **Purpose:** Under --force, kills whatever holds the port, waits, then force-kills survivors; the sleep gives processes a moment to exit and release the socket before re-checking.
- **Design:** Sends SIGTERM, sleeps a hardcoded 1s, re-queries pids on the port, and escalates to kill -9 with another 1s sleep if any remain. No adaptive backoff; fixed one-second grace windows.
- **Impact:** Adds up to ~2s to a forced restart. If a process needs longer than 1s to release the port, the subsequent bind can still fail; the fixed grace is a heuristic rather than a guarantee.
- **Blast radius:** Only runs on --force. A refactor toward polling-until-released would remove the guesswork but touch nothing outside this function.
- **Insight:** Classic sleep-after-kill smell: a timer standing in for a completion signal (port actually freed). Low stakes since it is dev-only and gated on --force.

#### `scripts/start-local-dev.sh:376-377` — fixed sleep before liveness check of spawned background service  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `top-level (--bg-updater branch)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed sleep before liveness check of spawned background service
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  BG_PID=$!
  cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.."
  sleep 2
  ensure_process_running "background service" "$BG_PID" "$BG_LOG"
  ```
- **Purpose:** After launching the background-piece-service, waits 2s then checks the process is still alive, catching an immediate crash (e.g. bad config) before reporting success.
- **Design:** Single hardcoded 2s sleep, then a one-shot kill -0 / zombie check. Not a loop; a fixed settling window chosen to be long enough to catch fast failures.
- **Impact:** Adds a flat 2s to --bg-updater startup and can miss a crash that happens after the 2s window. Comment elsewhere notes the service then polls bgUpdater every 60s.
- **Blast radius:** Only the --bg-updater path; stop script and PID-file tracking depend on the recorded BG_PID, not on this timing.
- **Insight:** A settle-then-check heuristic; would be more robust waiting on the listen/ready marker like the other servers, but the crash it guards against is usually immediate so 2s is pragmatic.

#### `scripts/stop-local-dev.sh:70-77` — fixed sleep between SIGTERM and SIGKILL escalation  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `top-level (background-piece-service teardown)`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed sleep between SIGTERM and SIGKILL escalation
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  kill "$BG_PID" 2>/dev/null
  sleep 1
  if ps -p "$BG_PID" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    kill -9 "$BG_PID" 2>/dev/null
  ```
- **Purpose:** Gives the background-piece-service one second to exit gracefully after SIGTERM before force-killing it, so a clean shutdown is preferred over an abrupt kill.
- **Design:** Single hardcoded 1s sleep between the graceful kill and the escalation check; no loop, no adaptive wait. PID read from the .bg-piece-service.pid file.
- **Impact:** Adds up to 1s to teardown and may SIGKILL a process that would have exited at 1.1s. Dev-tooling stop script, so effect is negligible.
- **Blast radius:** Self-contained teardown path; nothing depends on its timing beyond the removed PID file.
- **Insight:** Standard graceful-then-force shutdown grace period. A poll-until-dead loop would be marginally cleaner but the fixed 1s is fine for a stop script.

### `skills`

#### `skills/deno-memory-profiler/scripts/memory.ts:416-417` — fixed 200ms settle delay to drain CDP snapshot chunks  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `takeSnapshot`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed 200ms settle delay to drain CDP snapshot chunks
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await client.send("HeapProfiler.takeHeapSnapshot", { reportProgress: false });
  // Small delay to ensure all chunks arrive
  await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 200));
  ```
- **Purpose:** After requesting a heap snapshot over the Chrome DevTools Protocol, waits 200ms so any straggling addHeapSnapshotChunk events accumulate before joining and parsing them.
- **Design:** Hardcoded 200ms setTimeout sleep after the takeHeapSnapshot send resolves; chunks are collected by an event handler pushing into an array. No signal that all chunks have arrived is awaited.
- **Impact:** If chunks arrive later than 200ms the parse could see a truncated snapshot; if they arrive faster it wastes up to 200ms. This is a timer standing in for an end-of-stream signal, so it is the fragile kind of sleep.
- **Blast radius:** Affects snapshot, diff baseline, and diff compare commands. A refactor would key off a completion signal (takeHeapSnapshot resolution should follow the last chunk, or CDP provides a finished marker), removing the guess.
- **Insight:** A classic settle-delay smell: a fixed sleep substituting for a proper end-of-chunks signal. Load-bearing enough that a too-large snapshot on a slow link could corrupt the parse, but this is a manual dev profiler so severity stays low.

#### `skills/deno-memory-profiler/scripts/memory.ts:207-209` — setTimeout-based async sleep to bound a sampling window  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `cmdSample`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout-based async sleep to bound a sampling window
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  console.error(`Sampling for ${duration}s...`);
  await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, duration * 1000));
  const result = ... client.send("HeapProfiler.stopSampling")
  ```
- **Purpose:** Runs V8 heap allocation sampling for a fixed user-specified duration (default 5s), then stops sampling and reports the top allocation sites.
- **Design:** Awaits a Promise wrapping setTimeout for duration*1000 ms between HeapProfiler.startSampling and stopSampling. Duration is a CLI arg (--duration). No cancellation.
- **Impact:** Determines how long the profiler observes allocations; longer means more representative sampling. Dev/profiling tool only, invoked manually, so no production or test impact.
- **Blast radius:** Local to the sample command of a standalone profiler script. Trivially adjustable.
- **Insight:** Legitimate fixed-window sampling sleep -- a duration is inherently the mechanism here, not a workaround. Defensible.

### `tasks`

#### `tasks/integration.ts:118-122` — fixed 3s settle sleep after start-local-dev.sh returns  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `startServers`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / fixed 3s settle sleep after start-local-dev.sh returns
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  console.log("Waiting for servers to be fully ready...");
  await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 3000));
  ```
- **Purpose:** After start-local-dev.sh reports success, waits an extra 3 seconds on the belief that the servers need a bit more time to be fully ready before integration tests hit them.
- **Design:** Unconditional 3000ms setTimeout sleep tacked onto every successful server start; not adaptive, not tied to any readiness signal (the script already waited on HTTP 200).
- **Impact:** Adds a flat 3s to every integration run's setup. Because start-local-dev.sh already polls for HTTP 200, this extra sleep is largely redundant belt-and-suspenders; if a server truly needs more than 3s the test flakes anyway.
- **Blast radius:** Every server-backed integration run (runner, shell, patterns, cli, runtime-client) pays this. Removing it would speed CI slightly but risks exposing any residual startup race the sleep currently papers over.
- **Insight:** Redundant-settle smell: the underlying script already gates on readiness, so this is a superstition sleep. Replaceable by trusting the script's HTTP gate, but low risk to leave.

#### `tasks/perf-lib.ts:320-323` — setTimeout promise sleep  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `sleep`
- **Category / subtype:** sleep / setTimeout promise sleep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  function sleep(ms: number): Promise<void> {
    if (ms <= 0) return Promise.resolve();
    return new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, ms));
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Asynchronous delay primitive used to pace retries between GitHub API/artifact-download attempts so the CI perf tooling backs off rather than hammering the API.
- **Design:** Wraps setTimeout in a promise; short-circuits to a resolved promise for non-positive durations. No cancellation/AbortSignal; the timer always runs to completion.
- **Impact:** Idles the caller for the requested backoff window. Purely additive latency in a CI script; no correctness impact but each retry adds wall time to the perf-check/perf-regression jobs.
- **Blast radius:** Called only from githubGet and downloadAndExtractArtifact retry loops in this file. Replacing it (e.g. with an abortable sleep) would touch those two call sites; no tests depend on its timing.
- **Insight:** Standard, defensible retry-pacing helper. Load-bearing for rate-limit resilience against the GitHub API. Not cancellable, which is fine for a short-lived batch CI tool.

## Wall-clock readings feeding control-flow  (43)

### `packages/background-piece-service`

#### `packages/background-piece-service/src/space-manager.ts:49-63` — deadline computation (Date.now + delay)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `SpaceManager.pushTask`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / deadline computation (Date.now + delay)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const when = whenInMs ?? this.rerunIntervalMs;
  const timestamp = Date.now() + when;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Compute the absolute wall-clock time at which a piece should next run, used to order and gate task execution in execLoop.
- **Design:** Reads Date.now() and adds the delay to form an absolute deadline stored on the Task; tasks are kept sorted by this timestamp and execLoop compares pendingTasks[0].timestamp against a fresh Date.now() to decide readiness.
- **Impact:** The scheduler's due-time gating is entirely wall-clock driven, so it is sensitive to system clock jumps (NTP steps, sleep/resume). Feeds directly into the control-flow decision in execLoop line 164.
- **Blast radius:** Any change to time source (e.g. monotonic performance.now, or an injectable clock for tests) touches pushTask, execLoop, and tests. Low-to-moderate.
- **Insight:** This is the deadline half of the poll loop; it belongs to the same mechanism. Using Date.now() rather than a monotonic clock is a minor fragility for a long-running service across suspend/resume.

#### `packages/background-piece-service/src/worker-controller.ts:165-177` — performance.now stopwatch for task duration  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `WorkerController.exec`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / performance.now stopwatch for task duration
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const task = { startTime: performance.now(), ... };
  ...
  const duration = (performance.now() - task.startTime).toFixed(0);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Record the start time of each IPC task so logTaskResults can report how long the worker took.
- **Design:** startTime captured with performance.now() at dispatch; logTaskResults subtracts it from a fresh performance.now() to compute a millisecond duration string.
- **Impact:** Purely observational: the duration is only logged, it does not gate, abandon, or bound anything (that is the setTimeout's job). Included per the wall-clock rule but borderline; it feeds no control-flow decision.
- **Blast radius:** Only the log line. Removing it changes log output only. Trivial.
- **Insight:** A stopwatch used solely for logging. Borderline exclude; noted as info because it measures elapsed time but drives no decision.

### `packages/fuse`

#### `packages/fuse/cell-bridge.ts:766-778` — TTL/age check on detached subtrees  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `CellBridge.cleanupRetainedSubtrees / retainDetachedSubtree`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / TTL/age check on detached subtrees
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (expiresAt > now) continue;
  this.retainedSubtrees.delete(ino); this.tree.clear(ino);
  ... this.retainedSubtrees.set(ino, Date.now() + ttlMs);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Keep a detached (renamed-away) subtree alive for a grace window (default 1000ms) so in-flight FUSE reads of stale inodes still resolve, then garbage-collect it once expired.
- **Design:** retainDetachedSubtree records Date.now()+ttlMs as an expiry; cleanupRetainedSubtrees (called at the head of prepareLookup/prepareDirectory/rebuildPieceProp) walks the map and clears any entry whose expiry is in the past. Time-driven, but swept lazily on the next relevant operation rather than by a timer.
- **Impact:** Uses wall clock to bound how long stale inode subtrees linger; affects memory (subtrees held up to TTL) and correctness of concurrent reads during a rebuild. Sweep only runs when those methods are called, so an idle mount may retain past TTL until the next access.
- **Blast radius:** retainedSubtrees map, tree.detach/clear, and every caller of cleanupRetainedSubtrees. Tightening the TTL risks EIO on slow concurrent reads; loosening grows memory. Refactor to refcounted retention (retainedSubtrees already coexists with a numeric retain map) would remove the clock dependence.
- **Insight:** A time-based grace period standing in for a proper 'no more readers' signal — a pragmatic workaround for FUSE inode caching. Lazy sweep is fine but means TTL is a lower bound, not exact.

#### `packages/fuse/cell-bridge.ts:1183,1274,1385` — elapsed-duration measurement (Date.now delta)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `CellBridge.rebuildPieceProp`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / elapsed-duration measurement (Date.now delta)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const startedAt = Date.now(); ... this.rebuildStats.lastDurationMs = Date.now() - startedAt;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Record how long the last subtree rebuild took, surfaced in the .status file's rebuilds.lastDurationMs field for operator visibility.
- **Design:** Captures Date.now() at entry and computes the delta at each return point, storing it in rebuildStats.lastDurationMs. Wall clock, not monotonic (performance.now).
- **Impact:** Pure observability — the duration is written to .status and never gates or abandons work. Using Date.now (wall clock) instead of performance.now means NTP steps could skew a reported figure, but nothing depends on it.
- **Blast radius:** rebuildStats.lastDurationMs and getStatusJson only. Zero control-flow impact; trivially replaceable with performance.now.
- **Insight:** Borderline: it measures elapsed time but feeds only a status readout, not a wait/abandon decision. Included as info-level per the audit's wall-clock category since it computes a duration; effectively a diagnostic metric.

### `packages/iframe-sandbox`

#### `packages/iframe-sandbox/src/common-iframe-sandbox.ts:441-446` — grace-period elapsed check since page load  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `CommonIframeSandboxElement.onHealthCheckFailure`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / grace-period elapsed check since page load
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if ((performance.now() - this.pageLoadTimestamp) < HEALTH_CHECK_LOAD_DELAY) {
    this.requestHealthCheck();
    return;
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Suppress crash-on-failure during the first 5000ms after page load, giving a freshly loaded piece time to settle before a failed health check is treated as a real crash; instead it re-queues another check.
- **Design:** Measures elapsed since pageLoadTimestamp (set at line 138 from performance.now()) against HEALTH_CHECK_LOAD_DELAY (5000ms). Under the grace window it recurses into requestHealthCheck; past it, sets crashed = true and initialized = false.
- **Impact:** Gates a correctness decision (crash vs retry) on wall-clock elapsed time. Within the window failures loop back into the poll rather than aborting. Only meaningful when health checking is enabled.
- **Blast radius:** Touches onHealthCheckFailure and the crashed/initialized state that drives the render() crash UI. Tied to the same disabled subsystem. Low-moderate difficulty.
- **Insight:** A TTL-style grace window feeding a control-flow decision, the textbook wall-clock gating case. Reasonable but wall-clock-based rather than tied to a real 'load complete' signal.

### `packages/memory`

#### `packages/memory/v2/server.ts:788-792` — TTL deadline computation  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `MemoryServer.nowSeconds / sessionOpenHandshake`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / TTL deadline computation
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  nowSeconds(): number { return this.options.sessionOpenAuth.nowSeconds?.() ?? Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000); }
  expiresAt: this.nowSeconds() + ttl,
  ```
- **Purpose:** Provides the current wall-clock second used to stamp session-open challenge expiry (expiresAt = now + ttl), which auth validation later compares against.
- **Design:** Reads Date.now()/1000 (or an injected clock for tests) and adds challengeTtlSeconds to produce an absolute expiry; the injected-clock seam makes it deterministic under test.
- **Impact:** Feeds the challenge-expired auth gate (session-open-auth.ts:110). A clock read that becomes a security-relevant deadline, so skew or drift affects whether handshakes are accepted.
- **Blast radius:** Session-open auth handshake and its verifier; changing clock source or ttl touches session-open-auth validation and its tests.
- **Insight:** Legitimate TTL clock use with a proper test seam. Included because it computes a deadline that gates auth, not a mere timestamp.

#### `packages/memory/v2/server.ts:134-158` — elapsed-time threshold gate  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `recordSlowQueryDuration (module-level)`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / elapsed-time threshold gate
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const elapsed = performance.now() - startedAt;
  if (elapsed <= SLOW_QUERY_THRESHOLD_MS) { return; }
  recordSlowQuery({ timestamp: Date.now(), elapsed, ... });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Records a slow-query diagnostic only when an operation exceeds a threshold (100ms), gating whether the elapsed measurement is retained.
- **Design:** Callers capture startedAt = performance.now() before the work (server.ts:1983, 2138, 2162, 2274) and pass it in; elapsed is compared against SLOW_QUERY_THRESHOLD_MS = 100 and below-threshold measurements are dropped, above-threshold ones recorded with a Date.now() wall timestamp.
- **Impact:** Diagnostics-only: the elapsed measurement feeds a keep/drop control-flow decision but has no effect on request behavior, retries, or correctness. Purely observational.
- **Blast radius:** Only the slow-query telemetry sink and the four call sites; retuning the threshold changes noise volume, nothing functional.
- **Insight:** Benign wall-clock gate -- included because the elapsed reading drives a branch (record vs return) rather than being a raw log timestamp. Not load-bearing.

#### `packages/memory/v2/session-open-auth.ts:109-124` — TTL/expiry check with clock skew  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `verifySessionOpen (challenge/exp validation)`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / TTL/expiry check with clock skew
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const challengeNow = options.nowSeconds ?? Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
  if (options.challenge.expiresAt <= challengeNow) throw authorizationError('challenge expired');
  if (invocation.exp < now - skew) throw authorizationError('authorization expired');
  ```
- **Purpose:** Rejects a session-open request whose challenge has expired or whose signed authorization exp has passed (allowing a configurable clock-skew tolerance), gating authentication on wall-clock deadlines.
- **Design:** Reads current seconds (injectable via options.nowSeconds for tests), compares challenge.expiresAt <= now to reject stale challenges, and invocation.exp < now - skew (default DEFAULT_CLOCK_SKEW_SECONDS) to reject expired tokens.
- **Impact:** Security-relevant control flow: clock reads decide accept/reject. Skew tolerance guards against minor clock drift between issuer and server; excessive drift or a wrong clock breaks or weakens auth.
- **Blast radius:** Any caller of verifySessionOpen and every session-open client; changing skew or expiry semantics affects auth acceptance and its tests. The nowSeconds injection point is the test seam.
- **Insight:** Standard, defensible token/challenge expiry validation. Included because the clock reads directly gate authorization decisions (abandon vs continue).

#### `packages/memory/v2/session-registry.ts:52-58, 178` — TTL-based session expiry sweep  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `SessionRegistry.#prune / SessionRegistry.detach`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / TTL-based session expiry sweep
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  #prune(now = Date.now()): void { if (session.expiresAt !== null && session.expiresAt <= now) this.#sessions.delete(key); }
  session.expiresAt = Date.now() + this.#ttlMs;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Detaching a session arms a TTL (default 30s) after which the session is eligible for eviction; #prune sweeps and deletes sessions whose expiresAt has passed, reclaiming detached-but-not-resumed sessions.
- **Design:** detach sets expiresAt = Date.now() + ttlMs (30_000 default); #prune (called opportunistically at open()) iterates all sessions and deletes those with expiresAt <= now. Lazy sweep -- no timer, expiry realized only when #prune runs.
- **Impact:** TTL/age check gating whether a session can be resumed; a resume arriving after the TTL finds the session gone. Because prune is lazy (only on open), actual eviction timing depends on traffic, not a scheduler -- mostly benign but means expiry is not punctual.
- **Blast radius:** Session resume/reattach semantics; changing ttlMs or making prune timer-driven touches every session lifecycle consumer and resume tests.
- **Insight:** Ordinary lazy TTL cache-expiry. Defensible; the lazy sweep avoids a timer but makes eviction opportunistic rather than deterministic.

### `packages/patterns`

#### `packages/patterns/google/extractors/email-ticket-finder.tsx:589-657` — wall-clock reference-date age/TTL check gating status classification  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `emailTicketFinder pattern body -> tickets computed()`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / wall-clock reference-date age/TTL check gating status classification
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const today = new Date(safeDateNow()); today.setHours(0,0,0,0); const daysUntil = calculateDaysUntil(result.eventDate, today); const status = calculateStatus(daysUntil);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Reads the current wall clock (safeDateNow) once per recompute to establish a 'today' reference, so each ticket's days-until-event and past/today/upcoming status can be derived from how far the event is from now.
- **Design:** safeDateNow() (the runtime's deterministic-in-replay Date.now wrapper) seeds a single Date snapshot normalized to midnight; calculateDaysUntil (lines 271-289) computes Math.ceil of (event - reference)/86400000; calculateStatus (294-300) maps <0 past, 0 today, else upcoming. No timer, no cancellation; recomputes when reactive inputs change.
- **Impact:** Output (status labels, which tickets appear in today/upcoming/past buckets and their sort order) is a function of real time, so it silently changes across midnight boundaries and is non-reproducible unless safeDateNow is virtualized. Determinism depends entirely on safeDateNow being controllable in tests.
- **Blast radius:** A refactor toward event-driven time (e.g. an injected clock cell) would touch calculateDaysUntil/calculateStatus, the tickets/todayTickets/upcomingTickets/pastTickets computeds, and any golden-output tests; observable UI bucketing would shift. Low difficulty, contained to this file.
- **Insight:** Defensible and idiomatic for a date-status view; the single-snapshot 'today' is a good pattern that avoids per-item clock skew. Load-bearing for correctness of the categorization but not fragile. Reliance on safeDateNow is the intended determinism seam.

#### `packages/patterns/google/extractors/united-flight-tracker.tsx:555-718` — wall-clock reference-date duration check gating isUpcoming / alert classification  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `unitedFlightTracker pattern body -> flights computed()`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / wall-clock reference-date duration check gating isUpcoming / alert classification
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const now = new Date(safeDateNow()); ... const daysUntilFlight = calculateDaysUntilFlight(...); const isUpcoming = daysUntilFlight >= 0;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Uses the current wall clock as a reference to compute days/hours until each flight's departure and decide whether a flight is upcoming vs past, which drives active-flight selection, the delay/cancellation alert list, and the past-flights list.
- **Design:** safeDateNow() seeds a 'now' Date; calculateDaysUntilFlight (~lines 400-418) and calculateHoursUntilFlight (~440-460) return elapsed durations as (departure - reference)/(ms per day|hour); isUpcoming = daysUntilFlight >= 0 (line 608) gates status coercion (completed if past) and downstream filters at lines 728, 743, 750, 772. No timer/cancellation; purely recompute-on-input.
- **Impact:** Which flights count as upcoming, which raise active delay/cancellation alerts, and sort order all depend on real time and flip as departures pass. Non-deterministic outside a controlled clock; a mocked safeDateNow is required for stable tests.
- **Blast radius:** Refactoring the time source would touch both duration helpers, the isUpcoming gate, activeFlights/alertFlights/pastFlights/trips computeds, and any snapshot tests of the alert section. Contained to this file; low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Standard time-relative dashboard logic; the hours-until helper hints at a future check-in-window feature. Load-bearing but defensible; the only real risk is test flakiness if the clock is not pinned.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/cfc-browser-helpers.ts:741, 1129-1135` — rt.idle() quiescence await + performance.now() step timing  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `collectSchedulerLoadSummary (rt.idle at 741); StepTimer.run`
- **Category / subtype:** wall-clock / rt.idle() quiescence await + performance.now() step timing
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const start = performance.now();
  try { return await fn(); } finally { this.#rows.push({ label, ms: Math.round(performance.now() - start) }); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** collectSchedulerLoadSummary awaits rt.idle() (quiescence) before snapshotting timing/graph stats; StepTimer measures elapsed ms per labeled async step for the load summary report.
- **Design:** rt.idle() at line 741 gates the snapshot on quiescence. StepTimer wraps each step with performance.now() bracketing and records ms even when the step throws.
- **Impact:** The rt.idle() await is a real quiescence gate (affects when the snapshot is taken). The StepTimer/performance.now measurements are diagnostic only — they feed printed summaries (logStepTimings/logBrowserLoadSummary), not control flow.
- **Blast radius:** StepTimer output is purely observational; the rt.idle() before the snapshot ensures the collected numbers reflect settled state. Removing StepTimer would only lose reporting.
- **Insight:** Mixed: the rt.idle() await is load-bearing quiescence (already covered by runtimeIdle finding); the performance.now() timing is benchmark/reporting instrumentation that does NOT bound or abandon work. Logged as info because the wall-clock reads here measure-for-reporting rather than gate control flow.

#### `packages/patterns/integration/reload/default-app-notebook.test.ts:97-121` — performance.now() elapsed for reload-to-rendered metric  &nbsp; ⚪ info
