  ... raw(wish, { debounce: WISH_DEBOUNCE_MS })
  ```
- **Purpose:** Registers the 'wish' builtin with a 50ms debounce so rapid successive wish invocations coalesce into a single scheduler re-run rather than firing on every input change.
- **Design:** A fixed 50ms constant passed as the `debounce` option to raw(); the actual debounce timer is implemented in the module/scheduler layer, not here. No jitter, no cancellation logic at this call site — just a magic-number timing value.
- **Impact:** Introduces a 50ms latency floor on wish re-runs and coalesces bursts; affects observable timing/ordering of wish evaluation and could influence determinism of tests that depend on wish firing promptly.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch the scheduler's debounce implementation and any test asserting wish coalescing/timing; the constant is local so changing the value is trivial but changing the mechanism (e.g. to signal-driven) touches the module option contract.
- **Insight:** A defensible small UX/perf debounce. The value is a bare magic number with no source cited; load-bearing only for the wish builtin. The real mechanism lives in the debounce option handler elsewhere — this is just the tuning knob.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/llm-dialog.ts:2801-2820,2925-2928,2961-2976` — AbortController/AbortSignal cancellation  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `llmDialog action + addMessage handler / AbortController lifecycle`
- **Category / subtype:** other / AbortController/AbortSignal cancellation
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  abortController?.abort("New request started"); ... abortController = new AbortController(); ... startRequest(..., abortController.signal, ...)
  ```
- **Purpose:** Cancels an in-flight LLM request when a newer request supersedes it or when the containing pattern is stopped, so a superseded/abandoned LLM call stops consuming work and does not write stale results.
- **Design:** A module-scoped AbortController is aborted (with reason string) on new-request start and on pattern-stop (addCancel), then a fresh controller is created per request and its signal handed to startRequest. Cancellation is event/signal-driven (not timer-driven); requestId equality checks guard against out-of-order aborts.
- **Impact:** Determines whether stale LLM results are suppressed; correct abort wiring is essential for correctness of overlapping requests. Signal-based, so deterministic relative to the triggering events. Not a wall-clock mechanism, but timing-adjacent because it bounds/abandons async work.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor touches startRequest's signal parameter, the gateway/LLM client honoring the signal, and tests around request cancellation/supersession. Difficulty moderate — the signal threads through several layers.
- **Insight:** Clean, signal-driven cancellation — the good pattern (in contrast to the tool-call timeout race). Load-bearing and defensible. Included as timing-related only because it bounds/abandons in-flight async work via a signal rather than a clock.

#### `packages/runner/src/builtins/stream-data.ts:36-39, 125-128, 156-158, 175-177, 220-221` — AbortController cancellation on run-generation change  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `streamData`
- **Category / subtype:** other / AbortController cancellation on run-generation change
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (status.controller) { status.controller.abort(); status.controller = undefined; }
  const controller = new AbortController(); const signal = controller.signal;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Cancels an in-flight fetch/stream when the inputs change (a new request supersedes the old) or when the run generation advances, so a stale stream stops writing to the result cell.
- **Design:** A per-action status.controller AbortController; a new action run aborts the prior controller before starting, and the reader loop aborts its own controller when thisRun!==status.run. AbortError is swallowed in the catch. No timer/deadline is attached to the signal.
- **Impact:** Pure cancellation wiring, not a timeout — no wall clock involved. Correctness: prevents stale streams from clobbering fresh results and from leaking. Determinism depends on run-generation counter, not time.
- **Blast radius:** Local; tied to the while(true) loop's stop condition and the previousCall re-entrancy guard. Any change to how supersession is detected touches both. Minimal.
- **Insight:** Textbook signal-based cancellation; the correct primitive. Included as 'other' because it is timing-adjacent (AbortSignal) but carries no timeout/deadline — it is completion/supersession driven, not clock driven.

#### `packages/runner/src/runner.ts:4047-4056, 4154-4166` — debounce/throttle pass-through to scheduler.subscribe  &nbsp; 🟡 low

  const debounce = module.debounce ?? builtinDebounce;
  const throttle = module.throttle ?? builtinThrottle;
  this.runtime.scheduler.subscribe(action, populateDependencies, { isEffect, debounce, noDebounce, throttle, ... });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Extracts per-node debounce/throttle/noDebounce timing options (from the module definition or from a raw builtin's result) and forwards them to the scheduler when subscribing the action, so the scheduler can rate-limit or coalesce re-runs of that computation.
- **Design:** Pure plumbing: reads optional debounce/throttle flags, applies module-over-builtin precedence via ?? , and passes them through scheduler.subscribe's options object. The actual timing behavior (delay values, coalescing) is implemented in the scheduler, not here.
- **Impact:** Controls whether a given reactive computation is debounced/throttled, affecting re-run frequency, latency, and CPU. As a pass-through it has no timing behavior of its own, but it is the wiring that enables scheduler-side timing for user builtins.
- **Blast radius:** Refactoring the option names or precedence would touch scheduler.subscribe's option contract, every builtin that returns debounce/throttle in its RawBuiltinResult, and module-definition typing. Behavior-observable via any test relying on debounced/throttled builtins.
- **Insight:** Configuration plumbing rather than a timing mechanism itself; the real timing lives in the scheduler. Included as timing-adjacent because it selects and forwards the debounce/throttle knobs.

#### `packages/runner/src/scheduler/subscriptions.ts:129-195` — throttle-gated control-flow skip  &nbsp; 🟠 medium

- **Symbol:** `markEffectDirtyIfStaleInputs / hasStaleWriterForEffectReads`
- **Category / subtype:** other / throttle-gated control-flow skip
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (state.isThrottled(writer)) continue; // Skip throttled - they trigger via storage
  ```
- **Purpose:** On effect resubscribe, decides whether to mark the effect dirty by scanning stale upstream computations. Throttled writers are deliberately skipped because their delayed (timer-driven) re-run will re-trigger the effect via storage changes later.
- **Design:** Iterates writersByEntity for the effect's reads; skips writers that are effects, non-stale, or throttled. The throttle check is a boolean state query (isThrottled) whose truth is maintained by a timer elsewhere; the skip is a pure control-flow branch, not itself a timer.
- **Impact:** Couples effect-scheduling correctness to timer state: if a writer is currently throttled the effect is intentionally NOT scheduled now, relying on a later timer-driven storage change to re-trigger. Wrong throttle bookkeeping could drop or duplicate effect runs, affecting convergence and determinism.
- **Blast radius:** Tied to isThrottled/setThrottle semantics and to the storage-change re-trigger path. Changing throttle semantics would touch this skip, the delay module, and scheduler convergence/throttle tests.
- **Insight:** A subtle but load-bearing dependency of a purely structural scheduling decision on timer-maintained throttle state; defensible but fragile — the comment itself flags the implicit contract that throttled writers WILL re-trigger via storage.

#### `packages/runner/src/storage/v2.ts:1792-1802` — async iterator while(true) consume loop  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `MemoryReplica.consumeUpdates`
- **Category / subtype:** other / async iterator while(true) consume loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  while (true) { const next = await iterator.next(); if (next.done) return; this.applySessionSync(next.value, "integrate"); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Continuously consumes session-sync updates from an async iterator and integrates each into the replica, until the stream ends.
- **Design:** Unbounded while(true) awaiting iterator.next(); exits only on next.done. Each iteration blocks on a real completion (the next update), so it idles between updates rather than spinning.
- **Impact:** This is the update pump that drives event-driven catch-up (and thus resolves the caughtUpLocalSeq waiters). Blocks on the iterator, so no CPU spin. Its liveness underpins the retry gating above.
- **Blast radius:** Central to sync integration; touching it affects all reactive update delivery. High difficulty but the pattern itself is standard.
- **Insight:** A classic await-driven consumer loop, not a busy-wait; while(true) is idiomatic here. Included for completeness because it is the completion-signal source that makes the polling-free retry gate work.

#### `packages/runner/test/compile-interleave.test.ts:33-51` — MessageChannel postMessage macrotask ordering probe  &nbsp; ⚪ info

  expect(queuedTaskRan).toBe(true);

- **Blast radius:** Couples to interleaveCompileYield(true) and the worker IPC ordering assumption; changing the yield mechanism touches this and worker delivery semantics.
- **Insight:** A timing-ordering assertion using MessageChannel rather than a wall clock; test-only but validates a real event-loop-turn contract. Genuinely timing-related, no wait/abandon decision, so 'other'.

#### `packages/runner/test/manual-compile-wedge.ts:52-59` — setInterval event-loop lag probe (5ms period)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level`
- **Category / subtype:** other / setInterval event-loop lag probe (5ms period)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  let lagExpected = performance.now() + 5;
  const probe = setInterval(() => { const now = performance.now(); const lag = now - lagExpected; if (lag > maxLag) maxLag = lag; lagExpected = now + 5; }, 5);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Measures the longest synchronous stretch (event-loop lag) during compile and evaluate by sampling how late a 5ms interval actually fires, mimicking what the browser runner.loop/workerLag would observe. A profiling/diagnostic wedge script, not a test.
- **Design:** A 5ms setInterval records the maximum overshoot between expected and actual fire time into `maxLag`, reset between the compile and evaluate phases, and cleared via clearInterval after a trailing macrotask. Pure measurement; does not gate or abandon any work.
- **Impact:** The probe itself adds minor scheduling overhead but does not affect correctness or control flow. It only produces a printed lag number. Determinism of the measured value depends on machine load.
- **Blast radius:** Standalone diagnostic script; nothing depends on its output programmatically. Removing or changing the probe affects only the console diagnostics.
- **Insight:** A clever manual instrumentation for spotting long synchronous stretches in the compiler; borderline wall-clock-measurement but categorized 'other' because it is a live sampling loop rather than a single elapsed reading. Load-bearing only as a dev tool.

#### `packages/runner/test/memory-v2-remote-session.test.ts:138-144` — signal-based waiter (event/promise handoff, no timer)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Category / subtype:** other / signal-based waiter (event/promise handoff, no timer)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  static whenDialed(count){ if(dialed.length>=count) return Promise.resolve(); return new Promise(resolve=>#waiters.push({count,resolve})); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Lets tests await until N WebSockets have been dialed so per-space host-wiring assertions run only once the expected connections exist, avoiding a polling loop.
- **Design:** A static array of {count,resolve} waiters; the constructor pushes each dialed URL and resolves+filters any waiter whose threshold count is met. No timers, no wall clock; resolution is driven purely by the dial event.
- **Impact:** Deterministic completion-signal synchronization; no flakiness or CPU spin. This is the correct alternative to timing-based waits and is the reason the test does not need a sleep/poll.
- **Blast radius:** Local to this test file. A refactor would only affect the three whenDialed(1|2) call sites; no production code depends on it.
- **Insight:** Explicitly documented as 'no polling'. Load-bearing and exemplary: a completion-signal barrier rather than a timeout. Included as 'other' only because it is timing-adjacent synchronization; it is not a smell.

#### `packages/runner/test/resume-append-scenario.ts:42-85,232-233` — signal-driven barrier (deliberate non-timer gate)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

  ... this.#firstHeldResolve?.();  // resolved when first matching doc is held
  ```
- **Purpose:** Holds inbound memory-transport documents until the test explicitly releases them, and exposes firstHeld so the test awaits the real edge (first held document) instead of sleeping or polling for the resume batch to reconcile.
- **Design:** A wrap() interceptor buffers payloads matching a regex while closed and resolves firstHeld the first time it holds one; release() flushes buffered payloads to the real receiver. Purely event/promise-driven with no timer, stopwatch, or polling.

Genuinely timing-related (it controls delivery ordering) but not a sleep/timeout/retry; the convergence waits in the same file (rt.settled(), rt.idle(), rc.pull()) are likewise completion-driven, not timer-based.

#### `packages/runner/test/scheduler-convergence.test.ts:411-422` — clearTimeout of scheduler's internal pendingQueueTaskTimer  &nbsp; 🟡 low

    clearTimeout(schedulerInternal.pendingQueueTaskTimer);
    schedulerInternal.pendingQueueTaskTimer = null;
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Reaches into scheduler internals to cancel its pending queue-task timer before and after manually invoking execute(), so the test drives the settle loop deterministically without the scheduler's own deferred macrotask re-scheduling interfering.
- **Design:** Casts scheduler to expose pendingQueueTaskTimer/scheduled/execute, clears the timer (a setTimeout handle the scheduler uses to defer its queue drain), calls execute() synchronously, then clears again and resets scheduled=false.

### `packages/runtime-client`

- **Design:** Pass-through await of patternManager.flushCompileCacheWrites(); the drain logic lives in the pattern manager. No timers here.

#### `packages/runtime-client/backends/web-worker/index.ts:49-62` — setInterval heartbeat (event-loop lag probe)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `module-init (loop-lag probe IIFE)`
- **Category / subtype:** other / setInterval heartbeat (event-loop lag probe)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  unrefTimer(setInterval(() => { const now = performance.now(); const lag = now - expected; if (lag > 0) loopLagLogger.time(expected, now, "workerLag"); expected = now + LOOP_LAG_SAMPLE_MS; }, LOOP_LAG_SAMPLE_MS));
  ```
- **Purpose:** A self-rearming 100ms heartbeat that measures how late each tick fires relative to when it was scheduled, so long synchronous stretches (compile, large traverses, GC, CPU starvation) surface as workerLag max/p95 for diagnosing wedged-thread vs slow-handler round trips.
- **Design:** setInterval at LOOP_LAG_SAMPLE_MS=100ms; each tick reads performance.now(), compares to `expected`, records the positive lag via loggerLogger.time, then resets `expected = now + 100`. Wrapped in unrefTimer so importing the entry for unit tests does not leak the interval or trip Deno's op-leak sanitizer; lives for the worker's lifetime.
- **Impact:** Purely observational telemetry; feeds no control-flow. Negligible CPU. Because it is unref'd it will not keep a process alive. The recorded lag is inherently timing-nondeterministic but only ever read as diagnostic stats, so it does not affect correctness.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch only telemetry consumers (integration-test load summaries reading `runner.loop/workerLag`) and the companion main-thread mainLag probe. Low difficulty; no behavioral invariants depend on it.
- **Insight:** Defensible diagnostic instrumentation, not a workaround. The self-rescheduling interval is the standard event-loop-lag idiom. Load-bearing only for observability; safe to disable.

#### `packages/runtime-client/client/connection.test.ts:34-36` — queueMicrotask async ack  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `FakeTransport.send`

- **Snippet:**

  ```
- **Purpose:** Makes the fake transport acknowledge requests on a later microtask, mimicking a real worker's asynchronous round-trip so the connection's pending/settle machinery is exercised as it would be in production rather than resolving synchronously.
- **Design:** queueMicrotask defers the emit of the response one microtask turn; requests whose type is in holdTypes are never acked (used to hold Idle/Dispose replies for disposal tests).
- **Impact:** Test-double timing fidelity only. Ensures pending requests genuinely exist across an await, which several tests depend on. Deterministic (microtask ordering is well-defined).

- **Insight:** Good test-design detail: deferring acks by a microtask is the minimal way to make a fake behave asynchronously without real timers, keeping tests fast and deterministic.

### `packages/ts-transformers`

#### `packages/ts-transformers/test/fixtures/closures/helper-owned-handler-nested-captures.input.tsx:43-53` — debounce (setTimeout + clearTimeout)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `pattern default export (inline action 'trigger') / helper 'flushLater'`
- **Category / subtype:** other / debounce (setTimeout + clearTimeout)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const prev = timer.get(); if (prev !== null) clearTimeout(prev); timer.set(setTimeout(() => { flushLater(...); }, 10));
  ```
- **Purpose:** Debounces a save: each trigger cancels the pending timer and re-arms a 10ms delayed flush so rapid successive triggers coalesce into one save. Exists to demonstrate/repro a transformer bug where nested-callback captures inside setTimeout are dropped from generated handler state.
- **Design:** Stores the timer handle in a Writable cell; on each invocation reads the prior handle, clearTimeout()s it if present, then setTimeout(...,10) re-schedules. Cancellation is explicit via the stored handle; delay is a fixed 10ms literal.
- **Impact:** This is transformer test-fixture INPUT (compiler test data), not executed production runtime; it is never run as a live pattern. As authored pattern source it would coalesce writes and add 10ms latency, but here its only role is to be type-checked/transformed and compared against golden output.
- **Blast radius:** A refactor of the debounce shape would change the fixture and its golden .expected output; it touches the ts-transformers fixture suite (fixture-based.test.ts) and any golden snapshot, not production code. The fixture deliberately encodes a setTimeout-inside-action capture scenario, so the timing construct is load-bearing to the test's intent.
- **Insight:** Defensible and intentional: the setTimeout/clearTimeout debounce is the exact construct under test (verifying nested setTimeout-callback captures survive transformation). Not a smell; do not 'fix' the timing here or the repro is lost.

### `packages/ui`

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-audio-visualizer/cf-audio-visualizer.ts:97-125` — requestAnimationFrame render loop  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFAudioVisualizer.startVisualization (update closure)`
- **Category / subtype:** other / requestAnimationFrame render loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  const update = () => { ... this.requestUpdate(); this.animationFrameId = requestAnimationFrame(update); }; update();
  ```
- **Purpose:** Drives a continuous per-frame read of live audio frequency data and re-render of the waveform bars, producing a real-time visualizer synced to the display refresh.
- **Design:** Self-rescheduling requestAnimationFrame loop; frame handle stored in animationFrameId and torn down via cancelAnimationFrame in stopVisualization/disconnectedCallback. The closure early-returns if the stream/analyser was cleared. Pre-allocated buffers avoid per-frame GC.
- **Impact:** Runs every frame while a stream is active, consuming CPU/GPU for the animation; correct teardown avoids leaks. Timing is display-driven, not a completion signal, but that is intrinsic to a live visualizer.
- **Blast radius:** Refactor would touch start/stopVisualization and disconnectedCallback. The loop is the component's whole purpose; not replaceable by an event/signal. Low difficulty, self-contained.
- **Insight:** Load-bearing and idiomatic for a real-time visualizer. Clean cancellation and buffer reuse make it defensible; not a smell.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-link-preview/cf-link-preview.ts:215-257` — AbortController fetch cancellation (supersede-in-flight)  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `CFLinkPreview._fetchPreview`
- **Category / subtype:** other / AbortController fetch cancellation (supersede-in-flight)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (this._abortController) this._abortController.abort();
  const controller = new AbortController();
  const response = await fetch(apiUrl, { signal: controller.signal });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Cancels any in-flight link-preview fetch when the url changes or the element disconnects, so a stale response cannot overwrite state for a newer url.
- **Design:** Stores a single AbortController per instance; aborts the previous one before starting a new fetch, and aborts in disconnectedCallback. After the await it also checks identity (this._abortController !== controller) to drop stale results and suppresses AbortError.
- **Impact:** Prevents last-writer-wins races between overlapping fetches; cancellation is driven by a real signal, not a timer, so it is deterministic. No wall-clock deadline is imposed on the fetch.
- **Blast radius:** Local to the component; any refactor to add a timeout (AbortSignal.timeout) would layer onto this. Low difficulty.
- **Insight:** Good signal-driven cancellation with no timeout; the identity re-check is a belt-and-suspenders guard. Included as 'other' since it is timing/lifecycle-related cancellation rather than a wait/deadline.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/components/cf-prompt-input/cf-prompt-input.ts:518-543, 999-1006` — Promise.allSettled await on in-flight uploads  &nbsp; 🟡 low

  ```
  const pending = Array.from(this._uploadPromises.values()); if (pending.length) { this._sending = true; ...; await Promise.allSettled(pending); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** On submit, waits for any in-flight attachment blob uploads to finish so each attachment carries its blob url before the cf-send event is emitted, fixing a race where a quick submit shipped a ref-less attachment and a captured image silently vanished.
- **Design:** Each addAttachment stores its upload promise in _uploadPromises (deleted on settle and on removeAttachment); _handleSend collects the current promises and awaits Promise.allSettled, toggling _sending to disable the send button during the wait. Not timer-based — driven by upload completion signals.
- **Impact:** Bounds emit on real completion (no timeout), so no arbitrary deadline; a hung upload would block send indefinitely (no timeout guard). Removes the previous data-loss race. Correctness-improving.
- **Blast radius:** Touches send flow, upload-promise bookkeeping, and the _sending UI state; consumers of cf-send depend on attachments having urls. Low-medium.
- **Insight:** Completion-signal-driven synchronization, not a timing hack — exactly the event-driven approach a refactor would want. Notable only for the absence of any timeout on a stuck upload.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/utils/events.ts:29-45` — throttle  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `throttle`
- **Category / subtype:** other / throttle
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (!inThrottle) {
    func.apply(this, args);
    inThrottle = true;
    setTimeout(() => { inThrottle = false; }, limit);
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Generic leading-edge throttle helper limiting a handler to at most one invocation per `limit` ms window.
- **Design:** Boolean `inThrottle` gate flipped true on first call and reset by a setTimeout(limit). Leading-edge only; calls during the window are dropped entirely with no trailing flush.
- **Impact:** Caps invocation rate; drops intermediate events so the last value in a window may be lost. Timer-driven, deterministic under a fake clock.
- **Blast radius:** Importable utility exercised by events.test.ts; semantics change (adding trailing call) would affect all consumers. Moderate difficulty.
- **Insight:** Simple leading-edge throttle. Dropping the trailing call is a known limitation that can lose the final position/value in a drag; defensible for its UI use but fragile if callers assume the last event lands.

#### `packages/ui/src/v2/utils/events.ts:8-24` — debounce  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `debounce`
- **Category / subtype:** other / debounce
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if (timeout !== null) { clearTimeout(timeout); }
  timeout = setTimeout(() => { func.apply(this, args); timeout = null; }, wait);
  ```
- **Purpose:** Generic trailing-edge debounce helper for web-component event handlers, collapsing bursts of rapid calls into a single invocation after a quiet period.
- **Design:** Closes over a single timeout handle; each call clears the pending timer and re-arms setTimeout(wait). Trailing-edge only, no leading edge, no maxWait, no cancel/flush method exposed.
- **Impact:** Delays handler execution by `wait` ms after the last call; affects latency of UI responses and can hide events in tests unless a fake clock ticks. Deterministic given a controlled timer.
- **Blast radius:** Widely importable utility (used in events.test.ts); changing semantics (e.g. adding leading edge or cancel) would ripple to every consumer and its tests. Moderate difficulty due to fan-out.
- **Insight:** Textbook debounce, correct and load-bearing. Only smell is the lack of a cancel handle, so a debounced handler can fire after its component is torn down.

  ```
- **Purpose:** Provides a minimal DOM window stub so component registration code that calls requestAnimationFrame can run under Deno during the test.
- **Design:** Implements rAF as setTimeout(callback, 0) and cancelAnimationFrame as clearTimeout. No frame timing, fires on the next macrotask.
- **Impact:** Test-only scheduling shim; changes rAF timing from frame-aligned to next-tick, which is acceptable for the registration assertions. No production effect.
- **Blast radius:** Confined to this test's stub; only matters if registration logic starts depending on real frame timing. Trivial.
- **Insight:** Common test polyfill. Info-level; interesting only as evidence that production components schedule via requestAnimationFrame.

### `packages/utils`

#### `packages/utils/src/sleep.ts:17-23` — Deno.unrefTimer detach  &nbsp; 🟡 low

- **Symbol:** `unrefTimer`
- **Category / subtype:** other / Deno.unrefTimer detach
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  (globalThis as { Deno?: { unrefTimer(id: number): void } }).Deno
    ?.unrefTimer?.(id as unknown as number);
  return id;
  ```
- **Purpose:** Detaches a long-lived interval/timeout from Deno's event-loop ref-count so a background/diagnostic timer never keeps the process alive or trips the --trace-leaks op sanitizer.
- **Design:** Optional-chains to Deno.unrefTimer; no-op in the browser. Pure side-effect on an existing timer id, returned for chaining. Does not create or clear the timer.
- **Impact:** Affects process-lifetime/test-teardown behavior rather than runtime timing; prevents false leak reports for heartbeat-style timers.
- **Blast radius:** Used wherever a background setInterval must not block clean shutdown; removing it would reintroduce --trace-leaks failures and hung test processes. Low code footprint, targeted.
- **Insight:** A pragmatic Deno-specific escape hatch tied to the setInterval heartbeat pattern. Defensible and load-bearing for clean teardown; only interesting as a marker of where long-lived timers live.

#### `packages/utils/test/sleep.test.ts:34-56` — MessageChannel macrotask ordering assertion  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `describe("yieldToEventLoop") > it("runs a message task ... ahead of the continuation")`
- **Category / subtype:** other / MessageChannel macrotask ordering assertion
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  port2.postMessage(null);
  await yieldToEventLoop();
  order.push("continuation");
  ```
- **Purpose:** Asserts yieldToEventLoop performs a real macrotask turn (via posted-message task source) rather than only a microtask, so a message queued before the yield runs before the continuation resumes.
- **Design:** Uses a MessageChannel to queue a message task, then awaits yieldToEventLoop and checks ordering; distinguishes microtask vs macrotask semantics.
- **Impact:** Test-only. Guards the macrotask-vs-microtask contract of the yield primitive, which affects fairness/ordering of scheduled work in production.
- **Blast radius:** Couples to yieldToEventLoop internals (must use a posted-message/macrotask source); a switch to microtask-based yielding would break this.
- **Insight:** Timing-related in the event-loop-ordering sense rather than clock-based; a precise, defensible contract test.

### `scripts`

#### `scripts/benchmark-object-hashing/chrome.ts:42` — networkidle0 navigation wait  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `main`
- **Category / subtype:** other / networkidle0 navigation wait
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  await page.goto(htmlUrl, { waitUntil: "networkidle0" });
  ```
- **Purpose:** Waits for the page to reach network idle before proceeding, a quiescence signal that the benchmark HTML and its resources have loaded.
- **Design:** Delegates to the browser automation library's network-idle heuristic (no in-flight requests for a debounce window) rather than a fixed sleep.
- **Impact:** Dev tooling only. Uses an event/quiescence signal instead of an arbitrary delay, which is the better pattern.
- **Blast radius:** Local; behavior depends on Astral's networkidle0 semantics.
- **Insight:** Preferable to a hardcoded sleep; a benign, event-driven wait.

#### `scripts/ci-gantt.ts:86-105` — worker-pool while(true) task-draining loop  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `pool`
- **Category / subtype:** other / worker-pool while(true) task-draining loop
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  while (true) { const i = next++; if (i >= items.length) break; out[i] = await fn(items[i], i); }
  ```
- **Purpose:** A bounded-concurrency worker pool: spawns up to `limit` async workers that each pull the next index off a shared counter until all items are drained, to parallelize gh API job fetches without unbounded concurrency.
- **Design:** while(true) with a shared `next` cursor and an in-bounds break; each worker awaits fn and loops; Promise.all joins all workers. Not timer-driven — the loop is completion-driven (breaks when the work queue is exhausted).
- **Impact:** Dev/CI-analysis tooling. Concurrency is bounded to avoid hammering the GitHub API; loop terminates deterministically once items run out.
- **Blast radius:** Local helper; used for run/job fetches. Refactor would touch both the RUN_IDS path and the completed-runs job-fetch path.
- **Insight:** A while(true) that is really a drain loop, not a busy-wait; standard, safe concurrency-limiting idiom.

#### `scripts/start-local-dev.sh:398-403` — blocking wait on child pids to keep process attached  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `top-level (KEEP_ALIVE branch)`
- **Category / subtype:** other / blocking wait on child pids to keep process attached
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  if [[ "$KEEP_ALIVE" == "true" ]]; then
    ...
    wait "$SHELL_PID" "$TOOLSHED_PID"
  fi
  ```
- **Purpose:** When run under Codex (CODEX_SANDBOX set), keeps the launcher attached to the two server children so Codex does not reap the dev servers when the launching command returns.
- **Design:** Uses bash `wait` on the two server pids, blocking until they exit. This is a completion-signal-driven wait, not a timer, but it is timing-related process lifecycle control.
- **Impact:** Determines whether servers survive the Codex sandbox; correct because it blocks on the actual child exit rather than a timeout. No busy-wait, no polling.
- **Blast radius:** Only the Codex path. Changing it risks orphaned or prematurely-killed dev servers under Codex.
- **Insight:** Proper signal/completion-driven wait -- the opposite of a smell. Included as 'other' because it is process-lifecycle timing but it is exactly the event-driven approach one would want.

### `tasks`

#### `tasks/integration.ts:224-284` — Promise.race concurrency-pool drain (not a deadline)  &nbsp; ⚪ info

- **Symbol:** `runPatternTests / enqueue`
- **Category / subtype:** other / Promise.race concurrency-pool drain (not a deadline)
- **Snippet:**

  ```
  enqueue();
  while (running.size > 0) {
    await Promise.race(running);
  }
  ```
- **Purpose:** Runs pattern test files with a fixed concurrency of 5 by keeping that many child processes in flight, refilling as each finishes; the Promise.race drains the pool without waiting for the slowest straggler each round.
- **Design:** A Set of in-flight promises; each completion re-invokes enqueue() to top up to the concurrency limit. The outer loop awaits Promise.race(running) purely to yield until the next completion, then loops until the set is empty. Promise.race here is a completion demux, not a timeout.
- **Impact:** Governs test parallelism and thus integration wall-time. Completion-driven, deterministic with respect to ordering of results (results pushed as they finish). No timer involved.
- **Blast radius:** Central to pattern-tests integration timing/throughput; the per-test --timeout 180000 (line 237) is the actual abandonment bound. Changing concurrency touches CI wall-time balance.
- **Insight:** Textbook worker-pool using Promise.race as a 'wait for any' primitive -- not a deadline. Load-bearing for CI throughput and correctly event-driven. Noted as 'other' because Promise.race is in the keyword list but its use here is not timing-abandonment.

---

## Appendix — excluded (non-control) wall-clock uses noted during audit

- **chunk 0:** docs/check.ts line 435 `while(true)` in pool() is an index-exhaustion-bounded concurrency worker pool (breaks when i >= items.length), not timer-driven, so not a timing pattern; its `await Promise.all(workers)` is completion-driven. docs/check.ts also spawns `deno` subprocesses but with no timeout. packages/api/cfc.ts line 58 'deadline' is prose in a comment describing the CFC `Expires` atom (a now<=timestamp TTL/expiration capability), not executable timing code here. packages/api/perf/ikeyable_schema.ts:68 and packages/api/perf/schema.ts:224 `retries` are JSON-schema property names (data fields), not retry logic. bgAdmin.tsx:52 and utils.ts:98,99,114 use Date.now() purely as createdAt/updatedAt/disabledAt data timestamps, not for any wait/abandon/bound decision. utils.ts uses `await runtime.storageManager.synced()` which is completion-signal driven, not timing.
- **chunk 1:** worker.ts line 314 comment 'No retry, since events already do that' is prose only, not a mechanism. worker.ts line 225 `await runtime.storageManager.synced()` is a completion-signal await (storage sync barrier), not timer-driven, so excluded as a pure completion await rather than a timing mechanism. bg-admin-module-subprocess.ts line 77 Date.now() and service-modules.test.ts lines 433/454/669/732/757 Date.now() are used purely as data timestamps (disabledAt fields and pendingTask timestamps in fixtures) — line 732/757 (Date.now()+10 / Date.now()-1) set a task's scheduled timestamp as fixture data that the exec loop compares, but the reading itself is a timestamp value, not an elapsed-time measurement, so excluded per the timestamp carve-out. service-modules.test.ts line 559/628/656/701/778/803 pollingIntervalMs/rerunIntervalMs/deactivationTimeoutMs/timeoutMs are configuration values passed to the production SpaceManager (the real timer-driven loop lives in space-manager.ts, not in these assigned files).
- **chunk 2:** cli.ts: (1) `const startedAt = Date.now()` (line 1896) and `const durationMs = Date.now() - startedAt` (line 2120) measure wall-clock elapsed time only to populate the `duration_ms` field of the batch-result JSON; it never bounds, abandons, retries, or gates any work, so it is an excluded pure-metric wall-clock reading. (2) `installCfHarnessSignalHandlers` / `defaultRegisterSignalHandler` (lines 266-321) handle OS SIGINT/SIGTERM signals via Deno.addSignalListener and terminalize the run; these are process-signal handlers, not AbortSignal or timer-driven timing mechanisms. (3) `while (true)` in `resolveHostPathThroughNearestRealParent` (line 813) is a lexical filesystem parent-walk that terminates on the nearest existing real path or the filesystem root; it is not a timed poll. interactive-chat.ts: `new Date().toISOString()` (lines 467, 509) and the `HarnessChatError.retryable?: boolean` type field (line 249) are pure record timestamps and a descriptive flag, not timing/retry control flow. engine.ts: the injectable `now?: () => string` / `#now` clock returns ISO timestamps used only to stamp run-state records and envelopes (excluded pure timestamps); the `while (true)` / `while (isHostPathWithinRoot...)` loops in `#nearestExistingRealHostPath` (line 1034) and `#missingHostPathCanResolveWithinWorkspace` (line 1004) are lexical filesystem parent-walks, not timed polling; `#cfcTransportChecked` is an idempotency guard, not a timing mechanism. No qualifying timeout/sleep/retry-loop/wall-clock-gating instances were found in this chunk.
- **chunk 3:** openai-client.ts uses new Date() (startedAt/endedAt, lines 372/376/403) and performance.now() (373/391/412) purely to compute durationMs and ISO timestamps for attempt diagnostics/telemetry; these measure elapsed time only for reporting and do not gate control flow, so excluded. interactive-chat-stdio.ts while(true)/for-await loops (lines 426, 483, 489, 522) are stream/line readers driven by reader.read() completion, not timers/polling. web-fetch.ts while/for loops for header reads (767), chunked-body decoding (960/1000/1022/1042), byte-writing (1093), redirect iteration (1351, bounded by MAX_REDIRECTS count not clock), body-read loop (1564), and anchor-regex link extraction (1639) are all I/O-completion or bounded-count driven, not timing; the many throwIfWebFetchAborted/withWebFetchAbort/signal helpers are abort-propagation plumbing that enforces the single web_fetch setTimeout deadline already captured. AbortController use in interactive-chat-service (708, 745) is turn cancellation driven by explicit cancelTurn, not a timer.
- **chunk 4:** interactive-chat-service.test.ts: the nextIsoNow() helper (lines 23-29) is a deterministic monotonic fake-clock injected as 'now' producing incrementing ISO timestamps for event ordering/ids, not a wall-clock timing decision, so excluded. Its many 'await service.waitForTurn(...)' calls await a completion signal/promise for a turn (not polling or timers), and the numerous 'await Promise.resolve()' / 'await Promise.all([...])' in the concurrency and event-serialization tests (e.g. lines 662-695) are microtask hops used to model async persistence ordering, not timing sleeps, so excluded. The AbortController/signal usage in the cancel/close tests (lines 383-517) is cancellation-signal plumbing driven by explicit abort()/resolve callbacks, not timers. interactive-chat.test.ts: 'retryable: true' (lines 330,340) and status 'idle' are plain data fields on error/status envelopes, not retry loops or waits, so excluded. openai-client.test.ts: 'connection error: timed out' strings are error-message fixtures, not real timeouts. process-runner.test.ts second test uses no timing.
- **chunk 5:** Multiple `now: (() => { const timestamps = [...]; return () => timestamps.shift() ... })()` clock stubs (lines ~282, 820, 3387, 3582, 3779, 5589, 5705) inject a deterministic ISO-timestamp clock into CfHarnessEngine. These feed run-state fields (createdAt/updatedAt/startedAt/endedAt) that are asserted as data/ordering (e.g. endedAt at lines 339, 3159, 3170, 3279); they are pure timestamps recorded into state, not readings used to measure elapsed time or gate control flow, so excluded per the timestamp/id exclusion. Many keyword hits are substring false positives: `await`/`runPrompt` (wait), `web_search`/`web_fetch` (etc.), and `settle`-like words do not appear as real quiescence loops here. No setTimeout/setInterval/Promise.race/sleep/poll/debounce/throttle/busy-wait patterns exist in this file. `endedAt` at line 339 etc. are assertion values sourced from the stubbed clock, not elapsed-time computations.
- **chunk 6:** sqlite-session-store.test.ts: the many service.waitForTurn(sessionId, turnId) calls (lines 88, 145, 217, 476) are completion-signal awaits that resolve when a turn finishes, not timers/polls/timeouts, so excluded. Deno.makeTempFile/remove are fixture IO, not timing. tools-execution.test.ts: numerous timeoutMs values passed as tool arguments (e.g. 1000, 20, 30_000, 999_999 at lines 361,380,434,774,808,854,899,953,1092,1109,1127,1203 etc.) are configuration data handed to the tool under test rather than a timing mechanism in the test itself; the ones that assert on timeout behavior are captured above, the rest are plain inputs. The fixed fetchedAt timestamp "2026-05-01T17:54:00.000Z" in web-fetch outputs is a frozen clock used as data in assertions, not a control-flow decision. crypto.subtle.digest calls are hashing, not timing. AbortSignal usage at lines 746-747 and 885 is asserted-on signal propagation (the production abort mechanism) covered within the connect/body-read timeout findings above.
- **chunk 7:** packages/cli/lib/callable-command.ts readJsonInputFromStdin (line 44) uses while(true) over reader.read() but is driven purely by the stream's done signal, not timing — excluded. packages/cli/lib/fuse.ts canonicalizeMountLookupPath (line 55) uses while(true) to walk up directory parents via Deno.realPath — a filesystem traversal loop, not timing — excluded; the many `startedAt`/`date` uses in mount state are timestamps for data (entry.startedAt), not control-flow, and are excluded. callable.ts calls to runtime.idle()/synced()/storageManager.synced() are completion-signal awaits (not timers) and are excluded. exec.ts pieceCell.pull() at line 246 is a single pull, not a loop. fuse-exec.sh path_exists uses `timeout --signal=KILL` as a per-probe bound; captured indirectly via the polling helpers rather than as its own finding. Deno.kill/Deno.Signal handling in fuse-supervisor.ts (signal forwarding, addSignalListener) is OS-signal plumbing, not timing, and is excluded.
- **chunk 8:** piece.ts timeCliPhase (lines 159-173) wraps calls in performance.now() only to console.error an elapsed ms line, gated by CLI_TRACE_TIMINGS; pure benchmark/diagnostic logging that never bounds or abandons work, so excluded. Many performance.now() reads in multi-user-test-runner.ts (197,294,313,335,421,432) and test-runner.ts (878,1169,1190,1213,1266,1376,1383,1582,1597,1603,1619,1645,1673) compute durationMs/elapsed values recorded into TestResult/stats or printed for verbose logging only; excluded as pure measurement not feeding a wait/abandon decision. Exceptions retained as findings: the wall-clock deltas at multi-user-test-runner.ts:329 (assertion deadline), multi-user-test-worker.ts:106 and test-runner.ts:1221 (2ms quiescence fast-exit) DO gate control flow and are reported. `test-pattern-result-${Date.now()}` (test-runner.ts:1097) is a name/id timestamp, excluded.
- **chunk 9:** The for(;;) loops in actions.ts (33), editbuffer.ts (514,539), and parse.ts (360,545,1009,1180) are pure text/token/word-boundary scanning loops bounded by string/AST length, not timing. The for(;;) loop in view/mod.ts (181-185) reads stdin via reader.read() and is driven by the stream completion signal (done), not a timer or poll. pager.ts references to signal are Deno.Signal OS-signal listeners (SIGINT/SIGTERM/SIGWINCH) for terminal lifecycle, not AbortSignal timing. 'settle'/'idle'/'wait'/'tick' keyword hits were substrings in prose/identifiers with no timing semantics. No wall-clock (Date.now/performance.now) reads appear in any assigned file.
- **chunk 10:** Many keyword hits were not timing-control constructs and were excluded: (1) settleInspectorCommand and waitForWebSocketOpen in capture-deno-inspector-profile-lib.ts (lines 96-153, 186-217) resolve/reject purely on WebSocket open/close/error events with no timer or wall clock, so they are completion-driven, not sleeps or timeouts. (2) The pervasive 'signal' hits are Deno.Signal OS-signal handling (SIGINT/SIGTERM registration, kill, removeSignalListener) and Deno.CommandStatus.signal fields, not AbortSignal timing. (3) 'settle' in test names/helpers and 'flush' (decoder flush in mirrorOutput tail decode) are not timing waits. (4) The while(true) drain loop in cf-profile.test.ts (lines 28-33, a test drainStream helper) is the same completion-driven reader idiom as mirrorOutput and adds nothing new. (5) profileTimestamp() / new Date().toISOString() in cf-profile-lib.ts line 111-113 is used only to build an output-directory name (a timestamp label for data), not any control-flow decision. (6) parseProfileArgs explicitly REJECTS --profile-timeout-ms / --profile-connect-timeout-ms (lines 80-84), i.e. the tool deliberately has no timeout mechanism. (7) The *.test.ts files contain no setTimeout/sleep/delay; their waits are all await on Promise.withResolvers or process status, which are deterministic and completion-driven.
- **chunk 11:** packages/cli/test/exec.test.ts: numerous `await` on Deno FS ops and the harness `idle`/`commit`/`synced` tracker events are completion-signal driven, not timers, so excluded (the one real timing item is the 15_000ms waitForResult assertion at 1490-1498, reported). packages/cli/test/piece-integration.test.ts line 19 Date.now() is a pure id nonce (noted as an 'other' finding for completeness). packages/cli/test/fuse.test.ts: the many `signal: 'SIGTERM'/'SIGKILL'` occurrences are OS process signals, not AbortSignal timing, and are excluded except where they participate in the terminate-then-kill escalation (reported). fetch-mock.test.ts uses of `signal` are AbortSignal cancellation (reported).
- **chunk 12:** packages/data-model/test/schema-hash.test.ts: all Date.now() uses (lines 116,128,141,395,506,511,521,539) are pure uniqueness suffixes in generated titles (`schemaHashTestAt${Date.now()}-${Math.random()}`), timestamps for id/nonce generation, not feeding any wait/timeout/gating decision -- excluded. packages/data-model/src/frozen-builtins.ts line 75 `while(true)` is an iterator-drain loop (breaks on next.done), no timing. packages/content-hash/test/createHasher.test.ts line 98 `while(true)` is a fixture-processing loop over hash chunks (breaks when inProgress empty), no timing. view-parse-cov.test.ts (lines 436,437,870), view-parse-cov2.test.ts (line 178) and view-parse-gate.test.ts (line 196) `for(;;)`/`while(true)` occurrences are string literals used as parser input fixtures, not executed loops. valueEqual.ts line 47 'settled' is prose in a comment about Object.is, not a timing construct.
- **chunk 13:** packages/data-model/test/schema-utils.test.ts: all Date.now() uses (lines 906, 930, 955, 971, 992, 1060, 1074) are only string-suffix uniqueness for test titles/keys combined with Math.random(); pure timestamp-as-id, no wait/bound decision. packages/deno-web-test/harness/harness.js: performance.now() at lines 20 and 30 measures a single test's duration purely for reporting (returned as TestResult.duration), not to bound or abandon work. packages/deno-web-test/runner.ts: the while(true) at line 49 drains runNextTest() until it returns falsy (a completion signal), not a timer/poll; excluded. packages/deno-web-test/utils.ts: the while(true) at line 273 (findWorkspaceConfigPath) walks parent directories until the filesystem root (parentDir === currentDir), a structural traversal not a timing loop. packages/felt/builder.ts: performance.now() at lines 44/92/96 measures and prints total build time only, not bounding work.
- **chunk 14:** daemon.ts acquireLock (lines 26-47) probes an existing lockfile's PID liveness via Deno.kill(pid, 'SIGCONT') and reclaims stale locks; this is process-liveness detection, not a timing/wait mechanism, so excluded. Deno.watchFs loop (lines 236-241) and editsCell.sink (244) are event-driven triggers, not timers. cell-bridge.test.ts also contains many bare `await`s on loadPieceTree/hydratePieceProp/addPiece and `await Promise.resolve()` (2628) microtask flushes that yield ordering but carry no timer/deadline; the firstCanFinish/firstGetGate gates (1084, 2582) are explicit promise handoffs (completion signals), not timing. All excluded as non-timing.
- **chunk 15:** Line 316 `this.startedAt = new Date().toISOString()` is a plain startup timestamp surfaced in .status (not a duration/deadline) — excluded. The `debounceTimer = undefined` resets and `clearTimeout` cleanup calls are part of the debounce/timer findings, not separate instances. rebuildStats counters (scheduled/coalesced/completed) are plain metrics, not timing. Note: rebuildPieceProp's Date.now duration (lines 1183/1274/1385) is reported as info-level wall-clock because it computes an elapsed duration, though it only feeds the .status readout and gates nothing.
- **chunk 16:** packages/fuse/mod.ts line 85 uses Date.now() only as a timestamp prefix in a supervisor log line (not feeding any wait/abandon/bound decision) - excluded. The many flush/flushing/settle/drain/reconcile references in fuse/mod.ts (e.g. lines 1408-1701 flushHandle body, finalizeWritePath, writeStats.flushed/flushErrors) are the write-commit machinery; the timer-driven parts are captured in the scheduleFlush/queueMicrotask/invalidation findings and the remaining occurrences are plain async awaits with no timer/poll, so not separately reported. In counter-complex-union-state.test.ts the strings "retry" (lines 65,73,80,103) are pattern payload/note data and "loading:1:retry" expected values - not timing constructs - excluded; that file has zero timing findings. runtime.idle() calls (pattern-harness lines 104,123,160) await the runtime's own quiescence signal (completion-driven, not a timer/poll) so are not reported as findings.
- **chunk 17:** mood-diary.test.ts and sleep-journal.test.ts had only false-positive keyword hits: 'deadline' appears solely as tag string data in expected values, 'sleep journal' is a pattern/scenario name, and the harness call runPatternScenario is a normal await with no timing logic. No timing findings in either test file. In debug.ts, the queueMicrotask(() => cancel()) at line 53 defers unsubscribe out of the subscription callback (ordering/safety, not a wait/abandon decision) and is not reported as a standalone finding; it is covered as part of the readCellAsync mechanism.
- **chunk 18:** No pure-timestamp, id/nonce, log, seed, or benchmark wall-clock uses were found in these three files. No Date.now()/performance.now() appear at all. The AbortController/signal usage in main-renderer.test.ts (lines 14-47, MockConnection lifetime) is a lifecycle/teardown abort, not a timeout or wall-clock timing decision, so it was excluded. The drainViewUpdates test steps (lines 46-114) exercise a single-pass structural scan of updateComplete promises and are covered indirectly by the viewSettled loop finding; the single scan itself is not a timing wait so no separate finding was created for them.
- **chunk 19:** Excluded await runtime.idle() (worker-reconciler-cell-props.test.ts lines 1562 and 1574): this is a completion-signal-driven quiescence await resolved by the scheduler becoming idle, not a timer/stopwatch, so it is not a timing mechanism per the category definitions. Excluded Identity.fromPassphrase (both files, the only 'signer' keyword hit) and tx.commit()/runtime.dispose() awaits — these are ordinary async completions, not timing constructs. No Date.now/performance.now, retry loops, polling loops, or deadlines appear in either file; the only timing construct present is the repeated fixed 10ms sleep captured above.
- **chunk 20:** packages/iframe-sandbox/test/iframe-csp.test.ts had zero timing constructs. In common-iframe-sandbox.ts, `this.pageLoadTimestamp = performance.now()` (line 138) is the timestamp capture itself and is not a finding in isolation, but it feeds the grace-period gating at line 442 which is reported. instanceId/ensureSameDocument staleness checks (lines 267-269, 302-336, 396-457) are async-boundary identity guards, not timer-based, so excluded. HEALTH_CHECKING_ENABLED=false disables the whole health-check subsystem at runtime; findings on it are reported as its timing design is intact and re-enablement is a flag flip.
- **chunk 21:** packages/integration/cdp-profiler.ts:115,206,215 and 308 use performance.now()/attachedAt purely as a sort key / report wall-time printout (renderProfileReport prints elapsed ms; no work is abandoned) — excluded as timestamp/benchmark data. packages/integration/page.ts:135 Date.now() is a console-log-tail timestamp, not a control decision. packages/integration/utils.ts:64-73 awaitViewSettled awaits commonfabric.viewSettled() — a real completion signal, not a timer. packages/js-compiler/source-map.ts:39 for(;;) and compiler.ts:337 for(;;) are string-decode / generator-drain loops with no timing element. compiler.ts:403,414,424,446 performance.now() only feed compileTimingLogger.time(...) on a logger constructed with {enabled:false} — pure phase-duration diagnostics, no control flow. packages/lib-shell/src/runtime.ts idle()/synced()/signal (lines 371-373,391-393,446-448) are thin delegations to #client completion signals / an AbortSignal for cancellation, and the line 321 'retries' comment is cache eviction on rejection, not a retry loop — all completion-driven, not timer-based. lib-shell/test/runtime.test.ts:13,40,605-611 exercise that same delegated AbortSignal/idle() (assertion plumbing), and the queueMicrotask calls at 394,409,460 are StubWorker scaffolding simulating async worker message delivery, not re-queuing the same work.
- **chunk 22:** packages/llm/src/prompts/pattern-guide.ts line 113: `const cacheBreaker = Date.now()` appears inside a template-literal documentation string (a prompt-authoring example), used purely as a cache-busting nonce appended to an LLM message; it feeds no wait/abandon/bound decision and is not even executed code, so excluded. packages/memory/interface.ts lines 656-657: the ConflictError interface declares optional `retryAfterSeq?: number` and `readyToRetry?: () => Promise<void>` fields, describing a retry-on-signal contract; this is a pure TypeScript type declaration with no runtime timing logic in this file, so no finding is raised here (the actual retry mechanism would live in an implementing module). Other keyword grep hits in interface.ts (Await/AwaitResult/Awaited) are unrelated substring matches, not signal/timeout usage. In client.ts, the `while (!doneReading)` stream loop (617) and inner newline-scan `while` (625) are completion-signal-driven reader loops / string parsing, not timing polls, so excluded.
- **chunk 23:** Numerous FakeTime.tickAsync / time.runMicrotasks calls (e.g. lines 1108, 2663-2688, 2707-2721, 2928-2951 in v2-client-test.ts) are the deterministic virtual-clock advancement mechanism of @std/testing FakeTime; the substantive timing behaviors they exercise (reconnect backoff, ack scheduling) are captured as findings above rather than double-counting each tick call. queueMicrotask calls at v2-client-test.ts lines 98, 1741, 2849, 3010 and v2-restore-flush-test.ts line 67 are mock-transport devices that defer a synthetic close/response to the microtask queue to simulate async network delivery within the test double; they do not implement retry/quiescence loops and are excluded. Date.now() readings inside the two waitFor helpers ARE reported (they compute a wall-clock deadline). No pure-timestamp/id/log/seed uses of Date.now or performance.now were found in either file.
- **chunk 24:** v2.ts is a pure protocol/types module with zero timing mechanisms: line 104 'retry' is prose in a doc comment; line 592 `retryAfterSeq?: number` is a wire-protocol data field (conflict-resolution hint), not control flow; SessionOpenChallenge.expiresAt (line 273) is a data field, not an elapsed-time measurement in this file. In the test files, the many `await ...receive(...)` / `await server.close()` / `await server.flushSessions(...)` awaits and the manual promise gates (releaseTx2/releaseTx3/releaseFirstRefresh/waitForRefresh at ~2462, 2619-2632, 2835-2848, 1327-1367) are completion-signal-driven (deferred promises resolved by the test), NOT timers, so they are excluded. Numerous additional `time.tickAsync(0/1/300)` + `time.runMicrotasks()` calls throughout are the same FakeTime debounce-driving pattern already characterized by the three reported findings and are not separately listed.
- **chunk 25:** No pure-timestamp/id/log/random-seed wall-clock uses found. crypto.randomUUID() (lines 864, 1040) is used only as request ids, not for any timing decision, so excluded. Math.random at line 87 is timing-related (backoff jitter) and is captured in the reconnectDelayMs finding. The #watchMutation promise chain (runWatchMutation, 876-882) serializes watch mutations but is a completion-driven queue with no timer/poll, so not a timing finding.
- **chunk 26:** packages/memory/v2/engine.ts is a 4350-line synchronous SQLite persistence and index-reconciliation engine for the reactive scheduler's observation/dependency records. A keyword scan and full contextual read found NO runtime timing mechanisms: no setTimeout/setInterval/queueMicrotask, no Date.now/performance.now, no Promise.race, no retry/backoff loops, no sleeps, no polling while/for loops. The only timing-adjacent tokens are: (1) the interface fields `actionOptions.debounceMs`, `actionOptions.noDebounce`, and `actionOptions.throttleMs` (lines 816-819) which are pure DATA fields carried inside a SchedulerActionObservation payload that is serialized to and deserialized from SQLite (normalizeSchedulerObservation/encodeSchedulerDependencySnapshot); they are never read in this file to drive any wait/abandon/bound decision (grep confirms they appear only at the declaration) -- the actual debounce/throttle timing behavior lives in the runner scheduler, not here. (2) The pervasive `scheduler`/`Scheduler` identifiers name a persistence domain (scheduler_observation, scheduler_read_index, scheduler_action_state SQL tables and their accessor functions), not a JS timer/scheduler. (3) `COALESCE` in SQL, `batch` for observation batches, and `.immediate()` on a SQLite transaction are all database concepts, not timing. The single async/await (open at line 1371) awaits Database construction, which is completion-driven, not timer-driven. Nothing was dropped that bounds or defers computation on a clock.
- **chunk 27:** server.ts:146 Date.now() and 2464/2472 Date.now() deltas are timestamp/duration bookkeeping for slow-query records and #lastRefreshDurationMs (the delta IS consumed by the adaptive drain budget, reported separately under flushScheduledSessions). server.ts:1725-1752 retryAfterSeq attaches a server-sequence number to a ConflictError so the client can catch up before retrying -- it is a version/sequence signal, not a clock or timer, so excluded as not timing-based. session-open-auth.ts:792 nowSeconds test-injection seam noted with the TTL findings. cf-code-editor-story.tsx only declares SelectControl UI options for a code editor's timingStrategy (immediate/debounce/throttle/blur) and timingDelay (100-500ms) as component props/data; the story itself implements no timing mechanism, so no findings -- the debounce/throttle keywords are configuration values passed to cf-code-editor, not code that debounces/throttles here.
- **chunk 28:** packages/patterns/deprecated/calendar-v512.tsx (7076 lines, a defunct/deprecated calendar pattern). No genuine timing-control code. All Date.now() calls (lines 1293, 1628, 1727, 2683, 2756, 3120, 4109, 4308, 4658, 4751, 4913) are pure timestamp-as-unique-id generation: note IDs (e.g. `Date.now().toString()`, `note_${Date.now()}`) and recurring-series IDs (e.g. `series_${Date.now()}`, sometimes with an index or Math.random suffix). None measure elapsed time, compute a deadline/TTL, or gate/bound any control flow, so all are excluded per the timestamp/id rule. The while loops at lines 1396, 1424, 1457, 1510 expand calendar recurrence rules (RRULE occurrence enumeration): they advance a millisecond calendar value `currentTime += interval * MS_PER_DAY` and are bounded by `effectiveEnd` and `maxOccurrences` — data-driven date arithmetic, not timer waits or wall-clock polling. The loops at 2248 (`left <= right`) and 2273 (`left < right`) are binary search over sorted time slots; 2321 is a two-pointer merge over grid/note arrays; 1859 walks the parentSeriesId chain. No setTimeout/setInterval/requestAnimationFrame/queueMicrotask/Promise.race/AbortSignal/sleep/delay/retry/backoff/poll/debounce/throttle, no recursive task re-scheduling, and no busy-wait or quiescence loop present anywhere in the file. `scheduledTime` occurrences are a data field (event start time in HH:MM), not a scheduling mechanism.
- **chunk 29:** Excluded pure-timestamp/false-positive uses: voice-note-simple.tsx:42 Date.now() as a fallback transcription timestamp (data, not gating). emoji-picker.tsx numerous 'sleep'/'wait'/'idle' hits are emoji searchAliases strings, not timing. fetch-delay.test.tsx (delayMs:50, {settle:true}) and fetch-mock.test.tsx ({settle:true}) exercise the fetch-mock delayMs feature and runtime.settled() gate; the delay lives in the mock infra, not in these audited pattern files, and settle awaits a completion signal rather than a timer, so no finding here. fair-share/main.tsx 'settle'/'settlements' refer to a debt settle-up computation, unrelated to timing. test-await-in-handler.tsx:92 and test-background-manual-trigger.tsx:76-80 use safeDateNow() as a trigger nonce / log timestamp, not a wait/abandon decision; the 'poll'/'polling'/'60 seconds' mentions in test-background-manual-trigger.tsx are prose describing the background-piece-service schedule, no timing code in this file.
- **chunk 30:** In calendar-write-client.ts, formatDateTime (lines 311-326) uses new Date(...).toISOString() purely to format event start/end times into API payload strings — not a wait/deadline decision, excluded. Numerous 'await fetch/res.json()' and integration-test 'await btn.click()/page.evaluate()' calls are ordinary async I/O, not timing mechanisms. The maxRetries/MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS constants are attempt-count bounds captured within their retry-loop findings rather than as separate wall-clock findings.
- **chunk 31:** gmail-client.ts:873-905 awaits new Promise resolved by refreshStream.send's onCommit callback (transaction-commit completion signal), not a timer or poll, so excluded as event-driven, not timing. bam-school-dashboard.tsx uses 'deadline' only as an LLM classification category / data label (lines 52,85,93,174,217,489) and has no timing mechanism, so it produced zero findings. Various Date-free timestamps: none of the audited files use Date.now()/performance.now() for measurement. The `delay`/`delayIncrement` config in gmail-client.ts is reported as a finding because it is dead-code backoff, not excluded.

The many 'ticket', 'delay', 'delayMinutes', 'checkInDeadline' identifiers are data field names (LLM-extracted flight/ticket attributes), not timing mechanisms. safeDateNow() at email-ticket-finder is only used for the reference date already reported; no setTimeout/setInterval/AbortController/backoff/poll loops exist in either tsx file.
- **chunk 33:** crypto.randomUUID() token generation in cfc-browser-helpers.ts (clickTrustedAction/clickCfButton) is an id/nonce, not timing. The SchedulerLoadSummary/BrowserLoadSummary types carry timing fields (p50/p95/max, ageMs, sentAtMs/doneAtMs) that are pure telemetry for logs. Per-attempt waitForSelector timeouts inside the disabled cf-render/cf-code-editor tests are captured under their polling-loop findings rather than as separate entries.
- **chunk 34:** chat-note.test.ts line 16 uses Date.now().toString(36) only to build a unique SPACE_NAME (uniqueness token, not a wait/deadline) — recorded as an 'other' info entry for completeness but it feeds no control flow. Numerous imported helper names (waitForText, waitForRuntimeIdle, waitForDisabled, waitForTextAbsent, waitForSelector, waitForState, waitForAuthorshipState) are the shared polling primitives from @commonfabric/integration and shell-utils; their in-file uses are captured as retry-loop findings per file, but the helper implementations themselves live outside this chunk. The header comments mentioning 'retry' (two-browsers line 20) and 'retry-storm' (convergence-storm line 19) are prose describing the system under test, not code.
- **chunk 35:** default-app.test.ts uses performance.now() purely as a stopwatch to compute and log benchmark durations, not to bound or abandon work: lines 388/410/508 compute createToViewMs/returnToHomeMs/totalMs pushed into noteCreateTimings and logged as JSON; lines 1714/1729 compute loadDurationMs reported in a summary object. These feed no control-flow/gating decision (excluded as pure benchmark wall-clock). The 'settle' identifiers around lines 208, 2481-2489, 2636-2813 are metric field names (settleExecuteCalls, settleTotalDurationMs, settleMaxIterations, settleHistory) collected from rt.getSettleStatsHistory() for reporting only, not a timing mechanism in this file. home-profile.test.ts and home-rehydration-churn.test.ts contain no local timing mechanism: they only call the imported harness helpers waitForRuntimeIdle/waitForRuntimeSynced/waitForText (rt.idle()/allSynced() completion signals); their 'settle'/'idle'/'drains' mentions are explanatory comments. counter.test.ts has no wall-clock or sleep, only waitFor/waitForSelector polling (reported above).
- **chunk 36:** note-append-link.test.ts line 27: `Date.now().toString(36)` builds a unique SPACE_NAME suffix (id/nonce), not an elapsed/deadline measurement — excluded. StepTimer timing in lunch-poll is benchmark/observability only (logged, not gating).
- **chunk 37:** lunch-poll/main.tsx: all safeDateNow() uses are excluded pure-timestamp/id uses — newOptionId (line 212) and newHistoryId (line 252) build base36 element ids; parseVisitDate (lines 261/263) and the logVisit `when` (line 413) produce a stored data timestamp (the visit date), and visitLabel (line 275) formats a stored timestamp for display — none feed a wait/abandon/bound decision. The file comment at line 42 mentions historical "async settle races" but that SQLite-backed code was removed; no timing control-flow remains. The line 323/470 comments describe a read-triggered commit-conflict-and-retry, but that retry is the runtime's optimistic-commit machinery (not code in this file); the handler only performs an explicit read to opt into it. shared-profile.test.ts SHARED_PROFILE_TIMEOUT is also passed to fillCfInput (line 140) as the same 30s bound, covered by the waitForSelector finding.
- **chunk 38:** common-fabric.tsx lines 91-100 and 105-114: the two while(true) loops are a recursive-descent math expression parser (parseTerm/parseExpression) that break on non-operator characters; token-scanning loops, not polling/busy-wait, so excluded. extractor-module.tsx safeDateNow() at lines 952 and 1636 and home-ben.tsx safeDateNow() at lines 116 and 144 are pure data timestamps (trashedAt / entry.timestamp fields) not feeding any wait/abandon/bound decision, so excluded. extractor-module.tsx line 1985 comment ('waiting for OCR') and line 2291 ('Rate limited - please wait') are user-facing message strings, not timing mechanisms.

Its harness.settle(3)/settle(5) calls (lines 547, 657) are quiescence waits but the settle mechanism itself lives in multi-runtime-harness (not in this chunk); this file only consumes reported settle statistics (settledEarly, maxRecentSettleMs, iterations, totalDurationMs) as data fields (lines 53-64, 104-109, 336, 475-527). pattern-source-coverage-child.js line 139 Date.now() is a data timestamp on a mock event, not a control-flow decision. generate-importer.ts line 231 only mentions retries in a comment (raw fetch, single non-streaming call, no timing code). profile-create.tsx lines 68/83-86 mention retry/race only in explanatory comments about the runtime's cross-space-commit retry cycle; the file contains no timing code of its own.
