Excluded pure-timestamp/id uses of Date.now(): manager cause strings, pieces-controller `home-pattern-${Date.now()}` / `space-root-${Date.now()}` cause labels (lines 251-270), basic-persistence cause 'test-object-'+Date.now() (line 39), pattern-and-data-persistence testId=Date.now().toString() and per-test passphrases (line 423-424), and memory-v2-reactivity passphrases `...-${Date.now()}` (lines 33,128,228) — all identifiers/nonces, not timing decisions. Excluded external retry mechanisms referenced but not implemented here: runtime.editWithRetry(...) calls and scheduler retry/onCommit-after-retries comments in manager.ts and pieces-controller.ts delegate to the scheduler (scheduler.ts, out of chunk scope). runtime.idle()/storageManager.synced()/piece.sync() are completion-signal awaits (quiescence via signal, not polling) and are not timer-driven.
- **chunk 41:** packages/runner/src/builder/module.ts:41 uses Date.now() only to build a unique flag id ('sample-${Date.now()}-N') — pure id/nonce, not a timing decision; excluded. packages/runner/integration/reconnection.test.ts:35 uses Date.now() as a passphrase uniquifier ('runner-memory-v2-reconnect-${Date.now()}') — pure id; excluded. packages/runner/src/builder/pattern-metadata.ts:118 has while(true) in resolveOriginal, but it is a bounded graph walk toward a root original with a cycle-guard Set (break when no next / self / seen) — a data-structure traversal, not a poll, busy-wait, or timing loop; excluded. sync-schema-path.test.ts and the sqlite tests use many await sync()/synced()/idle() calls that are completion-signal-driven (not timers) and are not findings on their own.
- **chunk 42:** builder/safe-builtins.ts line 14 (`return Date.now();`) is a safe-builtin wrapper exposing the wall clock to sandboxed pattern code as a timestamp source; it feeds no wait/abandon/bound decision in this file, so excluded as a pure timestamp provider. builder/pattern.ts line 671 is only a doc comment mentioning 'post-retry success run' (describes caching of a re-run) with no timing code. In fetch-program.ts line 270 and fetch.ts, Date.now() is also written into cache/internal as startTime/lastActivity; those writes are the data behind the timeout findings above and are captured there rather than double-counted. The many `await`/`idle`/`settle`/`wait`/`flush` tokens in filter.ts and fetch prose are reactive completion awaits, not timers, and are covered by the retry-loop findings above; the FetchState `{ type: "idle" }` literals are state-machine labels, not idle timers.
- **chunk 43:** flatmap.ts contains no timer/clock mechanisms: its 'await/settle/republish/resume' machinery (trackUntilSettled, awaitInputThenSettle, awaitPendingThenRepublish, result.sync()/pull() finally-seed) is entirely completion-signal (Promise) driven and scheduler re-trigger via journaled reads, not sleeps, timeouts, or polling — so no findings. In llm-dialog.ts, numerous runtime.editWithRetry(...) call sites (e.g. 2323, 2565, 3244) are conflict-retry transaction commits, but the retry loop is implemented in the runtime layer, not in these files, so they are call sites rather than local timing mechanisms; runtime.idle()/settled()/pull()/sink() are completion-signal waits, not timers. Date.now() at lines 2329 and 2933 write lastActivity timestamps into state; they are load-bearing for the TTL gate at 2900-2902 and are folded into that finding rather than excluded as pure data.
- **chunk 44:** runtime.idle()/settled()/trackAsyncWork()/trackUntilSettled(), Cell.sync()/provider.synced(), inputs.pull()/pullContextCells(), and getOrCreateQueue().enqueue() throughout llm.ts and the other files are scheduler/storage completion signals (event-driven waits), NOT timers/sleeps/wall-clock, so they are excluded per the completion-signal carve-out. The many prose mentions of 'idle', 'settle', 'flush', 'drain', 'retry', 'poll', 'reconnect' in doc comments are descriptive, not code. hashOf(requestSnapshot) / requestId in stream-data.ts and sqlite-builtins.ts are content hashes used as dedup keys, not timestamps. No Date.now()/performance.now() usage appears in any of the five files.
- **chunk 45:** performance.now() at lines 123, 143, 1090, 1905, 1967 (recordWishPhaseTiming / measureWishPhase / shared-action and main-action totals) is pure telemetry feeding wishFlowLogger.time(); it prints/aggregates durations and never bounds, abandons, or gates work — excluded. Date.now() at line 947 (resolveSpaceTarget, #now target) is a timestamp exposed as the #now cell's DATA value, not a wait/deadline decision; the surrounding nowCell caching (lines 220, 943-951, 1499-1501, 2002-2003) exists only to keep the reactive action idempotent (avoid emitting a new immutable cell each re-run because Date.now() differs per call) — a determinism guard over a data timestamp, not a timing-control mechanism, so excluded. No setTimeout/setInterval/polling loops/Promise.race/AbortSignal present.
- **chunk 46:** cell.ts: numerous 'retry on conflict' comments (lines 1220, 1279, 1357, 1417, 2104, 2175, 2633-2634, 2677) describe the storage-layer optimistic-concurrency commit-retry and scheduler re-trigger-on-change behavior, but the actual retry mechanism lives in the scheduler/storage layer, not this file; the local 'if (!this.synced) this.sync()' calls merely kick off a sync without awaiting or looping. The sink resubscribe path (sinkHelper, ~2690) is event/data-change driven, not timer-based. scheduler.idle() usages at 1022 (doc comment) and 1074/1082 (the counted finding above) are the only idle waits. The 'while (index < 50)' at 2284 is an array-iterator element cap, not timing. atom-pattern.ts: the Expires/'deadline' atom (lines 282-313) compares a stored timestamp field (a.timestamp <= b.timestamp) as a lattice entailment ordering for TTL/expiry access labels; the comment's 'now <= t' describes semantics but there is NO live Date.now()/performance.now() read here feeding a wait/abandon decision, so it is excluded as a pure data-field comparison rather than a wall-clock control-flow read.
- **chunk 47:** packages/runner/src/cfc/prepare.ts (4546 lines) is a large synchronous Common Fabric Confidentiality (CFC) label-preparation module: it walks IFC schemas, analyzes transaction reads/writes, and computes flow/confidentiality labels. It contains no timing mechanisms. All loops are bounded for...of iterations over finite collections (labelMap entries, write/read activities, schema walks, Object.entries) used for data transformation, not polling or quiescence. There are no while loops, no timers (setTimeout/setInterval/etc.), no async/await, no Promise.race, no AbortController/signal, and no Date.now/performance.now readings. The only match against the timing keyword families is the word 'settled' inside a prose comment at line 4077 ('// SC-4 (C3, disciplines settled with the spec 2026-07-06 — freeze-at-...'), which is documentation of a spec decision, not a timing/quiescence mechanism, and is therefore excluded.
- **chunk 48:** pattern-manager.ts performance.now() at lines 667, 711, 764, 911, 1340, 1369 are all paired with logger.time(start, ...) and measure only for telemetry/log output; none gate, bound, or abandon work, so excluded per the timestamp/benchmark rule. schema-refs.ts:249, ensure-piece-running.ts:47, link-resolution.ts:141 are while(true) loops but are bounded graph/$ref traversals guarded by seen-sets and/or an explicit iteration/depth cap (MAX_PATH_RESOLUTION_LENGTH, MAX_RESULT_LINK_DEPTH) — pure structural iteration with no timing, so excluded. data-updating.ts uses of 'settle' (lines 86, 551, 616) are prose comments about doc-creation ordering, and the while at line 842 is an array-path walk — not timing. module.ts debounce/noDebounce/throttle (lines 22-30, 100-105, 142-144) are only type-field declarations forwarded to the scheduler config; module.ts defines no timing mechanism itself, so excluded (the actual scheduler debounce/throttle behavior lives in the scheduler package).
- **chunk 49:** All performance.now() calls in this file (1379, 1437, 1491, 1850, 1954) are telemetry-only: each is paired with logger.time(start, ...) / logger.timeEnd to record span durations for the resume/start path (rootCellSync, loadPatternByIdentity, startCoreResume, syncCellsForRunningPattern, resumeCellSync). None feeds a wait/abandon/deadline decision, so they are excluded per instructions. The while(isWriteRedirectLink(value)) loop at 2633 is a data-structure traversal chasing write-redirect links, not a timing/poll loop. awaitSyncBeforeInitialRun / awaitSync (528-554, 1499, 1826-1841, 4350) gate an action's initial run on space-sync completion, but that is driven by a completion signal (sync promise), not a timer, so it is not a sleep/timeout. resultCell.sync()/cell.sync() awaits (1737, 1881, 1890, 1955, 3328) are completion-signal-driven awaits, not timed waits.
- **chunk 50:** compiled-bundle-verifier.ts: every performance.now() (lines 282, 350, 527, 829, 906, 1020, 1190, plus timeStart/timeEnd at 111/271) is captured as `start`/`measureStart` and fed only to logger.time()/logger.timeStart()/logger.timeEnd() to emit a diagnostic elapsed-time log line; none bounds, abandons, or gates any work, so excluded as pure benchmark/diagnostic wall-clock. runtime.ts: idle(), dispose(), trackAsyncWork(), and Symbol.asyncDispose are completion-signal driven (await scheduler.idle()/storageManager promises), not timer- or wall-clock-driven, so not findings. The health-check fetch (1467-1475) uses plain fetch + Promise.all with no timeout/deadline, and commitBackpressure (318-322) is only config plumbing for backoff defined in scheduler/backpressure.ts (outside this chunk).
- **chunk 51:** Excluded pure profiling/timestamp uses that do not gate control flow: action-run.ts logger.timeStart/timeEnd wrappers and the performance.now-based duration in the schedule-action-timing debug log (lines 62-100, 316-390, 547-561, 819-835) are instrumentation only; appendActionRunTrace recordedAt (line 231) and pushBoundedHistory recordedAt (scheduler.ts:1527) are timestamps for trace/history records, not gating decisions. dependency-collection.ts uses only logger.timeStart/timeEnd around populateDependencies (profiling). constants.ts is pure numeric config (thresholds/limits) with no runtime timing itself. causalEdges timestamp (scheduler.ts:355) is diagnostic metadata. The retries/actionStats WeakMaps and setDebounce/setThrottle option plumbing are covered under the delays findings rather than duplicated. MAX_ITERATIONS_PER_RUN / loopCounter reset (scheduler.ts:2131) is an iteration-count guard, not a timing mechanism, so not reported as timing.
- **chunk 52:** Excluded pure-telemetry / timestamp uses that do not gate control flow: diagnosis.ts recordIdempotencyViolation (331,336) and captureDiagnosisRecord (364) attach performance.now() timestamps to diagnostic records; diagnosis.ts detectCausalCycles (523) stamps cycle reports; diagnosis.ts stopSchedulerDiagnosis (438) computes a duration only for the report. dirty-dependencies.ts collectStart (75) and lookupStart (237) are handed to logger.time() as pure timing telemetry, not used for any wait/abandon decision. graph-snapshot.ts (44,69-104) reads debounce/throttle values and computes nextDebounceRunInMs/nextEligibleRunInMs purely to populate an inspector graph snapshot (display), not to gate execution. events.ts scheduleEventQueueWake also uses performance.now() but is reported as a finding since it drives the timer. event-identity.ts contains only a comment mentioning 'retries' (no timing code). lineage.ts 'settled' refers to origin settlement status flags (event/lineage lifecycle), not a timer-based settle, so no timing finding.
- **chunk 53:** pull-notifications.ts:89 (timestamp: performance.now()) and notifications.ts:103 (recordedAt: state.now ?? performance.now()) plus the CausalEdge.timestamp field (notifications.ts:248) are diagnostic/trace timestamps attached to records, not feeding any wait/abandon/bound decision. node-record.ts SchedulerNode.retries (lines 20,64) is a per-node retry COUNTER (data), not a retry loop; the while(parentAction) loop at node-record.ts:174 is ancestor-chain traversal, not polling. persistent-observation.ts debounceMs/throttleMs (lines 17,19) are plain option/data fields on a serialized observation type with no control flow. pull-execution.ts uses performance.now() extensively (lines 106,109,134,144,225,255) purely to measure durations for settle stats/telemetry (durationMs, totalDurationMs, logger.time) — measurement only, not gating; excluded per benchmark/measurement rule.
- **chunk 54:** timing.ts recordActionTime/getActionStats: performance.now() (default arg) is stored only as lastRunTimestamp telemetry data and elapsed is accumulated into stats for reporting; not used to bound/abandon/gate any computation, so excluded as pure timestamp/metrics. inspector.ts: create(time = Date.now()) and the Time/opened/updated/time fields throughout Model/Channel/update are connection-status timestamps for the storage inspector UI model (opened/updated markers on subscriptions, ConnectionError.time), not feeding any wait/retry/abandon decision; the ConnectionError.reason 'timeout' is a label on an already-produced error, not a timeout computed here — excluded. extended-storage-transaction.ts: all await points are on commit()/flush() completion promises (real completion signals, not timers); no setTimeout/polling/Promise.race/backoff/wall-clock deadline exists in the file; the words 'flush' refer to CFC outbox side-effect flushing and 'signal' appears only in a prose comment — nothing timing-related to report. subscriptions.ts also references debounceMs only via type wiring already covered.
- **chunk 55:** No executable timing mechanisms were found in any of the 5 assigned files. Details of excluded/keyword-only hits: (1) interface.ts is entirely TypeScript interface/type declarations plus doc comments. Its timing-related content is declarative only: `IRemoteStorageProviderSettings.connectionTimeout` (line 219) declares a numeric WS-connect timeout budget but the abort logic lives in the provider implementation elsewhere; `IStorageTransaction.immediate` (line 552) is a boolean flag whose batch-signing debounce/flush semantics are implemented elsewhere; and comment mentions of settle/idle/retry/backoff/debounce/flush (lines 175-216, 549, 570, 973-976, 1211) describe behavior owned by the scheduler and batch-signer, not implemented here. (2) reactivity-log.ts contains only Metadata marker constants and predicates; the 'conflicts-and-retries' phrase (line 90) is a doc comment about conflict handling elsewhere. (3) rejection.ts exposes three pure error-name classifier predicates (isPermanentRejection, isConflictRejection, isStorageTransactionInconsistent); the retry/backoff/wait-for-catch-up prose in its doc comments describes downstream consumers, and the predicates themselves perform no timing. (4) telemetry.ts uses Date.now() once (line 27) purely as a seed timestamp on a default connection-state object for diff tracking; it is a data timestamp, not a wait/abandon/deadline read. (5) v2-remote-session.ts WebSocketTransport.open()/close()/send() are fully event-driven: the opening and closed promises resolve on the socket's 'open'/'close'/'error' events with no setTimeout, no polling loop, and no timeout race, so they are completion-signal driven and out of scope; its two Date.now() reads are a JWT issuance timestamp iat=Math.floor(Date.now()/1000) and exp=iat+SESSION_OPEN_TTL_SECONDS (line 192, 204), which stamp a signed session.open token's validity window (data/security, not a control-flow elapsed-time gate) and are excluded per the timestamp rule.
- **chunk 56:** Excluded pure-timestamp / measurement-only uses: write-stack-trace.ts:182 performance.now() is a recordedAt timestamp on a debug trace entry (data, no gating). telemetry-otel-bridge.ts:263 Date.now() reconstructs a span start/end for a trace timeline, and nowMs() (340-343, used at 194/199) only measures storage-op durationMs recorded as a span attribute — telemetry emission, not work-bounding. telemetry.ts:17,43-44,174-181 are type/interface field declarations (lastRunTimestamp, debounceMs, throttleMs, retryAttempt, backoffMs) describing timing metadata shapes with no active behavior in this file. v2.ts withCommitTiming (149-159, used at 1615/1654/1929/1974/1985/1991/2008) is a logger.timeStart/timeEnd stopwatch wrapper for instrumentation only, does not gate or abandon work. Numerous await/Promise.all in v2.ts (e.g. 806-816, 988, 1315, 1380, 1542-1581) are ordinary completion-driven awaits, not timing mechanisms. toolshed-http-auth.ts other awaits are body/signature IO, not timing.
- **chunk 57:** traverse.ts (4350 lines) contains no timing APIs whatsoever: no setTimeout/setInterval/queueMicrotask/nextTick, no Promise.race, no AbortController/signal, no Date.now/performance.now, no sleep/delay/backoff/retry/poll/debounce/throttle. It is synchronous storage traversal (getAtPath, followPointer, schema resolution, meta-doc walking). The three while-loops are deterministic data-structure iteration bounded by input, not timing: (1) line 1605 'while(true)' in getAtPath resolves a link/path chain, terminating when the remaining path is exhausted or a non-link node is reached; (2) line 2161 'while(pendingDocs.length > 0)' is a breadth-first work-list drain over meta-linked docs with a visited-set guard, not a quiescence poll; (3) line 2440 'while(pathIndex < ... )' is a bounded index walk comparing a doc path against a selector path. None wait on, abandon, or bound work by a clock or by re-scheduling. No excluded pure-timestamp uses either.
- **chunk 58:** Both files are test-only and contain no timing mechanisms. array-push-mergeable.test.ts: all keyword hits are completion-signal awaits (tx.commit(), rt.storageManager.synced(), cell.sync(), cell.pull(), rt.dispose(), storage.close()); the words 'retry' (lines 228) and 'wait'/'stale' appear only in comments describing the live system's conflict-and-retry behavior, not in test code. cell-callbacks.test.ts: all hits are the same completion-signal awaits plus one await runtime.scheduler.idle() (line 1468), which blocks on the scheduler's drain/quiescence promise rather than a timer, and test-description/comment uses of 'wait' (e.g. 'should wait for dependent computations', 'Pull should wait for the computation to run') which describe signal-driven pull() awaits. No setTimeout/setInterval, no Promise.race deadlines, no polling while/for loops, no Date.now()/performance.now() reads, no AbortSignal, no sleep/delay/backoff, no debounce/throttle. Nothing timing-related to report.
- **chunk 59:** cell.bench.ts: Date.now() at lines 908 and 921 is used only to populate a data 'timestamp' field on the cell value (pure data timestamp, not a control-flow/wait/deadline decision) — excluded. The many 'await runtime.idle()' calls (e.g. lines 878, 923) and 'await tx.commit()' calls are completion-signal-driven waits for scheduler quiescence, not timer/stopwatch mechanisms, so they are not timing findings. cfc-atom-pattern.test.ts: the 'deadline'/Expires timestamp ordering at lines 347-359 tests entailment ordering over literal constant timestamps (1000/2000) as data — no wall clock read and no waiting — excluded. No setInterval/backoff/retry/poll/busy-wait patterns found in any of the three files.
- **chunk 60:** The only other keyword hits are the outbox-effect flush() method and the 'flushed'/'flushes outbox effects' test names around lines 1074-1175 — these refer to committing/emitting outbox effects after a successful commit (business-logic naming), not to any timer, buffer-flush timing, or wait mechanism, so they are excluded. There are no setTimeout/setInterval/queueMicrotask/Promise.race/AbortSignal/Date.now/performance.now/sleep/debounce/throttle uses and no raw while/for polling loops anywhere in the file. The many 'await runtime.dispose()' and 'await runtime.setup(...)' calls are ordinary promise awaits on runtime lifecycle APIs, not timing constructs.
- **chunk 61:** cfc-existence-channel.test.ts, cfc-flow-labels.test.ts, cfc-implementation-identity.test.ts, and cfc-label-view.test.ts contain no timer/wall-clock timing patterns: their many 'await', 'commit', 'settle', 'idle', and 'dispose' hits are completion-driven awaits (await tx.commit(), await runtime.idle(), await runtime.dispose()) and comment prose ('settled with the spec', 'flush', 'retry'), not timers, sleeps, or elapsed-time gating. cfc-flow-labels.test.ts references RetryImmediately (a scheduler control signal / abort-and-rerun mechanism, not a timed backoff) purely to assert taint-restoration behavior, so it is not a retry-loop timing pattern. No Date.now()/performance.now() timestamp-only uses were present.
- **chunk 62:** cfc-sink-request-policy.test.ts: flushCount counters and the 'retry' in URLs/test names are labels, not timing (the retry finding is the editWithRetry call itself). compile-cache-writeback-conflict.test.ts: flushCompileCacheWrites()/synced()/'settle so its outcome is observable' are completion-signal awaits (event-driven), not timers, so excluded. fetch-binary-schema-materialization.test.ts: runtime.settled() + runtime.idle() are completion-signal awaits/drains, not timer-based waits — no timing finding. Numerous await runtime.idle()/pull()/commit()/synced()/dispose()/close() across all files are signal-driven awaits, not timing mechanisms. Identity.fromPassphrase('...request policy'/'...conflict') strings contain keyword substrings only. Comments referencing 'retry budget'/'wait for the conflict' describe the production semantics under test rather than test-local timing.
- **chunk 63:** Excluded as non-timing: numerous runtime.idle() awaits across generate-object-tools.test.ts (lines 79,181,278,...,2267) and generate-object-outbox.test.ts (54,124,221,315,415) -- these are completion-signal quiescence waits driven by the scheduler, not timers. Excluded as pure timestamps-for-data: Date.now() pushed into generateObjectCalls/sendRequestCalls arrays (generate-object-outbox.test.ts lines 113,208,295,395) which record call occurrence/ordering, not elapsed-time gating (the gating there is done by the separate polling helpers, which are reported). The Date.now() reads inside the polling helpers ARE reported since they compute deadlines. Many setTimeout sleeps within a file share identical purpose and were grouped into one finding per file with all line numbers listed.
- **chunk 64:** generate-text-outbox.test.ts lines 105,175,251,336 and generate-text.test.ts line 113 push Date.now() into a sendRequestCalls array as a per-call timestamp; these timestamps are never read to compute elapsed time or gate control flow (the array is only checked for length via waitForCallCount), so they are pure timestamp data and excluded. Numerous await runtime.idle() / rc.pull() / sm.synced() / tx.commit() awaits across all files are completion-signal-driven quiescence primitives, not timer-based, and are excluded except where wrapped in fixed-count polling loops (reported above). The 'settle'/'drain'/'flush' keyword hits are prose comments or method names (flushCompileCacheWrites, sm.synced) that await real signals, not timing constructs.
- **chunk 65:** Excluded pure-timestamp / non-timing uses: llm-dialog-outbox.test.ts:122 `sendRequestCalls.push(Date.now())` records timestamps only to later assert length>0 (never used for a wait/bound decision). memory-v2-emulation.test.ts (lines 70,113,134,157,174) and memory-v2-pull-reactivity.test.ts (lines 85,92,181) use `Date.now()` only to build unique of: URIs (id/nonce), not timing. memory-v2-lazy-session.test.ts:146 'retries lazy session creation after a transient failure' asserts the storage manager's own retry behavior via a fault-injecting create() and commit counts; the test contains no timer/poll/backoff mechanism of its own, so no finding was created (the retry lives in production code outside this chunk). Numerous `await runtime.idle()`, `.pull()`, `storageManager.synced()`, and `tx.commit()` awaits across all files are completion-signal-driven (not timers) and are not timing findings.
- **chunk 66:** Excluded as pure timestamps/data (not feeding a wait/abandon/bound decision): the many `of:...-${Date.now()}` URI constructions across memory-v2-subscription.test.ts (unique doc ids) and memory-v2-stacked-commit.test.ts. The `await Promise.resolve()` double-yields used to check `settled===false` (subscription lines 745-746, 792-793, 937-938) are microtask yields to observe held state, not sleeps/timers, so not reported as findings. - **chunk 86:** All three files are compile-time TypeScript AST transformer code with zero timing-related patterns. Keyword hits were all false positives: (1) dataflow.ts:673 has a while(true) inside findRootIdentifier that unwraps an expression tree (identifier/property-access/element-access/parenthesized/as/call) and returns on reaching a leaf or unhandled node - a bounded syntax-tree traversal, not a poll/busy-wait; each iteration walks one level up toward a leaf. (2) dataflow.ts:131 'signal' is prose in a doc comment ('signal to recognize reads through them as reactive'), not an AbortSignal. (3) normalize.ts:242 while(true) inside normalizeExpression strips syntactic wrappers (parens/type-assertions/non-null/method-call receivers) walking toward the underlying expression, terminating by return - AST normalization, not timing. (4) array-method-transform.ts:56 while(true) inside lowerMapReceiverMemberAccess collects member-access segments walking up the receiver chain and returns when it hits a non-member node - AST lowering, not timing. No setTimeout/interval/microtask/Promise.race/Date.now/performance.now/sleep/retry/backoff/debounce/throttle usage of any kind; no wall-clock reads.
- **chunk 87:** All three files are compile-time TypeScript AST transformers with no timing behavior. capability-analysis.ts has three while(true) loops (lines 538, 684, 706): line 538 in extractAccessPath walks a property/element access chain unwinding one node per iteration and breaks when the current node is neither a property nor element access; lines 684 and 706 (unwrapIdentifierUsageSite / unwrapExpressionUsageSite) walk up the parent chain past parenthesized/as/type-assertion/satisfies/non-null wrappers and return when no more wrappers apply. expression-site-lowering.ts has one while(true) at line 635 (getDataFlowRootIdentifier) that descends the leftmost side of a property-access / .key() call chain to find the root identifier. All four loops are structurally bounded AST traversals terminated by the finite syntax tree, not polling/retry/timing loops driven by a clock or condition-re-check. mergeable-push-validation.ts keyword hits for 'retry'/'retries' (lines 11, 17, 28, 48, 57) are all inside JSDoc comments and user-facing diagnostic message string literals describing runtime write-contention/conflict-and-retry semantics of addUnique vs push; they describe behavior of other subsystems, not any timing mechanism in this transformer. No setTimeout/interval/microtask/Date.now/performance.now/sleep/AbortSignal usage anywhere in the three files.
- **chunk 88:** while(true) loops in opaque-get-validation.ts:198 (isReactiveInitializer), opaque-roots.ts:62 and :229 (getOpaqueAccessInfo receiver unwrap), pattern-body-reactive-root-lowering.ts:1348 (stripSyntacticWrappers) and :1498 (receiver-chain walk), expression.ts:16 (unwrapExpression), and identifiers.ts:132 (unique-name generation) are all bounded AST-structure walks or a monotonically-incrementing name-collision counter, not polling/timing loops — excluded. complex-nested-types.input.tsx:29 uses Date.now().toString() purely as a synthetic record id (timestamp-as-id), excluded per rules. fixture-based.test.ts uses performance.now() at lines 169,171,212,220 only to compute and console.log batch/config type-check durations for reporting; it never bounds or abandons work, so it is pure benchmark timestamping — excluded.
- **chunk 89:** ts-transformers files contain no runtime timing. logical-nullish-coalescing.input.tsx mentions 'timeout'/'retries' only as field names in a fixture pattern and 'retry'/'Will retry' as JSX string content. pattern-context-validation-coverage.test.ts contains while/for/for(;;)/switch constructs, but they are inside TypeScript source *strings* fed to the validator (testing that reactive reads in loop heads are rejected), not executable timing loops. transformed-ast.ts has a `while (true)` at line 118 that is a pure AST-unwrap loop (strips parenthesized/as/satisfies wrappers, returns on first non-wrapper) with no timing, wait, or polling semantics. In the two authorship files, the various setTimeout(resolve, 0) awaits elsewhere in the test flush microtasks and are covered by the one sleep finding. No pure-timestamp Date.now()/performance.now() uses were present in any file.
- **chunk 90:** cf-code-editor.ts line 627: Date.now().toString(36) is used only to synthesize a random-ish unique noteId (id/nonce), not to gate or bound any work, so excluded per the timestamp-for-data rule. cf-code-editor.ts line 661 and cf-code-editor.test.ts lines 94-96: rt.signal.aborted is checked to distinguish a disposal/cancellation from a real error after an awaited createPage; this is an AbortSignal completion check, not a timeout or deadline, so excluded. cf-drag-source.ts line 164 only contains a comment noting that drop detection is done by external drop-zones polling the drag state; no timing mechanism exists in this file (no timer/loop), so no finding. The various while((match=regex.exec(...))!==null) loops in cf-code-editor.ts (lines 533, 582, 1617, 1763) are regex-iteration loops over static text, not timing/polling loops, and are excluded. CSS transition-delay declarations in cf-fab.ts (lines 381, 389) are pure stylesheet values, not JS control flow, so excluded.
- **chunk 91:** cf-location.ts lines 342 and 390 use Date.now() purely as a `timestamp` field in cf-location-start event payloads (data, not gating) and are excluded. cf-input/cf-input.test.ts lines 51 (timingStrategy 'debounce') and 191 (comment mentioning flush) are test assertions/comments about the debounce config, not a timing mechanism, so excluded; the real debounce lives in the cell controller. cf-location TODO comments about throttling/maxWatchDuration describe absent (unimplemented) mechanisms and were not counted as findings.
- **chunk 92:** cf-prompt-input.ts _generateAttachmentId (line 986) uses Date.now() purely to build a unique attachment id string, not to measure elapsed time or gate control flow — excluded per rules. CSS-based timing (animation-delay/keyframes in cf-message-beads bead-in and spin, transition durations in cf-modal/cf-oauth/cf-prompt-input styles) is pure presentation, not JS control-flow timing, so excluded. cf-oauth OAuthData.expiresAt/expiresIn are data fields carried through, not read here for any wait/abandon decision. The many try/catch for-loops in cf-map that iterate reactive-proxy arrays are defensive property-access guards, not polling loops, so excluded.
- **chunk 93:** cf-tabs.test.ts lines 134-135 stub requestAnimationFrame/cancelAnimationFrame as no-ops (g.requestAnimationFrame = () => 0) purely so the component under test does not schedule real frames in a non-DOM test environment; this is a test harness stub, not a timing mechanism, so no finding. cf-tabs.test.ts line 345 mentions the CT-1677 'Too many iterations' settle class only in a doc comment describing the contract, not executable timing code.
- **chunk 94:** cf-voice-input.ts line 329 (`voice-${Date.now()}-...`) and line 665 (`timestamp: Date.now()` in an emitted event) use Date.now() purely as an id component / event-payload timestamp, not to gate control flow, so they are excluded. cf-voice-input line 504 `this.startTime = Date.now()` and line 520-522 recompute of duration on stop are wall-clock reads, but they only compute the displayed/emitted recording duration; the control-flow-bearing consumer (the 250ms interval and max-duration timeout) is already captured, so the raw startTime capture is not a separate finding. cf-updater.ts line 67 `cursor: wait` and line 97/146 'idle'/'pending' state-name strings are CSS/enum tokens, not timing. cf-textarea.test.ts line 50 asserts the default timingStrategy value ('debounce') and is a test assertion about the config, not a mechanism. cf-tags.ts, cf-tools-chip, cf-voice-input 'idle'/'settle'/'wait'/'drain' keyword hits in state enums, comments, and CSS were verified non-timing.
- **chunk 95:** cell-controller.ts: its settle/release logic (_settledAwaitingRelease, _bindGeneration, onWritesSettled) is completion/signal-driven off write settle callbacks, NOT timer-driven, so it is excluded despite heavy use of the word 'settle'. Its only timing is delegation to InputTimingController via _inputTiming?.flush() (line 337) and the debounce config literals at lines 157/564 (captured under the input-timing-controller findings). form-field-controller.ts commit()/flush() (lines 182-193, 237-250) await cell.set() to completion — completion-driven, no timer, excluded. cell-controller.test.ts uses a fake clock (time.tick / time.runMicrotasks) to drive debounce tests deterministically; those tick() calls exercise the InputTimingController timer rather than being independent timing mechanisms. Test files' many 'settle'/'flush'/'debounce' references are exercising the mechanisms above, not new ones.
- **chunk 96:** packages/utils/src/logger.ts timeStart/timeEnd/time/record (lines 241, 958, 975, 1008) read performance.now() only to measure and record elapsed timing into stats/log output; none gate, abandon, or bound control flow, so they are excluded as pure measurement. logger.ts line 1212 'poll'/'idle' keyword hits were prose comments, not timing logic. packages/ui/src/v2/utils/file-cell-storage.ts line 36 Date.now() is stored as a createdAt data timestamp on an uploaded blob record, not feeding any wait/abandon decision, so excluded. events.test.ts uses a fake-clock time.tick harness purely to drive the debounce/throttle unit tests and is covered by the events.ts findings.
- **chunk 97:** Pure timestamp / benchmark-measurement uses (excluded per rules, not fed into any wait/abandon decision): scripts/bench-setup.ts line 210/284 `t0 = Date.now()` and `setup_ms = Date.now() - t0` — deploy-duration printed into the JSON manifest only; timestamp() at 164-171 formats Date into a space name; embedded prose in NOTES/AGENTS content mentions setTimeout(0) tree rebuilds but is string data, not code. scripts/benchmark-object-hashing/main.mjs and main.ts: benchmark() functions (main.mjs 205-217, main.ts 926-953) use performance.now() only to time hashing throughput and print ops/sec; testData metadata.timestamp = Date.now() (main.mjs 101, main.ts 113) is data content; these bound nothing. scripts/ci-gantt.ts: Date.parse / Date.now / (en-st)/1000 (hasTiming 126-131, timing accumulation 275-307) compute CI job start/finish/duration statistics for chart data, not control flow; new Date().toISOString() at 587 is a report date label. scripts/diagnose.ts: `.claude-${Date.now()}` at line 83 is a temp-directory name (id use only); no timing control flow. scripts/fuse-bench.ts: performance.now() in benchReaddir/benchStat/benchReadScalar/benchReadJson/benchGrep/benchConcurrentRead and percentile/statsFromSamples measure per-op latency for reporting (not abandon/bound decisions); the write-op t0/Date.now inside benchWrite that IS used for the deadline is captured as a finding above.
- **chunk 98:** share-pattern-via-tailscale.sh uses `date`/timestamps nowhere for gating; its INSPECT_PORT etc. are config not timing. memory.ts uses new Date().toISOString() at lines 470 and 573-574 purely as timestamps in baseline/diff JSON output (data, not gating) -- excluded. perf-lib.test.ts is dense with ISO datetime string literals (created_at/started_at/completed_at, e.g. lines 44-51, 78-99) used as fixture data for duration extraction; the tests assert computed durationSeconds but the timestamps themselves are test inputs, not runtime timing -- excluded except where they exercise retry logic (reported above). integration.ts uses `ports` json and PORT_OFFSET arithmetic which is port config, not timing. start-local-dev.sh SECONDS usage inside the wait loops is captured under the two wait_ functions above.
- **chunk 99:** tasks/test.ts uses Date.now() at lines 47/75 (startedAt/durationMs in testPackage) and 102/137 (suiteStartedAt / total wall time in runTests) purely to measure and print per-package and total test durations; these feed only console reporting and a descending sort for display, not any wait/abandon/bound decision, so excluded as pure measurement (though the printed numbers are conceptually related to the perf tooling elsewhere). tasks/post-coverage-comment.test.ts contains no timing constructs at all: its fetch mock and assertions are synchronous/promise-resolved with no timers, sleeps, polling, or wall-clock reads. In perf-lib.ts, `new Date().toISOString()` at lines 515 and 565 (serializePerfMetrics/serializePerfMetricsBackfill generatedAt) are pure data timestamps and are excluded.
